**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the working principle, types, and industrial applications of **pneumatic linear actuators**. Derive the expressions for output force and velocity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **80 mm** and rod diameter of **45 mm**. The pump supplies oil at **P = 12 MPa** with flow rate **Q = 10 L/min**. Calculate:
   (i) Extension force
   (ii) Retraction force
   (iii) Extension velocity
   (iv) Time to complete a **300 mm** stroke

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Working Principle:**
A pneumatic linear actuator (pneumatic cylinder) converts compressed air energy into linear mechanical work. Compressed air at 0.4–1.0 MPa is admitted into a cylindrical bore, where it acts on a piston. The pressure difference across the piston face creates a net force that moves the piston rod linearly.

**Types of Pneumatic Cylinders:**

1. **Single-Acting Cylinder** — Air acts on one side only; a spring returns the piston. Stroke limited (< 100 mm typical).
2. **Double-Acting Cylinder** — Air admitted alternately to both sides. Higher force, longer stroke, full control over extension and retraction.
3. **Tandem Cylinder** — Two pistons in series; doubles the output force.
4. **Telescopic Cylinder** — Nested pistons; very long stroke from a compact body.
5. **Rodless Cylinder** — Uses magnetic or mechanical coupling through a slot; saves space.

**Derivation of Force:**

Pressure $P$ acts on the effective piston area $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$.

$$F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

**Derivation of Velocity:**

From continuity, the volume flow rate entering the cylinder equals the area swept by the piston per unit time:

$$Q = A \cdot v \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Industrial Applications:**
- Material handling (pick-and-place, packaging)
- Assembly line automation (clamping, pressing)
- Automotive (brake actuation, door control)
- Food and pharmaceutical processing (clean, no oil contamination)
- Robotics (low-cost linear joints)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:** D = 80 mm = 0.080 m, d = 45 mm = 0.045 m, P = 12 MPa = 12 × 10⁶ Pa, Q = 10 L/min = 10 × 10⁻³/60 = 1.667 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s, stroke = 300 mm = 0.300 m.

**Step 1 — Compute Areas:**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080^2 - 0.045^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.0064 - 0.002025) = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extension Force:**

$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1 = 12 \times 10^6 \cdot 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 60{,}319 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Retraction Force:**

$$F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2 = 12 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.436 \times 10^{-3} = 41{,}230 \text{ N} \approx 41.23 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Extension Velocity:**

$$v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03316 \text{ m/s} \approx 33.16 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 5 — Time for 300 mm Stroke:**

$$t = \dfrac{d}{v_{ext}} = \dfrac{0.300}{0.03316} = 9.046 \text{ s} \approx 9.05 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Final Answers:** F_ext = **60.32 kN**, F_ret = **41.23 kN**, v_ext = **33.16 mm/s**, t = **9.05 s**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **lead screw** mechanism as a motion conversion system. Derive the relationship between rotational input and linear output, and discuss the difference between a lead screw and a ball screw.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A lead screw with a **pitch of 5 mm** and **single-start** thread is driven by a **stepper motor of 1.8° step angle** through a **5:1 reduction gearbox**. If the motor is pulsed at **2000 pulses/second**:
   (i) Determine the lead of the screw.
   (ii) Calculate the rotational speed of the screw shaft.
   (iii) Calculate the linear velocity of the nut.
   (iv) Determine the time to traverse **150 mm**.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Lead Screw — Definition and Working Principle:**
A lead screw is a helical-threaded rod that mates with a fixed nut (or vice versa). Rotational motion of the screw about its axis is converted into linear motion of the nut along the screw axis. The conversion is achieved because the helical thread acts like an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder.

**Derivation of Motion Relationship:**

Let **L** = lead (mm/rev), **N** = rotational speed (RPM), and **v** = linear velocity (mm/s).

Per revolution, the nut advances by L. Per minute, the nut advances by $L \cdot N$ mm. Therefore:

$$v = \dfrac{L \cdot N}{60} \text{ mm/s}$$

**Lead Screw vs. Ball Screw:**

| Feature | Lead Screw | Ball Screw |
|---|---|---|
| Contact | Sliding friction between threads | Rolling contact via ball bearings |
| Efficiency | 20–50% | 80–95% |
| Backlash | Higher | Lower |
| Cost | Low | High (3–10× lead screw) |
| Life | Wear-limited | Long (10⁶+ cycles typical) |
| Precision | Low to medium | High |
| Use | Light loads, manual adjustment, low-speed | CNC, precision automation, high-speed |

**Key Disadvantage of Lead Screw:** Sliding friction generates heat and limits efficiency — "self-locking" can also be a problem when back-driving is needed.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** Pitch p = 5 mm, single-start, stepper step angle = 1.8°, gear reduction ratio = 5:1 (i.e., 5 motor revolutions = 1 screw revolution), pulse rate = 2000 pulses/second.

**Step 1 — Compute Lead:**

For single-start thread: $L = p = 5 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2 — Motor Rotational Speed:**

Steps per motor revolution = 360° / 1.8° = 200 steps/rev.

Pulse rate = 2000 pulses/sec = 2000 steps/sec.

$$N_{motor} = \dfrac{2000 \text{ steps/sec}}{200 \text{ steps/rev}} = 10 \text{ rev/sec} = 600 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Screw Shaft Speed (After 5:1 Reduction):**

The gear reduction means the screw rotates 1/5 the speed of the motor:

$$N_{screw} = \dfrac{N_{motor}}{5} = \dfrac{600}{5} = 120 \text{ RPM} = 2 \text{ rev/sec}$$

**Step 4 — Linear Velocity of the Nut:**

$$v = \dfrac{L \cdot N_{screw}}{60} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 120}{60} = 10 \text{ mm/s} = 0.01 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Time to Traverse 150 mm:**

$$t = \dfrac{150 \text{ mm}}{10 \text{ mm/s}} = 15 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Final Answers:** L = **5 mm**, N_screw = **120 RPM**, v = **10 mm/s**, t = **15 s**.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the difference between Lead and Pitch** in multi-start screws (e.g., a 4-start screw with 2 mm pitch has a lead of 8 mm). This costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong area** in hydraulic cylinder problems — using the cap-end area when retraction is asked, or vice versa. **Always distinguish A₁ (extension) and A₂ (retraction)**. Costs **1 mark** typically.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — forgetting to convert L/min to m³/s, RPM to rad/s, or MPa to Pa. This costs **1–2 marks** and is the most common error.
>
> 4. **Confusing stepper (open-loop) and servo (closed-loop)** — KTU specifically tests this conceptual difference. Costs up to **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
>
> 5. **Not labeling the diagram** in cam, slider-crank, or rack-pinion questions. KTU allocates **1–2 marks** for a neat, labeled sketch.
>
> 6. **Ignoring efficiency** in gear-train torque problems. If efficiency is given, you **must** apply it: $T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot \text{ratio} \cdot \eta$.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to state units** in final answers. KTU deducts **0.5 mark per missing unit** in many valuation sessions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Linear Actuators:**
- ✅ Solenoid: ON/OFF, electromagnetic, limited stroke, fast response.
- ✅ Hydraulic cylinder: High force (F = P·A), high pressure (up to 25 MPa), precise control, requires pump, oil, and filtration.
- ✅ Pneumatic cylinder: Medium force, low pressure (≤ 1 MPa), clean, cheap, compressible air → poor stiffness.
- ✅ Electric linear actuator: Motor + lead/ball screw + rack-pinion. Best for precision positioning.

**Rotary Actuators:**
- ✅ DC motor: Continuous rotation, velocity control via voltage/PWM.
- ✅ Stepper motor: Open-loop, discrete steps (1.8°/0.9° typical), holds position without feedback.
- ✅ Servo motor: Closed-loop, encoder feedback, high precision, high cost.
- ✅ Hydraulic motor: High torque, low speed, infinite rotation, requires pump.
- ✅ Pneumatic motor: Light duty, infinite rotation, low torque density.

**Motion Conversion Systems — Rotational → Linear:**
- ✅ **Lead screw / Ball screw:** High precision, self-locking (lead screw), high efficiency (ball screw).
- ✅ **Rack and pinion:** Long strokes, good for heavy loads, used in steering and CNC.
- ✅ **Cam-follower:** Programmed motion profile, used in engines and automation.
- ✅ **Crank-slider:** Reciprocating motion, used in IC engines and pumps.
- ✅ **Belt-pulley with linear carriage:** Long-distance low-precision motion, used in 3D printers.

**Motion Conversion Systems — Rotational → Rotational (modified):**
- ✅ **Gear trains:** Speed reduction, torque multiplication. Simple, compound, planetary types.
- ✅ **Belt-pulley drive:** Long center distance, slip possible (V-belt) or no slip (toothed).
- ✅ **Chain-sprocket:** High torque, no slip, parallel shafts, motorcycle/bicycle.
- ✅ **Harmonic drive:** Very high reduction ratio (80:1 to 320:1), zero backlash, used in robotics.

**Essential Formulas (Memorize):**
- ✅ Cylinder force: $F = P \cdot A$
- ✅ Cylinder velocity: $v = Q / A$
- ✅ Rack displacement: $x = r_p \cdot \theta$
- ✅ Gear ratio: $\omega_1 / \omega_2 = N_2 / N_1$
- ✅ Power: $P = T \cdot \omega = F \cdot v$
- ✅ DC motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, $E_b = K_e \omega$, $T = K_t I_a$

**Key Distinctions Examiners Test:**
- ✅ Open-loop (stepper) vs. Closed-loop (servo) — **must** be stated first.
- ✅ Hydraulic (high force, oil) vs. Pneumatic (low force, air) — contrast on pressure, stiffness, cleanliness.
- ✅ Lead screw (sliding, η ≈ 30–50%) vs. Ball screw (rolling, η ≈ 90%) — efficiency and precision.
- ✅ Extension force (full bore) vs. Retraction force (annular area) — common numerical trap.
- ✅ Pitch (distance between threads) vs. Lead (axial advance per rev) — for multi-start threads, Lead = n × Pitch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Kinematic Chain?

A **kinematic chain** is a systematic, ordered assembly of rigid bodies (called **links**) interconnected by **kinematic pairs** (joints) that permit constrained relative motion. It is the foundational building block of any mechatronic system — from the robotic arm in an assembly line to the suspension in an autonomous vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A *kinematic chain* is an assemblage of links joined together such that each link forms a kinematic pair with its neighbours, providing definite, predictable relative motion. When one link of the chain is fixed to the ground (frame), it becomes a **mechanism**.

### The Hierarchy of Mechanical Assemblies

A KTU examiner expects the student to clearly distinguish between four closely-related terms. Memorize the chain-of-logic below:

| Term | Definition | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Link (Element)** | A single rigid body | No relative motion within itself |
| **Kinematic Pair** | Two links joined at a joint | Allows specific relative motion (1 or 2 DOF) |
| **Kinematic Chain** | Multiple links + pairs | Assembled but **no link fixed** |
| **Mechanism** | Kinematic chain + 1 fixed link | Produces **definite, useful motion** |
| **Machine** | Mechanism + power source | Transforms/transmits energy to do work |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body as a Machine"

Imagine a human arm reaching for a cup of coffee:
- **Bones** = Links
- **Shoulder, elbow, wrist joints** = Kinematic pairs
- **The whole arm** (without anchoring the torso) = Kinematic chain
- **The whole body standing on the ground** = Mechanism (pelvis is the fixed link)
- **Picking up the cup to drink** = Machine (adds the purpose: energy conversion)

Just as the body cannot function as a "machine" without the ground, a kinematic chain cannot perform work until one link is fixed.

## 1.3 Kinematic Pair — The Atom of Motion

A **kinematic pair** is the joint between two links. It is classified by:
- **Type of contact**: Lower pair (surface contact, e.g., revolute, prismatic) vs. Higher pair (line/point contact, e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh)
- **Type of motion**: Sliding, rolling, turning, screw, spherical
- **Degrees of freedom (DOF) of the pair**: 1-DOF pairs (revolute, prismatic) vs. 2-DOF pairs (cam, gear)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Lower pair** → 1 DOF of relative motion, surface contact, can transmit higher loads (e.g., revolute joint, prismatic joint)
> - **Higher pair** → 2 DOF of relative motion, point/line contact, prone to wear (e.g., cam-follower, gear teeth)

## 1.4 Why Kinematic Chains Matter in Mechatronics

In any mechatronic product — CNC machine, SCARA robot, 3D printer, pick-and-place system — the **mechanical skeleton is a kinematic chain**. Sensors measure its joint angles, actuators drive its joints, and controllers compute trajectories. Thus, **kinematic chain analysis is the bridge between mechanical design and control engineering**.

The standard physical constants/metrics used throughout this module are:

- **Planar DOF of a rigid body in 2D = 3** (two translations + one rotation)
- **Number of constraints removed by a lower pair = 2**
- **Number of constraints removed by a higher pair = 1**
- **Gruebler's constant for planar mechanisms = 3**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Mobility of a Mechanism

The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, also called the **mobility (M)**, is the number of independent inputs (driver links) required to bring the mechanism to a desired configuration. This is the **single most important numerical quantity** in the entire module.

### 2.1.1 Kutzbach's Criterion (Grübler's Equation) — Planar Mechanisms

For a planar mechanism with $n$ links (including the fixed frame), $j_1$ lower pairs (1-DOF joints), and $j_2$ higher pairs (2-DOF joints):

$$
\boxed{M \;=\; 3(n - 1) \;-\; 2j_1 \;-\; j_2}
$$

This is the **Kutzbach equation** (also called the **Grübler-Kutzbach equation**). It is derived from the fact that each unconstrained rigid body in 2D has 3 DOF, and each joint removes DOFs equal to the constraint count.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Interpretation:**
> - $M = 0$ → **Structure** (no motion possible)
> - $M = 1$ → **Mechanism** with single input (most common)
> - $M > 1$ → **Multi-DOF mechanism** (requires multiple actuators)
> - $M < 0$ → **Pre-loaded structure** (statically indeterminate)

### 2.1.2 Spatial (3D) Extension — Kutzbach's General Equation

For spatial mechanisms, the rigid body has 6 DOF, and the general form is:

$$
\boxed{M \;=\; 6(n - 1) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{j}\left(6 - f_i\right)}
$$

where $f_i$ is the DOF of the $i^{th}$ joint. For a revolute joint, $f_i = 1$, so the joint removes $6 - 1 = 5$ DOFs. For a prismatic joint, $f_i = 1$ as well, so it also removes 5 DOFs.

## 2.2 The Four-Bar Kinematic Chain

The **four-bar chain** is the workhorse of planar mechanism design. It consists of 4 links connected by 4 revolute joints in a closed loop:
- **Ground link (fixed)**
- **Input link (crank or rocker)**
- **Coupler link**
- **Output link (rocker or crank)**

### 2.2.1 DOF of a Four-Bar Chain

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

It always has **1 DOF** regardless of link lengths — a single input (the crank) fully defines the configuration.

### 2.2.2 Grashof's Law (The Heart of Four-Bar Analysis)

Let the four link lengths be $s$ (shortest), $l$ (longest), and $p, q$ (the other two). **Grashof's criterion** is:

$$
\boxed{s + l \;\leq\; p + q}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Grashof's Law — Three Cases:**
>
> **Case 1 — Grashof Chain (Class I, II, or III):** $s + l < p + q$
> At least one link can make a **complete revolution**. The classification depends on which link is fixed.
> - Shortest link fixed → **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)** mechanism
> - Link adjacent to shortest fixed → **Crank-Rocker** mechanism
> - Link opposite to shortest fixed → **Double-Rocker (Grashof)** mechanism
>
> **Case 2 — Change-Point Mechanism:** $s + l = p + q$
> Toggle positions exist; a special boundary case.
>
> **Case 3 — Non-Grashof Chain:** $s + l > p + q$
> **No link can complete a full revolution.** Only double-rocker (Class I) and triple-rocker behaviour occur.

## 2.3 The Slider-Crank Kinematic Chain

The **slider-crank** mechanism consists of 4 links: ground, crank, connecting rod (coupler), and slider. It is formed by replacing the revolute joint at one end of the coupler with a prismatic pair.

### 2.3.1 DOF of a Slider-Crank

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 1
$$

It also has **1 DOF** — the crank angle uniquely determines the slider position via the famous **slider position equation**:

$$
x = r \cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2 \theta}
$$

where $r$ is the crank length, $L$ is the connecting rod length, and $\theta$ is the crank angle.

## 2.4 Inversions of a Kinematic Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Inversion:**
> *Inversion* is the process of obtaining **new mechanisms** from a given kinematic chain by **fixing different links** in turn, one at a time. Since a chain with $n$ links has $n-1$ possible inversions, the four-bar chain yields **3 inversions** and the slider-crank yields **3 inversions**.

### 2.4.1 The Four Inversions of the Slider-Crank Chain (KTU Favourite!)

| # | Fixed Link | Resulting Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ground link fixed (slider slides) | **Reciprocating engine / compressor** | IC engines, air compressors |
| 2 | Crank fixed (slider becomes eccentric) | **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** | Shaping machines |
| 3 | Connecting rod fixed (crank becomes slotted) | **Crank and slotted-lever quick-return** | Shaping & slotting machines |
| 4 | Slider fixed (sliding becomes rotation about a pin) | **Oscillating cylinder engine / Rotary IC engine** | Rotary steam engines, hypocycloidal engine |

### 2.4.2 The Three Inversions of the Four-Bar Chain

1. **Beam Engine Mechanism** (ground link is the long one, crank rotates) — early steam engines
2. **Coupling Rod of Locomotive / Drag-Link** (shortest link is the ground, both side links crank) — railway coupling
3. **Crank-Rocker** mechanism (rocker oscillates, crank rotates) — windshield wipers, sewing machines

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planar DOF (Kutzbach) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$=links, $j_1$=lower pairs, $j_2$=higher pairs | dimensionless |
| 2 | Spatial DOF (Kutzbach) | $M = 6(n-1) - \sum (6 - f_i)$ | $f_i$ = DOF of $i^{th}$ joint | dimensionless |
| 3 | Grashof's Inequality | $s + l \leq p + q$ | $s$=shortest, $l$=longest, $p,q$=other two | m or mm |
| 4 | Transmission Angle | $\mu$ | angle between coupler and output | degrees |
| 5 | Slider Position | $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ | $r$=crank, $L$=rod, $\theta$=crank angle | m |
| 6 | Velocity Ratio (four-bar) | $\dfrac{\omega_3}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{O_2A \cdot \sin(\theta_2 - \mu)}{O_4B \cdot \sin(\theta_4 - \mu)}$ | complex number / instant-centre method | rad/s per rad/s |
| 7 | Number of Inversions | $n - 1$ | $n$ = number of links | integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Grashof's Law** is the single most-used test by mechanical designers when sizing four-bar linkages for industrial robots, walking machines, and vehicle suspensions.
> - **Kutzbach's equation** is the first step in **mechanism synthesis** — if $M \neq 1$, the designer knows the topology is wrong before fabricating anything.
> - **Inversions** explain how the *same* chain of links can become a *pump*, a *shaper*, or a *robot arm* — a concept foundational to reconfigurable mechatronics.

## 2.6 Transmission Angle — The Hidden Design Constraint

The **transmission angle** $\mu$ is the angle between the coupler and the follower (output) link. It is critical because:

- If $\mu$ approaches $0°$ or $180°$, force transmission becomes impossible (the mechanism **locks**).
- **Acceptable range:** $40° \leq \mu \leq 140°$ (KTU/standard textbook rule).

Using the cosine rule on the four-bar triangle $ABO_4$:

$$
\cos\mu = \dfrac{c^2 + d^2 - a^2 - b^2 + 2ab\cos\theta_2}{2cd}
$$

where $a, b, c, d$ are the four link lengths and $\theta_2$ is the input crank angle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kutzbach's Equation (Grübler's Criterion)

We derive the planar DOF equation from first principles so you can write it down cold in the exam.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Count

A planar mechanism has $n$ links. The fixed frame counts as one link. Each link, considered in isolation in a 2D plane, possesses:

$$
\text{DOF per free link in 2D} = 3
$$

(since a 2D rigid body can translate along $x$, translate along $y$, and rotate about $z$).

### Step 2 — Total Unconstrained DOF

If all $n$ links were free-floating in the plane, the total degrees of freedom would be:

$$
\text{Total unconstrained DOF} = 3n
$$

### Step 3 — Account for the Fixed Link

The fixed link loses all 3 of its DOF (it cannot translate or rotate). Effectively, only $n - 1$ links are mobile. So the available DOF before applying joint constraints is:

$$
\text{Mobile DOF pool} = 3(n - 1)
$$

### Step 4 — Subtract Joint Constraints

- A **lower pair** (revolute or prismatic) allows only 1 relative DOF, so it removes $3 - 1 = 2$ DOF.
- A **higher pair** (cam, gear) allows 2 relative DOF, so it removes $3 - 2 = 1$ DOF.

If the mechanism has $j_1$ lower pairs and $j_2$ higher pairs, the total constraints removed are $2j_1 + j_2$.

### Step 5 — Combine

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \text{Mobile DOF pool} - \text{Constraints removed} \\
  &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the Kutzbach/Grübler equation, derived from first principles. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example: DOF of a Four-Bar Chain

A four-bar chain has 4 links, all connected by revolute (lower) joints. Find its DOF.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \quad \text{(ground, crank, coupler, rocker)} \\
j_1 &= 4 \quad \text{(4 revolute joints)} \\
j_2 &= 0 \quad \text{(no higher pairs)} \\
\\
M &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
  &= 3(3) - 8 \\
  &= 9 - 8 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the four-bar chain has **1 DOF**, requiring one actuator (e.g., a motor on the crank). $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example: DOF of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

A slider-crank has 4 links: ground, crank (revolute-revolute), connecting rod, slider. The slider is connected to ground by a prismatic pair.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \\
j_1 &= 4 \quad \text{(3 revolute + 1 prismatic, all are lower pairs)} \\
j_2 &= 0 \\
\\
M &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

So the slider-crank also has **1 DOF**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example: DOF of an Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train

A simple planetary gear set has:
- Sun gear (1), 3 planet gears (3), ring gear (1), arm/carrier (1), frame (1) → $n = 7$ links
- Sun–planet teeth contact: 3 higher pairs (each planet meshes with sun) → 3
- Ring–planet teeth contact: 3 higher pairs → 3
- Planet–carrier revolute: 3 lower pairs → 3
- Sun–frame revolute: 1 → 1
- Ring–frame revolute: 1 → 1

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
j_1 &= 3 + 1 + 1 = 5 \quad \text{(lower pairs)} \\
j_2 &= 3 + 3 = 6 \quad \text{(higher pairs)} \\
n &= 7 \\
\\
M &= 3(7 - 1) - 2(5) - 6 \\
  &= 18 - 10 - 6 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

The planetary train has **2 DOF** — we need two inputs (e.g., sun + carrier) to control it fully. This is the famous **Willis equation** foundation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Slider Position Equation

Consider a slider-crank with crank of length $r$ rotating at angle $\theta$, connecting rod of length $L$, and slider moving along the x-axis.

### Step 1 — Position Vectors

The crank end $A$ is at $(r\cos\theta,\; r\sin\theta)$. The slider $B$ lies on the x-axis at $(x, 0)$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Rod-Length Constraint

The distance $AB$ equals $L$:

$$
(x - r\cos\theta)^2 + (0 - r\sin\theta)^2 = L^2
$$

### Step 3 — Expand and Solve for $x$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x - r\cos\theta)^2 + r^2\sin^2\theta &= L^2 \\
x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + r^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\theta &= L^2 \\
x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + r^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) &= L^2 \\
x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + r^2 &= L^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Quadratic in $x$

$$
x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + (r^2 - L^2) = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Quadratic Formula

$$
x = \dfrac{2r\cos\theta \pm \sqrt{4r^2\cos^2\theta - 4(r^2 - L^2)}}{2}
$$

$$
x = r\cos\theta \pm \sqrt{r^2\cos^2\theta - r^2 + L^2}
$$

$$
x = r\cos\theta \pm \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}
$$

The **positive root** is taken for a conventional slider-crank where the slider is ahead of the crank pivot:

$$
\boxed{x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.6 Grashof's Law — Worked Numerical Example

A four-bar mechanism has link lengths: $a = 50\,\text{mm}$ (input crank), $b = 150\,\text{mm}$ (coupler), $c = 100\,\text{mm}$ (output), $d = 200\,\text{mm}$ (ground). Determine the mechanism type.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 50, \quad l = 200, \quad p = 100, \quad q = 150 \\
\\
s + l &= 50 + 200 = 250 \\
p + q &= 100 + 150 = 250 \\
\\
s + l &= p + q
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **change-point** case (boundary). In practice, with the **shortest link (50 mm) fixed as the ground**, the mechanism behaves as a **double-crank** (drag-link). If instead the link of length 100 mm (adjacent to shortest) is fixed, it is a **crank-rocker**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Inversions — Analytical Decision Matrix

| Original Chain | Fixed Link Choice | Resulting Mechanism | KTU Exam Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slider-Crank | Ground | Reciprocating engine/compressor | **Most common** |
| Slider-Crank | Crank (shortest) | Whitworth quick return | Shaper |
| Slider-Crank | Coupler (long) | Crank-and-slotted-lever Q.R. | Shaper alternative |
| Slider-Crank | Slider | Oscillating cylinder engine | Rotary IC engine |
| Four-Bar | Shortest link fixed | Double-crank (drag-link) | Locomotive coupling |
| Four-Bar | Link adjacent to shortest | Crank-rocker | Sewing machine, wiper |
| Four-Bar | Link opposite to shortest | Double-rocker (Grashof) | Walking robot legs |
| Four-Bar | Longest link fixed | Triple-rocker (non-Grashof) | Suspended load oscillation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kinematic Pair Classification Flow

The following Mermaid block maps the **hierarchical classification of kinematic pairs** as expected in any KTU 2-mark or 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Kinematic Pair] --> B{Type of Contact}
    B -- Surface --> C[Lower Pair]
    B -- Line or Point --> D[Higher Pair]
    C --> E{Relative Motion}
    E -- Pure Rotation --> F[Revolute R]
    E -- Pure Sliding --> G[Prismatic P]
    E -- Helical --> H[Screw S]
    E -- Combined Rotation plus Sliding --> I[Rolling plus Sliding]
    D --> J{Relative Motion}
    J -- Cam plus Follower --> K[Cam Pair]
    J -- Gear Teeth --> L[Gear Pair]
    J -- Ball on Flat --> M[Ball Bearing]
    A --> N{DOF of Pair}
    N -- 1 DOF --> O[Lower Pair Members]
    N -- 2 DOF --> P[Higher Pair Members]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Kinematic Chain to Machine

The diagram below shows the **functional flow** from a single link up to a complete mechatronic machine, mapping the role of sensors and actuators at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Link 1 Rigid Body] --> J1{Kinematic Pair}
    L2[Link 2 Rigid Body] --> J1
    J1 --> KC[Kinematic Chain n links n-1 joints]
    KC --> FIX{Fix one link to ground}
    FIX --> MECH[Mechanism Definite Motion]
    MECH --> ACT[Add Actuator Driver Link]
    ACT --> MAC[Machine Energy Transformation]
    MAC --> SENS[Add Sensors Joint Encoders]
    SENS --> CTRL[Add Microcontroller]
    CTRL --> MECHTronic[Full Mechatronic System]
```

## 4.3 Four-Bar Chain Architecture (Block Diagram)

Since Mermaid cannot accurately sketch physical linkages, we represent the **functional interaction** of the four-bar chain as a directed block diagram showing the motion propagation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GR[Ground Link Fixed] --- RK1[Revolute Joint O2]
    GR --- RK4[Revolute Joint O4]
    RK1 --> CR[Input Crank Link a]
    CR -- Revolute Joint A --> CPL[Coupler Link b]
    CPL -- Revolute Joint B --> RKR[Output Rocker Link c]
    RKR --> RK4
    style GR fill:#f9c74f
    style CR fill:#90be6d
    style CPL fill:#43aa8b
    style RKR fill:#577590
```

## 4.4 Slider-Crank Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GND[Ground Link Fixed] --- RJ1[Revolute Joint O]
    RJ1 --> CRK[Crank Link r rotates]
    CRK -- Revolute Joint A --> ROD[Connecting Rod L]
    ROD -- Revolute Joint B --> SLD[Slider]
    SLD --- PJ[Prismatic Joint along x axis]
    PJ --> GND
    style GND fill:#f9c74f
    style CRK fill:#90be6d
    style ROD fill:#43aa8b
    style SLD fill:#f3722c
```

## 4.5 Mechanism versus Structure — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Assemble Links and Joints] --> COUNT[Count n j1 j2]
    COUNT --> KUTZ[Apply Kutzbach M equals 3n-1 minus 2j1 minus j2]
    KUTZ --> DEC{M equals}
    DEC -- 0 --> STRUC[Structure No motion]
    DEC -- 1 --> MECH1[Mechanism 1 DOF single input]
    DEC -- Greater than 1 --> MECHM[Multi DOF mechanism]
    DEC -- Less than 0 --> STATIC[Preloaded statically indeterminate]
    MECH1 --> GRASH[Apply Grashof Test s plus l versus p plus q]
    GRASH --> CLASSIFY{Type}
    CLASSIFY -- s plus l less than p plus q --> GR[Grashof Chain]
    CLASSIFY -- s plus l equals p plus q --> CP[Change Point]
    CLASSIFY -- s plus l greater than p plus q --> NG[Non Grashof]
```

## 4.6 Inversion Tree — Slider-Crank (3 Inversions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ORIG[Original Slider Crank Chain 4 links] --> INV1{Fix Link}
    INV1 -- Ground Fixed --> M1[1 Reciprocating Engine Compressor]
    INV1 -- Crank Fixed --> M2[2 Whitworth Quick Return]
    INV1 -- Coupler Fixed --> M3[3 Crank and Slotted Lever Q.R.]
    INV1 -- Slider Fixed --> M4[4 Oscillating Cylinder Engine]
    style M1 fill:#90be6d
    style M2 fill:#43aa8b
    style M3 fill:#577590
    style M4 fill:#f3722c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q: Define a kinematic chain. How is it different from a mechanism?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *kinematic chain* is an assembly of links connected by kinematic pairs such that each link moves relative to its neighbours with a definite relative motion. (1 mark)
> A *mechanism* is a kinematic chain in which **one link is fixed** (called the frame) so that the motion of all other links is defined with respect to it. (2 marks)
> **Distinction:** A chain is a *floating* assembly; a mechanism is a chain with a fixed reference, which converts it into a *constrained, useful motion producer*.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q: State Grashof's law. List the four-bar mechanism types obtained from it.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Grashof's Law:** For a four-bar linkage with link lengths $s, l, p, q$ (shortest to longest in some order), $s + l \leq p + q$. (1 mark)
> - $s + l < p + q$ → Grashof chain (crank-rocker, double-crank, or double-rocker) (1 mark)
> - $s + l = p + q$ → Change-point mechanism (1 mark)
> - $s + l > p + q$ → Non-Grashof (triple-rocker) (1 mark of credit for full classification)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Question Pattern, Dec 2024 Style]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define a kinematic pair. Classify kinematic pairs based on the type of contact and the type of relative motion. Give two examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **four inversions of the slider-crank kinematic chain**. State one practical application of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A kinematic pair is a joint between two links that allows a definite relative motion while constraining other motions.
>
> **Classification Table (5 marks):**
>
> | Basis of Classification | Class | Sub-type | Example |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Type of Contact** | Lower pair (surface contact) | Revolute, Prismatic, Screw, Spherical | Door hinge, piston in cylinder |
> | | Higher pair (line/point contact) | Cam-follower, gear, ball bearing | Cam and follower, meshing gears |
> | **Type of Relative Motion** | Sliding pair | Pure translation | Piston in cylinder |
> | | Turning pair | Pure rotation | Shaft in bearing |
> | | Rolling pair | Pure rolling | Ball bearing rolling |
> | | Screw pair | Helical | Lead screw of lathe |
> | | Spherical pair | 3D rotation | Ball-and-socket joint |

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The slider-crank chain has 4 links, so it admits $4 - 1 = 3$ inversions. The four commonly-listed inversions (counting the original as one) are:

1. **Original — Reciprocating Engine/Compressor (Ground link fixed)** *(1.5 marks)*:
   The slider moves linearly as the crank rotates. The piston is the slider, the connecting rod is the coupler. Used in IC engines, reciprocating compressors, and pumps.

2. **Crank Fixed — Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism (1.5 marks)**:
   The crank becomes a fixed short link; the slider becomes an eccentric slider. The cutting tool moves forward slowly and returns quickly. Used in shaping machines for metal cutting.

3. **Coupler Fixed — Crank and Slotted Lever Quick-Return (2 marks)**:
   The connecting rod is fixed and becomes a slotted lever pivoted on it. The crank rotates, the slider in the slot oscillates the slotted lever. Used in shaping and slotting machines.

4. **Slider Fixed — Oscillating Cylinder Engine (2 marks)**:
   The slider is fixed to the ground, and the cylinder (which contains the slider) oscillates around a pivot. Used in rotary steam engines and the hypocycloidal (rotary) IC engine.

> **Examiner Tip:** In a 7-mark sub-part, you need *at least one neat sketch* of the original slider-crank and one inversion. Sketches carry **2 of the 7 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and derive Kutzbach's criterion for the degrees of freedom of a planar mechanism. Apply it to find the DOF of a five-bar chain with 5 revolute joints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-bar mechanism has link lengths $a = 30\,\text{mm}$, $b = 80\,\text{mm}$, $c = 70\,\text{mm}$, $d = 100\,\text{mm}$. Apply Grashof's law and identify all possible inversions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Statement (1 mark):** The DOF (mobility) of a planar mechanism is given by:
$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Derivation (4 marks):** See Section 3.1 — start with 3 DOF per unconstrained planar link, total $3n$. Subtract 3 for the fixed link. Subtract joint constraints: $2j_1$ for lower pairs and $j_2$ for higher pairs.

**Application to 5-bar chain (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 5, \quad j_1 = 5, \quad j_2 = 0 \\
M &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(5) - 0 = 12 - 10 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$
A five-bar chain has **2 DOF** — it requires two actuators to control completely. Used in planar parallel manipulators.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Identify the link lengths: $a = 30$, $b = 80$, $c = 70$, $d = 100$.
- Shortest $s = 30$
- Longest $l = 100$
- Other two: $p = 70$, $q = 80$

Apply Grashof's inequality:
$$
s + l = 30 + 100 = 130, \quad p + q = 70 + 80 = 150
$$
Since $130 < 150$, the mechanism **satisfies Grashof's condition** (Class I, II, or III). **(3 marks)**

**Inversions available (4 marks):**

| Fixed Link | Resulting Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Shortest link (a = 30 mm) fixed | **Double-crank (Drag-link)** — both side links fully rotate |
| Link adjacent to shortest (b = 80 mm) fixed | **Crank-Rocker** — crank rotates fully, rocker oscillates |
| Link opposite to shortest (c = 70 mm) fixed | **Double-Rocker (Grashof)** — both side links oscillate, but coupler rotates fully |

> **Examiner Tip:** Students often confuse "which link is opposite the shortest." The opposite of the shortest is the link not connected to the shortest link by a revolute joint. In a four-bar loop, that is always the *coupler* if the shortest is a side link.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "kinematic chain" with "mechanism"** — a chain is *floating*, a mechanism has a *fixed link*. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to count the ground link** in $n$ when applying Kutzbach's equation. The ground IS a link. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong Grashof classification** — many students just say "Grashof" without specifying *which* of the 3 sub-classes. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Sketches missing in inversion questions** — in a 7-mark inversion question, the sketch carries 2 marks. Always draw neat free-body diagrams with labelled links and joints. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing DOF of joint vs DOF of mechanism** — Kutzbach's $M$ is *mechanism* DOF, not joint DOF. (–1 mark)
> 6. **In slider-crank inversions**, students often forget the *oscillating cylinder engine* (slider fixed) inversion — it is a favourite KTU question. (–2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A **kinematic chain** is an *unfixed* assembly of links joined by kinematic pairs.
- [x] A **mechanism** is a chain with **one link fixed** to the ground.
- [x] A **lower pair** has surface contact and 1 DOF; a **higher pair** has line/point contact and 2 DOF.
- [x] **Revolute (R)** = turning pair; **Prismatic (P)** = sliding pair; both are lower pairs.
- [x] **Kutzbach's equation (planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [x] **Kutzbach's equation (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - \sum(6 - f_i)$.
- [x] **M = 0** → structure; **M = 1** → single-DOF mechanism; **M > 1** → multi-DOF.
- [x] **Four-bar chain** always has 1 DOF regardless of link lengths.
- [x] **Grashof's law:** $s + l \leq p + q$ → Grashof; $=$ → change point; $>$ → non-Grashof.
- [x] **Grashof inversions (4-bar):** shortest fixed → double-crank; adjacent to shortest fixed → crank-rocker; opposite to shortest fixed → double-rocker.
- [x] **Slider-crank** has 1 DOF; position $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$.
- [x] **Slider-crank inversions:** reciprocating engine, Whitworth Q.R., crank-and-slotted-lever Q.R., oscillating cylinder engine.
- [x] **Four-bar inversions:** beam engine, locomotive coupling (drag-link), crank-rocker.
- [x] **Transmission angle** $\mu$ should be between $40°$ and $140°$ for good force transmission.
- [x] **Inversions** = fixing different links of the same chain to obtain new mechanisms; an $n$-link chain has $n - 1$ inversions.
- [x] **Epicyclic gear train** typically has $M = 2$ — needs two inputs.
- [x] In mechatronics, the kinematic chain is the *mechanical backbone*; sensors measure joint DOFs, actuators drive joint DOFs, and the controller coordinates them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **cam** is a mechanical link (rotating or reciprocating/oscillating) that imparts a prescribed motion to another link, called the **follower**, by means of direct contact between a curved surface (or a groove) of the cam and a roller, flat face, or knife-edge on the follower. The cam mechanism is classified as a **higher kinematic pair** since the two contacting surfaces have point or line contact with relative motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Cams belong to the category of *intermittent motion-generating mechanisms* and are extensively used in **mechatronic actuators** such as those found in internal combustion engines, automatic tool changers, packaging machinery, and pick-and-place robotic systems.

**Classification based on shape:**
- **Plate (Disc) Cams** – Flat, rotating disc with a contoured profile.
- **Cylindrical (Drum) Cams** – Cylindrical member with a groove cut on its surface.
- **Conical Cams** – Truncated cone with contoured surface.
- **Spherical Cams** – Hemispherical body with contoured profile.
- **Face Cams** – The follower rides on the face of the cam (groove on the face).

**Classification based on follower type:**
- **Knife-edge follower**
- **Flat-faced follower**
- **Roller follower** (most common – reduces wear and friction)
- **Spherical-faced (mushroom) follower**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **winding road going up a hill**. As your car (the follower) follows the curves of the road (the cam), your vertical position changes in a very specific way based on the shape of the road. Some sections are gentle, some are steep, and some are flat. The road *dictates* the motion. The cam is that "road" — it is master to the follower's slave.

A cam converts **continuous rotary motion** (e.g., a motor shaft spinning at constant speed) into a **predetermined, often complex oscillatory or reciprocating motion** of the follower. The key engineering insight is that the *shape* of the cam profile directly *programs* the output motion.

**Key Performance Metrics** (used as standard KTU values):
- **Base Circle Radius ($R_b$):** The smallest circle that can be drawn tangent to the cam profile.
- **Pressure Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the normal to the cam profile at the point of contact. Should be ≤ **30°** for force-closure cams.
- **Number of Standard Finite Programs:** 4 (Uniform Velocity, Simple Harmonic, Cycloidal, Constant Acceleration/deceleration).

## 1.3 Cam Nomenclature – Core Terms

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Trace Point** | A theoretical point on the follower used to generate the cam profile. |
| **Pitch Point** | Point on the pitch curve where pressure angle is maximum. |
| **Pitch Curve** | Locus of the trace point. |
| **Pitch Circle** | Circle passing through pitch points. Radius = $R_p$. |
| **Base Circle** | Smallest circle tangent to cam profile. Radius = $R_b$. |
| **Prime Circle** | Smallest circle that the follower can enter. Radius = $R_0$. |
| **Pressure Angle** | Angle between follower direction and normal at contact point. |
| **Lift ($h$)** | Maximum displacement of follower from its lowest position. |
| **Dwell** | Period where follower remains stationary. |
| **Rise-Return (Outstroke & Return Stroke)** | The portions of follower motion. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Follower Motion Programs (Displacement Diagrams)

The four canonical motion programs studied in KTU examinations are:

### (A) Uniform Velocity (UV)
- **Displacement:** Linear with respect to cam angle.
- **Velocity:** Constant.
- **Acceleration:** Zero except at the ends (theoretically infinite spikes at start/end).
- **Use:** Low-speed, low-precision machinery.
- **Drawback:** Sudden jumps in acceleration cause vibration, noise, and impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Uniform velocity is *never* used in high-speed mechanisms because the theoretical infinite acceleration at the start and end of the stroke is physically impossible and causes severe shock loading.

### (B) Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
- **Displacement:** $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$
- **Velocity:** $v = \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$
- **Acceleration:** $a = -\dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2}\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$
- **Use:** Medium-speed mechanisms (e.g., pumps, small engines).
- **Drawback:** Acceleration curve is discontinuous at the ends — moderate shock.

### (C) Cycloidal Motion (CM)
- **Displacement:** $y = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$
- **Velocity:** $v = h\omega\left[\dfrac{1}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{\beta}\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$
- **Acceleration:** $a = h\omega^2\cdot\dfrac{2\pi}{\beta^2}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$
- **Use:** High-speed machinery (e.g., I.C. engine inlet/exhaust valves).
- **Advantage:** Acceleration is **zero at both ends** — no shock at transition.

### (D) Constant Acceleration and Deceleration
- **Displacement:** $y = 2h\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^2$ for $0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$, then $h\left[1 - 2\left(1-\dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^2\right]$ for $\beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta$.
- **Velocity:** Triangular profile (peaks at mid-stroke).
- **Acceleration:** Constant $\pm a_0$ during each half — sudden sign reversal at mid-stroke.
- **Drawback:** Infinite jerk at mid-stroke (discontinuity in acceleration).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base Circle Radius** | $R_b = R_0 - r_f$ (roller follower) | mm |
| 2 | **Pressure Angle** | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v - \omega R_p}{\omega(R_0 + y)}$ | degrees |
| 3 | **Minimum Base Circle** | $R_{b,min} = h\tan\alpha_{max}$ | tangent follower |
| 4 | **SHM Displacement** | $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | mm |
| 5 | **SHM Max Velocity** | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}$ | m/s |
| 6 | **SHM Max Acceleration** | $a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2}$ | m/s² |
| 7 | **Cycloidal Displacement** | $y = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | mm |
| 8 | **Cycloidal Max Velocity** | $v_{max} = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ | m/s |
| 9 | **Cycloidal Max Acceleration** | $a_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | m/s² |
| 10 | **Const. Accel. Max Acceleration** | $a_{max} = \dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | m/s² |
| 11 | **Cam Speed** | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s, N in rpm |
| 12 | **Force on Follower** | $F_n = \dfrac{F_t}{\cos\alpha}$ | N, $F_t$ tangential load |
| 13 | **Side thrust on follower** | $F_s = F_t \tan\alpha$ | N |
| 14 | **Torque on cam shaft** | $T = F_t \cdot R_p$ | N·m |
| 15 | **Length of roller follower stem** | $L \ge h + (R_0 - R_b)\cos\alpha_{max}$ | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The pressure angle determines the **side thrust** on the follower stem, which causes **friction, wear, and bending stress**. The KTU standard limit is **30°** for a translating roller follower and **45°** for an oscillating roller follower.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cams are fundamental in:
- **Automotive:** Valve actuation in I.C. engines (high-speed cycloidal cams).
- **Mechatronics:** Pick-and-place robots, packaging machines, automatic lathes.
- **Textile:** Cam-actuated shed-forming mechanism in looms.
- **Hydraulics/Pneumatics:** Cam-actuated spool valves in fluid power systems.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Cam-driven mechanisms in printers and scanners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Displacement Equation for Simple Harmonic Motion

**Step 1:** Assume the follower undergoes simple harmonic motion about the midpoint of the stroke. Let the total rise be $h$ and the cam angle during rise be $\beta$.

**Step 2:** The displacement from the mid-position follows the SHM form:
$$y - \dfrac{h}{2} = -\dfrac{h}{2}\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply boundary conditions:
- At $\theta = 0$: $y = 0$ (substitute) → $0 - h/2 = -h/2 \cos(0) = -h/2$ ✓
- At $\theta = \beta$: $y = h$ → $h - h/2 = -h/2 \cos(\pi) = h/2$ ✓

**Step 4:** Solve for $y$:
$$y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate to obtain velocity. Using chain rule with $\theta = \omega t$:
$$v = \dfrac{dy}{dt} = \dfrac{dy}{d\theta}\cdot\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$$

**Step 6:** Differentiate the displacement:
$$\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{\beta}\cdot\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta}\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:
$$v = \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 8:** The maximum velocity occurs at $\theta = \beta/2$ (where $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$):
$$v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}$$

**Step 9:** Differentiate $v$ to obtain acceleration:
$$a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \omega \dfrac{dv}{d\theta} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{\beta}\cdot\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 10:** Simplify:
$$a = \dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2}\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 11:** Maximum (magnitude) acceleration occurs at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$:
$$a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign is dropped because we are interested in magnitude. The KTU board accepts the magnitude form. Always mention the **boundary condition that produced the result**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cycloidal Motion Equation

**Step 1:** The cycloidal motion is the motion of a point on the circumference of a circle of radius $h/(2\pi)$ rolling along the displacement line.

**Step 2:** Let the rolling circle rotate by angle $\phi$ while the cam rotates by $\theta$. The relationship:
$$\phi = \dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}$$

**Step 3:** The displacement is the rolling displacement minus the vertical component of the rolling circle:
$$y = \dfrac{h}{\beta}\theta - \dfrac{h}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Rewriting compactly:
$$y = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$:
$$\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = h\left[\dfrac{1}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\cdot\dfrac{2\pi}{\beta}\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right] = \dfrac{h}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6:** Velocity:
$$v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 7:** Max velocity at $\theta = \beta$ (where $\cos(2\pi) = 1$):
$$v_{max} = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$$

**Step 8:** Differentiate velocity to get acceleration:
$$a = \omega\dfrac{dv}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h\omega^2}{\beta}\cdot\dfrac{2\pi}{\beta}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^2}{\beta^2}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 9:** Max acceleration at $\theta = \beta/4$ (where $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$):
$$a_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

**Step 10:** Observe that at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$, $\sin(0) = \sin(2\pi) = 0$, so **acceleration is zero at both ends** — the hallmark property of cycloidal motion and the reason it is used in high-speed cams.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Angle Analysis (Knife-edge Tangent Follower)

**Step 1:** Draw the cam profile with the follower. Let $R$ = radius of curvature of cam profile at point of contact, and $r$ = distance from cam centre to trace point.

**Step 2:** The pressure angle is the angle between the normal at the contact point and the line of follower motion.

**Step 3:** Using velocity analysis: Let the contact point have absolute velocity components.

**Step 4:** Velocity of the contact point on the cam (rigid body rotating with cam):
- Tangential: $v_t = \omega r$ perpendicular to $r$
- The cam's surface has velocity along the tangent to the cam at the contact point.

**Step 5:** The follower moves with velocity $v_f$ along the follower axis. The component of the cam surface velocity along the follower axis must equal $v_f$ (no separation).

**Step 6:** The component of cam surface velocity along follower direction:
$$v_f = \omega r \cos\alpha - v_f^{rel} \text{ (accounting for relative sliding)}$$

**Step 7:** For the general case (Skibinski' formulation), the standard KTU textbook expression is:
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \mp e}{R_0 + y \pm e\cdot\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}$$

Where the upper signs are for **rise** and lower for **return** with offset followers. For a **radial (in-line) follower** with zero offset ($e = 0$):
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v - \omega R_p}{\omega(R_0 + y)}$$

where $R_p$ is the pitch circle radius and $v$ is the follower velocity.

**Step 8:** The pressure angle is **maximum** when $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ is maximum (i.e., at peak velocity).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minimum Radius of Base Circle for a Radial Cam

**Step 1:** For a tangent follower, the displacement is $y$ at a pressure angle $\alpha$.

**Step 2:** The geometric relation between the cam radius and follower position:
$$R = R_b + y$$

**Step 3:** For a circular arc cam with a knife-edge follower, the contact normal is along the radius $R$. The pressure angle satisfies:
$$\sin\alpha = \dfrac{R_b + y - R_b}{R} = \dfrac{y}{R_b + y}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum pressure angle occurs at maximum $y = h$:
$$\sin\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{h}{R_b + h}$$

**Step 5:** For a specified maximum pressure angle, the minimum base circle radius is:
$$R_{b,min} = h\left(\dfrac{1}{\sin\alpha_{max}} - 1\right) = h\cdot\dfrac{1 - \sin\alpha_{max}}{\sin\alpha_{max}}$$

**Step 6:** Equivalently using tangent (for small angles, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha$):
$$R_{b,min} \approx h\tan\alpha_{max}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cam Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Motor / Prime Mover] -->|Rotary input| CS[Cam Shaft]
    CS -->|Rotates with omega| CP[Cam Profile Surface]
    CP -->|Direct contact at trace point| FT[Follower Tip Roller or Edge]
    FT -->|Translates or oscillates| FO[Follower Output Link]
    FO -->|Mechanical work| LD[Load / Actuator]
    CP -->|Normal force Fn| FT
    CP -.->|Pressure angle alpha governs| PA[Side Thrust and Friction]
    PA -->|Limits cam size| RB[Base Circle Radius Rb]
    RB -.->|Design constraint| CP
```

## 4.2 Cam Profile Generation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Lift h, Cam angle beta, omega, Follower type] --> B{Choose Motion Program}
    B -->|Low speed| C1[Uniform Velocity]
    B -->|Medium speed| C2[Simple Harmonic Motion]
    B -->|High speed| C3[Cycloidal Motion]
    B -->|Special use| C4[Constant Accel Decel]
    C1 --> D[Compute displacement y theta]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Compute velocity and acceleration]
    E --> F[Check max pressure angle andlt 30deg]
    F -->|Satisfied| G[Plot cam profile using inversion method]
    F -->|Violated| H[Increase Rb or use offset follower]
    H --> F
    G --> I[Manufacture cam: milling, EDM, or 3D print]
```

## 4.3 Cam Kinematic Pair Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CamMechanism
        D1[Cam Disc rotating at omega]
        D2[Pitch Circle of radius Rp]
        D3[Base Circle of radius Rb]
        D4[Trace Point of Follower]
        D5[Follower Stem moving linearly]
        D6[Roller at Follower Tip radius r]
        D7[Pressure Angle alpha at contact]
        D1 --- D2
        D2 --- D3
        D3 --- D4
        D4 --- D5
        D4 --- D6
        D4 --- D7
    end
```

## 4.4 Comparison of Four Standard Motion Programs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Uniform Velocity] -->|Linear y, Const v, Infinite a at ends| A1[Shock load, low speed only]
    B[Simple Harmonic] -->|Cosine y, Sinusoidal v, Cosine a| B1[Moderate shock, medium speed]
    C[Cycloidal] -->|Smooth y, Continuous v, Zero a at ends| C1[No shock, high speed preferred]
    D[Const Accel Decel] -->|Parabolic y, Triangular v, Square a| D1[Infinite jerk at mid stroke]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define the term **pressure angle** in a cam mechanism. What is the recommended maximum value for a translating roller follower and why?

**Model Answer:**

The **pressure angle** is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the **normal to the cam profile** at the point of contact.

For a **translating roller follower**, the KTU-recommended maximum is **30°**.

> **Reasoning:** Beyond 30°, the side thrust on the follower stem becomes excessive, leading to (i) increased **friction** in the guide, (ii) **wear** of the follower and cam surface, and (iii) **jamming** in extreme cases, which prevents proper follower motion.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Differentiate between **pitch circle** and **base circle** of a cam.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pitch Circle ($R_p$) | Base Circle ($R_b$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Circle passing through the pitch points of the cam profile. | Smallest circle that is tangent to the cam profile. |
| **Significance** | Used to find the **pressure angle** ($R_p$ appears in $\tan\alpha$ formula). | Defines the **minimum cam size** and the follower rest position. |
| **Magnitude** | $R_p > R_b$ | Smallest of all reference circles. |
| **Location** | Coincides with the **prime circle** for radial followers. | Centered at the cam rotation axis. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**

A cam rotates at a uniform speed of **1500 rpm** and gives the following motion to a **translating roller follower** during its outstroke of 60°:
- Rise of **30 mm** with **simple harmonic motion (SHM)**.
- Dwell of **20°**.
- Return of **30 mm** with **uniform velocity (UV)**.
- Remaining dwell to complete 360°.

The roller diameter is **15 mm** and the prime circle radius is **40 mm**.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Plot the **displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams** for the follower motion and state the **maximum velocity and acceleration** during outstroke.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Find the **minimum radius of the base circle** if the maximum permissible pressure angle is **30°**.

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a):**

**Given data:**
- Speed $N = 1500$ rpm → $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08$ rad/s
- Lift $h = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- Rise angle $\beta = 60° = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ rad
- Return angle $\beta_r = 60°$ (assumed equal, since the return is given with UV)

**Maximum velocity during SHM outstroke:**
$$v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}$$

Substituting:
$$v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.030 \times 50\pi}{2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{3}} = \dfrac{0.030 \times 50\pi \times 3}{2} = \dfrac{4.5\pi}{2} = 2.25\pi = 7.069 \text{ m/s}$$

**Maximum acceleration during SHM outstroke:**
$$a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2}$$

Substituting:
$$a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 0.030 \times (50\pi)^2}{2 \times \left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 0.030 \times 2500\pi^2}{2 \times \dfrac{\pi^2}{9}}$$

$$a_{max} = \dfrac{0.030 \times 2500\pi^4 \times 9}{2\pi^2} = \dfrac{67.5\pi^2}{2} = 33.75\pi^2 \approx 333.2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Displacement equation (SHM outstroke):**
$$y = \dfrac{30}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{60°}\right)\right] = 15\left[1 - \cos\left(3\theta\right)\right] \text{ mm}$$

The displacement starts at 0 (at $\theta = 0°$), reaches 30 mm at $\theta = 60°$, then dwells (constant at 30 mm) for 20°, returns uniformly over the next 60°, and dwells for the remaining 220°.

**Tabulated values for SHM outstroke** ($\theta$ in degrees, $y$ in mm):

| $\theta$ | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ (mm) | 0.00 | 0.67 | 2.68 | 6.00 | 10.68 | 17.32 | 30.00 |

**Plots:**
- **Displacement diagram**: A cosine curve from 0 to 30 mm during 0°–60°, flat at 30 mm during 60°–80°, straight line from 30 to 0 mm during 80°–140°, flat at 0 mm during 140°–360°.
- **Velocity diagram**: Sinusoidal (positive lobe) during 0°–60°, zero during dwell, constant negative during 80°–140° (UV), zero during final dwell.
- **Acceleration diagram**: Cosine curve during 0°–60°, zero during dwell, zero during UV return, zero during final dwell.

---

**Part (b):**

For SHM, the velocity at any angle:
$$v = \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

The pressure angle formula (radial follower, no offset):
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v - \omega R_p}{\omega(R_0 + y)}$$

For the maximum pressure angle to be **30°**, and the prime circle $R_0 = 40$ mm, we find the **minimum base circle radius**.

Using the simplified formula for minimum base circle:
$$R_{b,min} = h\tan\alpha_{max} = 30 \times \tan 30° = 30 \times 0.5774 = 17.32 \text{ mm}$$

However, this is for a knife-edge follower. For a **roller follower**, the base circle radius is:
$$R_b = R_0 - r_f$$

where $r_f$ = roller radius = $\dfrac{15}{2} = 7.5$ mm.

But the more accurate computation requires:
$$R_b = \sqrt{(R_0 + h)^2 \cos^2\alpha_{max} - (R_0 \sin\alpha_{max} + h\tan\alpha_{max})^2}$$

Wait — let us use the standard KTU expression. The minimum base circle radius is found by:
$$\boxed{R_{b,min} = h\left(\dfrac{1 - \sin\alpha_{max}}{\sin\alpha_{max}}\right) = 30 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.5}{0.5} = 30 \text{ mm}}$$

For the actual base circle, we apply the constraint that the cam should be **manufacturable** (no undercut). The actual $R_b$ is taken as the greater of $R_{b,min}$ and the prime circle minus roller radius:
$$R_b \geq R_0 - r_f = 40 - 7.5 = 32.5 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, the **minimum base circle radius = 30 mm** (from pressure angle constraint), and a practical value of **35 mm** is recommended for the design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

A **disc cam** with a **roller follower** (roller radius 10 mm) is required to give the following motion:
- **Outstroke (rise)** of 40 mm with **cycloidal motion** during 180° of cam rotation.
- **Dwell** for 30°.
- **Return stroke** of 40 mm with **SHM** during 150° of cam rotation.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the **maximum velocity and maximum acceleration** during the outstroke if the cam runs at **600 rpm**.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the prime circle radius is **50 mm**, calculate the **maximum pressure angle** during the outstroke. What happens if the cam speed is doubled?

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a):**

**Given data:**
- $N = 600$ rpm → $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 20\pi = 62.83$ rad/s
- Lift $h = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
- Rise angle $\beta = 180° = \pi$ rad

**Maximum velocity during cycloidal outstroke:**
$$v_{max} = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$$

Substituting:
$$v_{max} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.040 \times 20\pi}{\pi} = \dfrac{1.6\pi}{\pi} = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Maximum acceleration during cycloidal outstroke:**
$$a_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

Substituting:
$$a_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 0.040 \times (20\pi)^2}{\pi^2} = \dfrac{0.080\pi \times 400\pi^2}{\pi^2} = 0.080 \times 400\pi = 32\pi$$

$$a_{max} = 32\pi \approx 100.53 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Maximum pressure angle analysis:**

The pressure angle is maximum when the velocity $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ is maximum (peak velocity point).

For cycloidal motion:
$$\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

The maximum value occurs at $\theta = \beta$ where $\cos(2\pi) = 1$, giving:
$$\left(\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}\right)_{max} = \dfrac{2h}{\beta} = \dfrac{2 \times 40}{180°} = 0.4444 \text{ mm/degree} = 25.46 \text{ mm/rad}$$

(Note: $0.4444 \times \dfrac{180°}{\pi} = 25.46$ mm/rad)

The pressure angle formula:
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v/\omega - R_p}{R_0 + y}$$

At the peak velocity point ($\theta = \beta$ for cycloidal), $y = h = 40$ mm. We assume $R_p \approx R_0 = 50$ mm (radial follower, prime circle ≈ pitch circle at peak).

$$v_{max} = \omega \cdot \left(\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}\right)_{max} = 20\pi \times 0.02546 = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$$

(consistent with Part a)

$$\tan\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{\dfrac{v_{max}}{\omega} - R_p}{R_0 + y} = \dfrac{0.02546 - 0.050}{0.050 + 0.040} = \dfrac{-0.02454}{0.090} = -0.2727$$

Wait — the formula uses a different sign convention. Let us use the **standard textbook form**:
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \mp e}{R_0 + y \pm e\cdot\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}$$

For a **radial follower** with $e = 0$:
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}{R_0 + y}$$

Substituting:
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{25.46 \text{ mm/rad}}{50 + 40} = \dfrac{25.46}{90} = 0.2829$$

$$\alpha_{max} = \arctan(0.2829) = 15.79°$$

**Effect of doubling the speed:**

If $N' = 1200$ rpm, then $\omega' = 2\omega$. The displacement $y$ depends on $\theta$ (not on time), so the *shape* of the cam profile does not change. However:
- The maximum velocity **doubles** to 3.2 m/s.
- The maximum acceleration **quadruples** to $4 a_{max} = 402.1$ m/s².
- The pressure angle remains **unchanged** at 15.79°.

> **Important Insight:** The pressure angle is a **geometric** property of the cam profile and is independent of operating speed. It depends only on the cam shape, the follower type, and the position of the contact point. This is why the pressure angle is checked during *design*, not during operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** (or vice versa) in velocity/acceleration units. Always check units first.
> 2. **Confusing $\beta$ in degrees vs radians**. SHM and cycloidal formulas using $\beta$ in radians. Convert $\beta = 60°$ to $\pi/3$ rad.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the SHM acceleration formula. The correct formula is $a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}$, not $\dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$.
> 4. **Sign error in the pressure angle formula** when the follower is in the return phase. KTU expects students to consider both rise and return.
> 5. **Confusing base circle ($R_b$), pitch circle ($R_p$), and prime circle ($R_0$)**. A roller follower has $R_b = R_0 - r_f$, NOT $R_b = R_0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Cam Types**: Plate, cylindrical, conical, spherical, face — plate cam is the most common.
- **Follower Types**: Knife-edge, flat-faced, roller (most common), spherical.
- **Reference Circles**: Base circle ($R_b$) < Prime circle ($R_0$) < Pitch circle ($R_p$).
- **For Roller Follower**: $R_b = R_0 - r_f$ where $r_f$ = roller radius.
- **Pressure Angle Limit**: 30° (translating), 45° (oscillating). Beyond this, jamming occurs.
- **SHM Max Accel**: $a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2}$ — discontinuous at stroke ends.
- **Cycloidal Max Accel**: $a_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\beta^2}$ — **zero at both ends** (best for high-speed cams).
- **Uniform Velocity**: Theoretical infinite accel at ends — never use in high-speed systems.
- **Constant Acc/Dec**: Infinite jerk at mid-stroke — special applications only.
- **Min Base Circle Formula**: $R_{b,min} = h\left(\dfrac{1 - \sin\alpha_{max}}{\sin\alpha_{max}}\right)$ or approximately $h\tan\alpha_{max}$.
- **Side Thrust**: $F_s = F_t \tan\alpha$ — increases friction in follower guide.
- **Cam Speed Conversion**: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s, where $N$ is in rpm.
- **Trace Point**: Theoretical point used to draw the cam profile; for a roller follower, the trace point is at the **roller centre**.
- **Undercutting**: Occurs when $R_b$ is too small relative to the curvature of the pitch curve — produces a self-intersecting cam profile. KTU marks this as a **manufacturing defect**.
- **KTU Favourite Question Types**:
   - Derive SHM / Cycloidal displacement, velocity, acceleration equations.
   - Compare the four motion programs (table or graph).
   - Calculate maximum velocity and acceleration for a given cam.
   - Find the minimum base circle radius from pressure angle constraint.
   - Discuss undercutting and its prevention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Trains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gear Trains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Train:** A gear train is a mechanical system of two or more meshing gears that transmit rotational motion and power from one shaft to another. The meshing gears may be arranged on parallel, intersecting, or non-parallel/non-intersecting (skew) shafts, and the system is characterized by a **train value** (velocity ratio) that defines the speed and torque transformation between the driver and the driven shaft.

In the context of **Mechatronics (PCBRT601)**, gear trains form the foundational *actuation layer* that interfaces electrical prime movers (servomotors, stepper motors, DC motors) with mechanical loads. They are essential in robotic joints, CNC spindles, automotive transmissions, and precision positioning systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bicycle Gear System**
> Imagine riding a bicycle. When you shift to a *low gear* (small chainring, large rear sprocket), the wheel turns many times for each pedal stroke, making it easy to climb a hill but slow on flat ground. When you shift to a *high gear* (large chainring, small rear sprocket), each pedal stroke drives the wheel powerfully but fewer times. A **gear train** is the mechanical equivalent of this — it lets a single motor (your legs) operate efficiently across a wide range of speeds and torques, simply by changing the *gear ratio*.

## 1.3 Why Gear Trains Matter in Mechatronics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Functional Roles:**
> 1. **Speed Reduction / Multiplication** — Match motor RPM to load requirements.
> 2. **Torque Amplification** — Trade rotational speed for mechanical advantage (Power In ≈ Power Out, neglecting losses).
> 3. **Direction Reversal & Motion Routing** — Transfer rotation between non-collinear shafts (e.g., bevel, worm, hypoid).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics

- **Module (m)** — the metric standard for gear tooth size: $m = \dfrac{d}{T}$, where $d$ is the pitch diameter and $T$ is the number of teeth. Standard ISO values: **m = 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm**.
- **Pressure Angle (φ)** — typically **20°** (full-depth involute) for most modern gears.
- **Pitch Circle** — the imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll without slipping.
- **Center Distance (C)** — distance between the centers of two meshing gears: $C = \dfrac{d_1 + d_2}{2}$.

## 1.5 Classification of Gear Trains

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Classification (KTU Syllabus High-Yield):**
>
> | S.No | Type | Description |
> |------|------|-------------|
> | 1 | **Simple Gear Train** | Each shaft carries only **one** gear; every gear meshes with at most one other on adjacent shafts. |
> | 2 | **Compound Gear Train** | At least one shaft carries **two** or more gears (a compound gear), enabling large speed reductions in compact spaces. |
> | 3 | **Reverted Gear Train** | A special compound arrangement where the **input and output shafts are coaxial** (share the same axis). Common in automotive gearboxes. |
> | 4 | **Epicyclic / Planetary Gear Train** | Gears rotate about their own axes *and* the axes themselves move (planetary motion). High reduction ratios, coaxial input/output, and excellent for robotic joints. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle of Meshing

When two gears mesh externally, their linear pitch-line velocities are equal (no-slip condition):

$$v_1 = v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \pi d_1 N_1 = \pi d_2 N_2$$

Since the module $m$ is identical for mating gears ($d = mT$):

$$N_1 T_1 = N_2 T_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed is inversely proportional to the number of teeth.** A *small driver* (few teeth) turning a *large driven* (many teeth) gives **speed reduction** with **torque amplification**.

## 2.2 Train Value (Velocity Ratio)

> [!NOTE]
> **Train Value (i):** Defined as the ratio of the speed of the first driver to the speed of the last driven gear. It is always taken as a positive number representing the magnitude of speed change.
>
> $$i = \frac{N_{\text{first}}}{N_{\text{last}}} = \frac{\text{Product of teeth on DRIVEN gears}}{\text{Product of teeth on DRIVER gears}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference for Gear Train Problems**

| S.No | Gear Train Type | Key Formula | Variables / Notes |
|------|-----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Simple (two gears) | $i = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | $T$ = teeth, $N$ = RPM |
| 2 | Simple (multi-stage) | $i_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \dfrac{T_4}{T_3} \cdot \dfrac{T_6}{T_5}\cdots$ | Each intermediate shaft is *driven* then *driver* |
| 3 | Compound | $i_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{\text{Product of all driven teeth}}{\text{Product of all driver teeth}}$ | Count each gear only once in numerator or denominator |
| 4 | Reverted | $i = \dfrac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}$ AND $C_1 = C_2$ (coaxiality) | Used to solve for unknown teeth count |
| 5 | Epicyyclic (Tabular Method) | See Section 2.5 | Fix one arm/gear, use relative motion |
| 6 | Power transmission | $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$ → $T_{\text{in}} \omega_{\text{in}} = T_{\text{out}} \omega_{\text{out}}$ | $T$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular velocity |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Robotics:** Harmonic drives and planetary gearheads reduce motor speed (3000 RPM) to joint speeds (~60 RPM) while multiplying torque ~50×.
> - **Automotive:** Manual transmissions use compound + reverted gear trains to provide 5–6 forward ratios + reverse.
> - **CNC Machines:** Rack-and-pinion conversions of rotational gear motion into linear table feed.
> - **Aerospace:** Epicyclic gearboxes in helicopter rotor transmissions handle multi-MW power with coaxial shafts and self-contained lubrication.

## 2.5 Epicyclic Gear Train — The Heart of Modern Mechatronics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Critical):** An epicyclic gear train has one or more gears whose axes **revolve about another axis** (the *arm* or *carrier*). The central gear is called the **sun**, the orbiting gears are **planets**, and the outer ring is the **annulus** (or *ring gear*).

### 2.5.1 Types of Epicyclic Arrangements

- **P-type (Planetary):** Sun, planets, ring (annulus), arm. Most common.
- **N-type (Star):** No sun gear; only planets and annulus.
- **H-type (Solar):** Two sun gears (internal and external) with planets.

### 2.5.2 The Tabular (Relative Motion) Method

To analyze an epicyclic gear train, we **fix one component** (typically the arm) and compute speeds relative to it. This converts the planetary system into a simple gear train solvable by standard ratios.

**Step-by-step logic (intuitive):**
1. Imagine *freezing* the arm (carrier). The system now behaves like an ordinary simple/compound gear train.
2. Compute the speed of the *last* gear relative to the arm.
3. Re-introduce the arm's actual motion using vector superposition of angular velocities.
4. Solve for the unknown speed using the constraint (e.g., the ring gear is fixed → its absolute speed = 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **General Epicyclic Equation (Willis Equation):**
> For an epicyclic gear train with sun (S), planet (P), ring (R), and arm (A):
> $$\frac{N_S - N_A}{N_R - N_A} = \pm \frac{\text{Product of teeth on driving gears (relative motion)}}{\text{Product of teeth on driven gears (relative motion)}}$$
> The sign is **+** for external meshing reversal and **−** for internal (ring) meshing which preserves direction.

## 2.6 Efficiency Considerations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficiency (η):** Ratio of output power to input power. For gear trains, losses arise from:
> - **Sliding friction** at tooth contact (especially high in worm gears: η ≈ 0.5).
> - **Churning** of lubricant (epicyclic trains are prone).
> - **Windage** losses at high speeds.
>
> $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3 \cdots \eta_n$$
> (Efficiencies of stages multiply.)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Train Value of a Simple Gear Train

**Setup:** Three gears in mesh on three parallel shafts: Gear 1 (driver) meshes with Gear 2 on intermediate shaft 2; Gear 2 (acting as driver) meshes with Gear 3 (driven) on shaft 3.

**Step 1 — Apply the no-slip condition at the first mesh:**

$$\pi d_1 N_1 = \pi d_2 N_2$$

$$\Rightarrow N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{d_1}{d_2} = N_1 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the no-slip condition at the second mesh:**

$$\pi d_2 N_2 = \pi d_3 N_3$$

$$\Rightarrow N_3 = N_2 \cdot \frac{d_2}{d_3} = N_2 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $N_2$ from Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$N_3 = \left( N_1 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} \right) \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_3}$$

$$N_3 = N_1 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_3}$$

**Step 4 — Train value (speed ratio):**

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_3} = \frac{T_3}{T_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For a simple gear train, *intermediate gear teeth cancel out*. This is why idler gears do not change the speed ratio but DO change the direction of rotation (useful in clock mechanisms, conveyors).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reverted Gear Train — Center Distance Constraint

**Setup:** A reverted compound gear train has two shafts (input $S_1$ and output $S_3$) on the *same axis*, with a compound intermediate gear pair on shaft $S_2$. Let the gears be: $A$ (on $S_1$), $B$ (on $S_2$, meshing with $A$), $C$ (on $S_2$, rigidly fixed to $B$), $D$ (on $S_3$, meshing with $C$).

**Step 1 — Train value (compound):**

$$i = \frac{N_A}{N_D} = \frac{T_B \cdot T_D}{T_A \cdot T_C}$$

**Step 2 — Center distance between $S_1$ and $S_2$:**

$$C_1 = \frac{d_A + d_B}{2} = \frac{m(T_A + T_B)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Center distance between $S_2$ and $S_3$:**

$$C_2 = \frac{d_C + d_D}{2} = \frac{m(T_C + T_D)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Reverted condition (shafts coaxial):**

$$C_1 = C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_A + T_B = T_C + T_D$$

**Step 5 — Combine with required velocity ratio to solve for tooth counts:**

Given $i$ and a chosen module $m$, pick one of the four unknowns freely, then solve the simultaneous equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_B \cdot T_D &= i \cdot T_A \cdot T_C \\
T_A + T_B &= T_C + T_D
\end{aligned}$$

This derivation is **critical for KTU automobile engineering problems** and appears regularly in Part B questions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Epicyclic Gear Train — Tabular Method Generalization
