| 4 | MSD System ODE | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | — | 2nd-order translational |
| 5 | Resistor | $e = R \cdot i$ | $R$ in $\Omega$ | Ohm's Law |
| 6 | Inductor | $e = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | $L$ in H | Faraday's Law |
| 7 | Capacitor | $i = C \cdot \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $C$ in F | Charge storage |
| 8 | Series RLC ODE | $L\ddot{q} + R\dot{q} + \tfrac{1}{C}q = e(t)$ | — | 2nd-order electrical |
| 9 | Transfer Function (MSD) | $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k}$ | — | Force input → Displacement output |
| 10 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ or $\sqrt{1/(LC)}$ | rad/s | Undamped oscillation |
| 11 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$ or $\dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{C/L}$ | dimensionless | $\zeta \lt 1$: underdamped |
| 12 | Time Constant (RL) | $\tau = L/R$ | s | First-order delay |
| 13 | Time Constant (RC) | $\tau = RC$ | s | First-order delay |
| 14 | Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil} = V_{control} / R_{coil}$ | A | Ohm's Law on coil |
| 15 | TRIAC Latch Current | $I_L$ (typically 50 – 100 mA) | mA | Min current to remain ON |
| 16 | SSR Power Dissipation | $P_d = I_{load} \times V_{drop}$ (typ. **1 – 1.5 V**) | W | Heat sink required |
| 17 | Galvanic Isolation | typically **2.5 kV RMS** | V | SSR opto-isolation rating |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $m$ and $k$ in the natural frequency formula. Always remember: **$\omega_n = \sqrt{\text{stiffness} / \text{inertia}}$** for mechanical systems. Inductance $L$ (inertia) maps to mass; inverse capacitance $1/C$ (stiffness) maps to spring constant $k$.

---

#### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **System Modelling** is the foundation of every modern control system — from the **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** in your car to the **gimbal stabilization** of a drone camera to the **inverted pendulum** control of a Segway. Without a mathematical model, designing a stable PID controller is impossible.
- **Mechanical Relays** dominate in **automotive applications** (horn, starter, fuel-pump relay), **PCB-mounted signal switching**, and **low-cost hobbyist projects**. Their robustness to voltage surges and ability to switch both AC and DC loads make them indispensable.
- **Solid-State Relays** are the *workhorse* of **industrial automation** — every PLC output module in a factory uses SSRs or their semiconductor equivalents (transistor/MOSFET outputs) to switch solenoid valves, contactors, motor starters, heaters, and lamps. Their silent operation, fast switching, and near-infinite lifespan are decisive advantages in high-cycle applications like **injection moulding**, **food packaging**, and **semiconductor fabrication**.

### 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

#### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Governing ODE of a Mass-Spring-Damper System

**System Setup:**
A mass $m$ slides on a horizontal frictionless guide. It is connected to a fixed wall on the right via a linear spring $k$ and a viscous damper $b$ (both in parallel). The mass is also subjected to an external horizontal force $F(t)$ to the right. The displacement of the mass from its static equilibrium position is $x(t)$ (positive to the right).

**Goal:** Derive the differential equation governing $x(t)$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram (FBD) of the Mass:**

List all forces acting on the mass along the horizontal axis (positive to the right):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= +F(t) \quad &\text{(external applied force, positive to the right)} \\
F_2 &= -k \cdot x(t) \quad &\text{(spring restoring force, opposes displacement)} \\
F_3 &= -b \cdot \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} \quad &\text{(damper resistive force, opposes velocity)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law:**

Sum of forces equals mass times acceleration:

$$\sum F = m \cdot a(t) = m \cdot \frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2}$$

Substitute:

$$F(t) - k \cdot x(t) - b \cdot \frac{dx(t)}{dt} = m \cdot \frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard Form:**

Move all terms to the LHS, ordered by descending derivative:

$$m \cdot \frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2} + b \cdot \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + k \cdot x(t) = F(t)$$

This is the canonical 2nd-order linear ODE of a translational mechanical system.

**Step 4 — Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions):**

Let $X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\}$ and $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{F(t)\}$. The transform rules are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dx}{dt}\right\} &= s X(s) - x(0) \stackrel{\text{ZIC}}{=} s X(s) \\
\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}\right\} &= s^2 X(s) - s x(0) - \dot{x}(0) \stackrel{\text{ZIC}}{=} s^2 X(s) \\
\mathcal{L}\{F(t)\} &= F(s) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying these:

$$m s^2 X(s) + b s X(s) + k X(s) = F(s)$$

$$X(s) \cdot (m s^2 + b s + k) = F(s)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Transfer Function:**

$$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{m s^2 + b s + k}}$$

**Step 6 — Identify Standard Second-Order Parameters:**

The denominator is in the form $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2$. Dividing numerator and denominator by $m$:

$$G(s) = \frac{1/m}{s^2 + \dfrac{b}{m} s + \dfrac{k}{m}}$$

Comparing with $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_n^2 &= \frac{k}{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \\
2 \zeta \omega_n &= \frac{b}{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

These two parameters completely characterize the *dynamic behaviour* of the system: $\omega_n$ sets the *speed of response*, and $\zeta$ sets the *shape of response* (overshoot, oscillation, settling time).

---

#### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series RLC Circuit ODE & Force-Voltage Analogy

**System Setup:**
A series RLC circuit consists of a resistor $R$, inductor $L$, and capacitor $C$ connected end-to-end, driven by a voltage source $e(t)$. The current in the loop is $i(t)$ and the charge on the capacitor is $q(t)$ where $i(t) = \dfrac{dq(t)}{dt}$.

**Goal:** Derive the ODE in $q(t)$ and show its structural equivalence to the MSD system.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**

Sum of voltage drops around the loop equals applied voltage:

$$e_R(t) + e_L(t) + e_C(t) = e(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Element Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_R &= R \cdot i(t) \\
e_L &= L \cdot \dfrac{di(t)}{dt} \\
e_C &= \dfrac{1}{C} \cdot q(t) \quad \text{(since } i = dq/dt \text{, } e_C = \frac{q}{C}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$R \cdot i(t) + L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \cdot q(t) = e(t)$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Charge $q(t)$:**

Since $i(t) = \dfrac{dq(t)}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{di(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 q(t)}{dt^2}$:

$$\boxed{L \cdot \frac{d^2 q(t)}{dt^2} + R \cdot \frac{dq(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \cdot q(t) = e(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the MSD Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mechanical (MSD):}\quad & m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t) \\
\text{Electrical (RLC):}\quad & L\ddot{q} + R\dot{q} + \tfrac{1}{C}q = e(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The structural analogy is exact under the **Force-Voltage Analogy**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &\;\longleftrightarrow\; L \quad &\text{(inertia ↔ inductance)} \\
b &\;\longleftrightarrow\; R \quad &\text{(friction ↔ resistance)} \\
k &\;\longleftrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{C} \quad &\text{(stiffness ↔ inverse capacitance)} \\
x &\;\longleftrightarrow\; q \quad &\text{(displacement ↔ charge)} \\
F &\;\longleftrightarrow\; e \quad &\text{(force ↔ voltage)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means: **any series RLC circuit can be mathematically modeled as an equivalent MSD system**, and vice versa. This powerful insight allows engineers to *simulate* mechanical systems using cheap electrical components (or vice versa) — the foundation of **analog computing** in the pre-digital era.

**Step 5 — Transfer Function:**

Apply Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$G(s) = \frac{Q(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{1}{L s^2 + R s + \frac{1}{C}}$$

Standard second-order parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_n &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \\
\zeta &= \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rotor (Rotational) Mechanical System

**System Setup:**
A rotating disc of moment of inertia $J$ is mounted on a shaft. The shaft has torsional stiffness $K$ and rotational viscous damping $B$. An external torque $T(t)$ is applied. Angular displacement is $\theta(t)$.

**Step 1 — Newton's Law for Rotation:**

$$\sum T = J \cdot \alpha = J \cdot \ddot{\theta}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify Torques:**

- Applied torque: $+T(t)$
- Spring (torsional) torque: $-K \cdot \theta(t)$
- Damper torque: $-B \cdot \dot{\theta}(t)$

**Step 3 — Form ODE:**

$$J \ddot{\theta}(t) + B \dot{\theta}(t) + K \theta(t) = T(t)$$

**Step 4 — Transfer Function:**

$$G(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{T(s)} = \frac{1}{J s^2 + B s + K}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Rotational ↔ Translational Mapping:**
> $m \leftrightarrow J$, $k \leftrightarrow K$, $b \leftrightarrow B$, $x \leftrightarrow \theta$, $F \leftrightarrow T$.

---

#### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EMR vs SSR

This is the most exam-relevant matrix for the relays portion:

| Parameter | Mechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) | Winner |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Switching Element** | Moving metallic contacts | Semiconductor (TRIAC, MOSFET, IGBT) | SSR (no wear) |
| **Switching Speed** | 5 – 15 ms | 0.05 – 1 ms | SSR (~100× faster) |
| **Lifetime (operations)** | $10^5 - 10^7$ | $>10^9$ | SSR |
| **Contact Bounce** | Present (0.5 – 2 ms) | None | SSR |
| **Operating Noise** | Audible *click* | Silent | SSR |
| **Galvanic Isolation** | Inherent (coil–contacts) | Opto-isolator (~2.5 kV) | Both adequate |
| **On-State Resistance** | <50 m$\Omega$ (near-zero) | 1 – 1.5 V drop | EMR (less heat) |
| **Off-State Leakage** | Zero (true open circuit) | 1 – 10 mA | EMR |
| **Surge Withstand** | High (10× rated current briefly) | Poor (semiconductor damage) | EMR |
| **Cost (low current)** | Low | Higher | EMR |
| **Cost (high cycle)** | Higher long-term | Lower long-term | SSR |
| **AC Switching** | Yes | Yes (with TRIAC) | Both |
| **DC Switching** | Yes | Yes (with MOSFET/IGBT) | Both |
| **Sensitive to Vibration** | Yes (contacts may bounce) | No | SSR |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Minimal | Significant (needs heat sink) | EMR |
| **Typical Applications** | Automotive, PCB, low-cycle | PLC outputs, industrial heaters, motor drives | — |

### 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

#### 4.1 Block Diagram — System Modelling Workflow

The diagram below depicts the standard KTU-recommended workflow for system modelling in mechatronics design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Physical System] --> B[Identify Domain: Mechanical / Electrical / Fluid / Thermal]
    B --> C[Identify Building Blocks: m k b or R L C]
    C --> D[Apply Governing Laws: Newton or KVL or KCL]
    D --> E[Form Differential Equation: nth Order ODE]
    E --> F[Take Laplace Transform: ZIC assumed]
    F --> G[Derive Transfer Function: G of s = Ys by Xs]
    G --> H{Analysis Type}
    H --> H1[Time Response via Inverse Laplace]
    H --> H2[Frequency Response via Bode Plot]
    H --> H3[Stability Analysis via Root Locus]
    H1 --> I[Validate Against Experiment]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Refine Model if Mismatch Greater than 5 Percent]
```

#### 4.2 Block Diagram — Analogous Systems Mapping (Force-Voltage Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MECH[Mechanical Translational MSD]
        M1[Mass m with input Force F] -->|f1| M2[Spring k in parallel]
        M1 -->|f2| M3[Damper b in parallel]
        M2 --> M4[Output displacement x of t]
        M3 --> M4
    end
    subgraph ELEC[Electrical Series RLC]
        E1[Voltage Source e of t] -->|e1| E2[Inductor L in series]
        E2 -->|e2| E3[Resistor R in series]
        E3 -->|e3| E4[Capacitor C in series]
        E4 --> E5[Output charge q of t]
    end
    M1 -.->|F maps to e| E1
    M2 -.->|k maps to 1 over C| E3
    M3 -.->|b maps to R| E2
    M4 -.->|x maps to q| E5
```

#### 4.3 Schematic — Mechanical (Electromechanical) Relay Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[Control Side Low Voltage Low Current]
        V1[DC Voltage Source V control] --> COIL[Coil N turns of copper wire]
        COIL --> CORE[Iron Core Magnetic Flux Phi]
    end
    CORE -->|magnetic attraction| ARM[Armature Pivoted Lever]
    SPRING[Return Spring] -->|restoring force| ARM
    ARM -->|mechanical motion| NO[Normally Open Contact NO]
    ARM -->|mechanical motion| NC[Normally Closed Contact NC]
    ARM -->|wiper| COM[Common Terminal COM]
    subgraph LOAD[Load Side High Voltage High Current]
        NO --> L1[Load Device: Heater / Motor / Lamp]
        L1 --> PSU[Load Power Supply]
        PSU --> COM
    end
    CTRL -.->|galvanic isolation via air gap| LOAD
```

#### 4.4 Schematic — Solid-State Relay Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP[Input Side 3 to 32 V DC]
        S1[Control Signal from PLC or MCU] --> RIN[Current Limiting Resistor R in]
        RIN --> LED[Infrared LED inside Opto Isolator]
    end
    LED -->|photons| OPTO[Phototriac or Phototransistor Opto Coupler]
    OPTO -->|isolated trigger| TRIG[Trigger Circuit Zero Cross Detector]
    TRIG -->|gate pulse| TRIAC[Power TRIAC AC load or MOSFET DC load]
    TRIAC --> OUT1[Output Terminal 1 to Load]
    subgraph LD[Load Side AC Mains or DC Bus]
        OUT1 --> LD1[Load: Heater or Solenoid or Motor]
        LD1 --> OUT2[Output Terminal 2]
    end
    OUT2 --> TRIAC
    HSINK[Aluminium Heat Sink thermal management] --> TRIAC
    INP -.->|galvanic isolation 2.5 kV| LD
```

#### 4.5 Operational State Diagram — Relay Switching Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> ON: Control voltage applied to coil or LED
    ON --> OFF: Control voltage removed
    ON --> ON: Holding current maintained
    OFF --> OFF: Dropout voltage below threshold
    note right of ON: EMR: armature pulled, contacts close\nSSR: TRIAC latches at next zero cross
    note left of OFF: EMR: spring returns armature\nSSR: TRIAC releases at zero current
```
