# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Servo Motor — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 DC Servo Motor Working Principle (Most Common in KTU Questions)

A **DC Servo Motor** is essentially a permanent-magnet DC motor optimized for **low inertia, high starting torque, and linear torque-speed characteristics**. Its armature current is controlled by a servo amplifier that receives an error signal from the position/velocity controller.

**Logical Steps of Operation:**

1. **Reference Input ($V_r$ or $\theta_r$)** is given — e.g., "move shaft to 45°."
2. **Feedback Sensor (Encoder/Resolver)** continuously reports the **actual position $\theta_a$**.
3. **Comparator (Summing Junction)** computes the **error** $e(t) = \theta_r - \theta_a$.
4. **Controller (P, PI, or PID)** processes the error into a control voltage.
5. **Power Amplifier (Servo Drive)** boosts the control signal to drive the armature.
6. **Motor Shaft** rotates, and the **mechanical load** is displaced.
7. As $\theta_a \to \theta_r$, the error $\to 0$, and the motor **holds position** against any disturbance torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Servo Holds Position Without External Locking:**
> The moment a disturbance tries to move the shaft, the encoder immediately detects the deviation from $\theta_r$, generating an error signal that drives the motor back to the commanded position. This is **active position holding** — vastly superior to a stepper's passive detent.

### 2.1.2 Transfer Function of an Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor

The **armature-controlled DC servo motor** is the most analytically tractable case and appears frequently in KTU derivations. The governing equations are:

**Electrical (Armature) Equation:**
$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

where the **back-EMF** is:
$$e_b(t) = K_b \cdot \omega_m(t)$$

**Mechanical (Torque) Equation:**
$$T_m(t) = J_m \frac{d\omega_m(t)}{dt} + B_m \omega_m(t) + T_L(t)$$

and motor torque:
$$T_m(t) = K_t \cdot i_a(t)$$

### 2.1.3 The Simplified Position Transfer Function (After Eliminating Variables)

In the **Laplace domain**, assuming zero initial conditions, zero load torque ($T_L = 0$), and using the assumption that $L_a$ is small (often neglected in first-order analysis for KTU), the **open-loop transfer function from armature voltage to angular position** is:

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This transfer function allows the designer to apply classical control theory (Bode plots, root locus, PID tuning) to predict and shape the servo system's response — critical for **CNC machines, robotic arms, and antenna positioning systems** where overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error are non-negotiable specifications.

---

## 2.2 Stepper Motor — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Construction & Stepping Action

A stepper motor's rotor and stator have **salient poles (teeth)**. When a stator phase is energized, the rotor aligns its teeth with that phase's teeth, creating a **minimum reluctance path**. Switching the energization sequence to the next phase causes the rotor to "step" to a new position.

**Types Covered in KTU Syllabus:**

1. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** — Rotor is a soft-iron gear-like piece (no permanent magnet). Rotation depends purely on the principle of *minimum reluctance*.
2. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** — Rotor is a permanent magnet. Has **detent torque** even without excitation.
3. **Hybrid Stepper Motor** — Combines VR (toothed structure) + PM (axially magnetized rotor). Has the **smallest step angle (0.72°–1.8°)** and highest precision.

### 2.2.2 Step Angle and Resolution

The fundamental step angle is determined by the number of rotor teeth ($N_r$) and the number of stator phases ($m$):

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

For a standard **1.8° hybrid stepper** with 200 steps/rev and 50 rotor teeth on a 2-phase motor: $m \cdot N_r = 200$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microstepping:** Modern drivers split each full step into 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, or even 1/256 microsteps by driving the two phases with **sinusoidally weighted currents**. This effectively multiplies the resolution — a 1.8° motor with 1/16 microstepping achieves a resolution of **0.1125° per microstep**.

### 2.2.3 Stepper Motor Torque-Speed Characteristics

Unlike a servo, a stepper motor's torque drops sharply as speed increases. The **pull-out torque curve** is the most important performance graph:

- At **low speeds** (0–100 RPM), the motor delivers **maximum holding torque**.
- As **speed increases**, the inductive reactance of the windings ($X_L = 2\pi f L$) increases, limiting the rate of current rise.
- Beyond a certain **slew rate**, the motor cannot deliver rated torque and eventually **loses synchronism**.

The **pull-out torque** as a function of stepping frequency $f$ is approximated by:

$$T_{po}(f) = \frac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_c)^2}}$$

where $f_c$ is the **cut-off frequency** related to the electrical time constant $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$ as $f_c = R_a / (2\pi L_a)$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes / Where Used** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step Angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ | degrees | $m$ = phases, $N_r$ = rotor teeth |
| Steps per Revolution | $N = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | dimensionless | Resolution check |
| Microstep Resolution | $\theta_{ms} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{k}$ | degrees | $k$ = microstep divisor (e.g., 16) |
| Holding Torque (Stepper) | $T_h = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | N·m | Energy-conversion method |
| Pull-out Torque vs. Frequency | $T_{po}(f) = \dfrac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_c)^2}}$ | N·m | High-speed operation analysis |
| Back-EMF (DC Servo) | $e_b = K_b \cdot \omega_m$ | Volts | $K_b$ in V·s/rad |
| Motor Torque Constant | $K_t = \dfrac{T_m}{i_a}$ | N·m/A | In SI: $K_t = K_b$ numerically |
| DC Servo Position TF (Neglecting $L_a$) | $\dfrac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s\left[R_a(J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t\right]}$ | rad/V | KTU board-favourite derivation |
| Steady-State Error (Unity Feedback) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ for step input | dimensionless | Position-loop analysis |
| Encoder Resolution | $\theta_{enc} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{ppr} \cdot 4}$ (with quadrature) | degrees | $N_{ppr}$ = pulses per revolution |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Table-Trap:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value or magnitude notation in tables — the raw pipe `\|` character breaks markdown table rendering. In LaTeX math blocks, `$\vert x \vert$` is preferred.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Applications — Real-World Utility

- **Servo Motors** dominate in **CNC machining centers** (where sub-micron tool positioning is mandatory), **industrial robotic arms** (where dynamic trajectory tracking is required), **autonomous vehicles** (steering, throttle-by-wire), and **telescope/antenna tracking systems**.
- **Stepper Motors** dominate in **desktop 3D printers** (open-loop simplicity, low cost), **textile and packaging machines** (where discrete indexing is needed), **disk drives** (legacy), **printers/scanners** (paper feed), and **medical infusion pumps** (precise low-speed positioning).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Position Transfer Function of Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor (Including $L_a$)

This is the **most important derivation** for the KTU 2024 Mechatronics Module 2 examination. We will derive the transfer function $\theta_m(s) / V_a(s)$ step-by-step, preserving the armature inductance $L_a$.

### Given
- Armature voltage $V_a$, resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, current $i_a$
- Back-EMF constant $K_b$ (V·s/rad), torque constant $K_t$ (N·m/A)
- Rotor inertia $J_m$, viscous friction $B_m$, angular velocity $\omega_m$, angular position $\theta_m$
- Load torque $T_L$ (assumed zero for this derivation)

### Step 1: Write the Armature Circuit Equation (Time Domain)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature circuit:

$$V_a(t) = R_a \cdot i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

The **back-EMF** is proportional to angular velocity:

$$e_b(t) = K_b \cdot \omega_m(t) = K_b \cdot \frac{d\theta_m(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Write the Mechanical (Torque) Equation

Newton's second law for rotation (sum of torques = inertia × angular acceleration + friction torque):

$$T_m(t) = J_m \frac{d^2\theta_m(t)}{dt^2} + B_m \frac{d\theta_m(t)}{dt} + T_L(t)$$

The **developed motor torque** is proportional to armature current:

$$T_m(t) = K_t \cdot i_a(t)$$

### Step 3: Transform All Equations to the Laplace Domain

Assuming **zero initial conditions** ($\theta_m(0) = 0$, $\omega_m(0) = 0$, $i_a(0) = 0$) and $T_L = 0$:

**Electrical Equation in s-domain:**

$$V_a(s) = R_a I_a(s) + L_a s \, I_a(s) + K_b \, s \, \theta_m(s)$$

Rearranging:

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \, I_a(s) + K_b s \, \theta_m(s)$$

**Mechanical Equation in s-domain:**

$$K_t \, I_a(s) = J_m s^2 \theta_m(s) + B_m s \, \theta_m(s)$$

Rearranging:

$$K_t \, I_a(s) = (J_m s^2 + B_m s) \, \theta_m(s) = s(J_m s + B_m) \, \theta_m(s)$$

### Step 4: Solve the Mechanical Equation for $I_a(s)$

$$I_a(s) = \frac{s(J_m s + B_m) \, \theta_m(s)}{K_t}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $I_a(s)$ into the Electrical Equation

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot \frac{s(J_m s + B_m) \, \theta_m(s)}{K_t} + K_b s \, \theta_m(s)$$

### Step 6: Factor Out $\theta_m(s)$ and $s$

$$V_a(s) = s \, \theta_m(s) \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

### Step 7: Common-Denominator Simplification

$$V_a(s) = s \, \theta_m(s) \cdot \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t}{K_t}$$

### Step 8: Solve for the Transfer Function $\theta_m(s) / V_a(s)$

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t \right]}$$

**Expanding the denominator** (useful for standard second-order form):

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ L_a J_m s^2 + (L_a B_m + R_a J_m) s + R_a B_m + K_b K_t \right]}$$

### Step 9: Special Case — Neglecting Armature Inductance ($L_a \approx 0$)

This simplification is often used in introductory KTU questions. With $L_a = 0$:

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ R_a (J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t \right]}$$

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / R_a}{s \left[ J_m s + B_m + (K_b K_t / R_a) \right]}$$

Defining the **motor gain** $K_m = K_t / (R_a B_m + K_b K_t)$ and **mechanical time constant** $\tau_m = R_a J_m / (R_a B_m + K_b K_t)$:

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The simplified form $\dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ is a **Type-1 second-order system**. Its open-loop Bode plot has a $-20$ dB/decade slope that becomes $-40$ dB/decade after the corner frequency $1/\tau_m$. This is the foundation of **PID tuning for servo systems**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Error of a Position Servo

Consider a **unity-feedback position servo** with forward-path transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

The **error** is $E(s) = \Theta_r(s) - \Theta_a(s) = \dfrac{\Theta_r(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.

**For a step input** $\theta_r(t) = A \cdot u(t)$:

$$\Theta_r(s) = \frac{A}{s}$$

Applying the **Final Value Theorem** (valid because the closed-loop is stable):

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot A/s}{1 + K_m / [s(\tau_m s + 1)]}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{A}{1 + K_m / [s(\tau_m s + 1)]}$$

As $s \to 0$, $\tau_m s + 1 \to 1$ and $K_m / s \to \infty$, hence:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{A}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A **Type-1 servo system** (one integrator in the forward path) has **zero steady-state error for a step input** — which is exactly why a well-tuned DC servo motor can hold its commanded position perfectly when the command is a constant angle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stepper Motor Step Angle (Number of Steps Calculation)

**Problem:** A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth. Calculate the step angle and the number of steps per revolution.

**Solution:**

The step angle formula:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

Substituting $m = 2$ (number of phases) and $N_r = 50$ (rotor teeth):

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

The number of steps per revolution:

$$N = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using $N = m \cdot N_r$:** $N = 2 \times 50 = 100$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison (Analytical View)

| **Performance Criterion** | **DC Servo Motor** | **Stepper Motor (Hybrid)** |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth (3 dB) | **High** (50–500 Hz typical) | **Low** (5–50 Hz typical) |
| Damping Ratio ($\zeta$) | Tunable via PID (typically 0.6–0.8) | Function of load + driver; often underdamped |
| Steady-State Error (Step Input) | **0** (Type-1 system) | **0** (open-loop, by discrete stepping) |
| Energy Efficiency at Standstill | Poor — idling current | Better — only holding current needed |
| Stall Behavior | Fights back with maximum torque | **Loses synchronism** silently |
| Encoder Required? | **Yes** (mandatory) | **No** (open-loop) |
| Cost per Axis | High | Low |
| Stepper-Specific: Resonance | Not a major issue | **Mid-frequency resonance** is a critical concern (must be avoided) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note on Stepper Resonance:** At certain stepping frequencies (typically 100–300 Hz for small motors), the rotor's mechanical natural frequency coincides with the stepping rate, causing **mid-band resonance** that can stall the motor. Solutions include **microstepping**, **anti-resonance drive algorithms**, and **mechanical damping**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — DC Servo Motor Position Control Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    refIn[Reference Input] --> sumA[Summing Junction]
    sumA --> ctrlA[PID Controller]
    ctrlA --> ampA[Power Amplifier]
    ampA --> motA[DC Servo Motor]
    motA --> loadA[Mechanical Load]
    loadA --> fbA[Position Feedback Encoder]
    fbA --> sumA
    motA --> tapA[Tachometer Optional]
    tapA --> fbA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The reference input (e.g., a target angle) enters the summing junction where the feedback from the encoder is subtracted. The resulting error is processed by the PID controller, amplified, and applied to the motor. The motor drives the load, and the encoder closes the loop. The tachometer is an *optional* inner velocity loop for higher-performance servos.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Stepper Motor Drive System

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Host Microcontroller PLC] --> nodeB[Step and Direction Pulse Generator]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stepper Driver IC Chopper Microstepping]
    nodeC --> nodeD[H Bridge Power Stage]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Stepper Motor Windings Phase A and Phase B]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Rotor and Shaft]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Mechanical Load Linear or Rotary]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Current Sensing Resistor]
    nodeH --> nodeC
```

**Description of the Functional Flow:**

1. The **host controller** (microcontroller or PLC) generates **step pulses** and a **direction signal**.
2. The **pulse generator** translates these into a precise switching sequence (full-step, half-step, or microstep).
3. The **driver IC** modulates the current using **PWM chopping** to maintain constant current through the windings despite changing back-EMF.
4. The **H-bridge power stage** drives the bipolar winding pairs (Phase A and Phase B).
5. The **rotor** moves in discrete angular increments, driving the **mechanical load**.
6. **Current sensing** provides feedback to the driver for chopper current regulation (this is *internal* current control, not position feedback — the system remains open-loop in position).

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Architectures

```mermaid
graph TB
    subA[Open Loop Stepper Architecture]
    refB[Command Pulses] --> drvB[Stepper Driver]
    drvB --> motB[Stepper Motor]
    motB --> loadB[Load]
    subB[Closed Loop Servo Architecture]
    refA[Reference Position] --> sumA2[Summing Junction]
    sumA2 --> ctrlA2[Controller]
    ctrlA2 --> ampA2[Servo Amplifier]
    ampA2 --> motA2[Servo Motor]
    motA2 --> loadA2[Load]
    loadA2 --> encA2[Encoder]
    encA2 --> sumA2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Takeaway:** The **only structural difference** between a stepper and a servo block diagram is the **presence of the feedback path from the load back to the summing junction** in the servo system. This single addition is what enables the servo's superior precision, stiffness, and dynamic response.

## 4.4 Step Sequence Timing Diagram — Full-Step Drive (2-Phase Stepper)

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1[Step 1: Phase A ON, Phase B OFF] --> p2[Step 2: Phase A OFF, Phase B ON]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Phase A ON Reverse, Phase B OFF]
    p3 --> p4[Step 4: Phase A OFF, Phase B ON Reverse]
    p4 --> p5[Return to Step 1]
```

**Description:** In **full-step single-phase-on (wave drive) mode**, only one phase is energized at a time, and the rotor advances by one step per pulse. In **full-step two-phase-on mode**, both phases are energized simultaneously, producing higher torque. **Half-stepping** alternates between these two modes, doubling the resolution.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with reference to stepper and servo motors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Open-Loop (Stepper) | Closed-Loop (Servo) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Not required | Position feedback (encoder) mandatory |
| Error Correction | None — relies on input pulse accuracy | Continuous — error is driven to zero by controller |
| Accuracy | Limited by step angle; degrades under load | High — limited by encoder resolution |
| Cost & Complexity | Low — only driver electronics | High — controller + amplifier + sensor |
| Loss of Synchronism | Possible (without detection) | Impossible (auto-corrected by feedback) |

**(3 marks)** — Awarded for: 1 mark for definition of each system (1×2 = 2), 1 mark for the differential comparison with at least one valid technical point.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define "holding torque" and "detent torque" of a stepper motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** The maximum **static torque** that a stepper motor can exert *while energized* at its rated current and *while stationary*, without causing continuous rotation. It is the **highest torque** the motor can deliver at **zero speed**. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Detent Torque ($T_d$):** The small **residual torque** present in a permanent-magnet (PM) or hybrid stepper motor *even when the stator windings are de-energized*. It arises from the attraction between the rotor's permanent magnet and the stator's salient poles. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Derive the **transfer function of an armature-controlled DC servo motor** relating the output angular position $\theta_m(s)$ to the applied armature voltage $V_a(s)$. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-phase hybrid stepper motor with **50 rotor teeth**, calculate:
   (i) The step angle in degrees. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The number of steps per revolution. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The shaft displacement after **320 pulses** in full-step mode and in **1/8 microstepping** mode. **(3 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Transfer Function Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions** (state explicitly — 1 mark):
- Load torque $T_L = 0$
- Zero initial conditions: $\theta_m(0) = 0$, $\omega_m(0) = 0$, $i_a(0) = 0$
- Constant field flux (permanent-magnet motor)
- Linear magnetic circuit ($K_t$ and $K_b$ are constants)
- All energy stored in armature inductance and rotor inertia

**Armature circuit equation:**

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

with $e_b(t) = K_b \omega_m(t) = K_b \dfrac{d\theta_m(t)}{dt}$

**Mechanical equation:**

$$T_m(t) = J_m \frac{d^2\theta_m(t)}{dt^2} + B_m \frac{d\theta_m(t)}{dt}$$

with $T_m(t) = K_t i_a(t)$

**Laplace transformation (with zero IC):**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b s \, \theta_m(s)$$

$$K_t I_a(s) = (J_m s^2 + B_m s) \, \theta_m(s)$$

From the second equation:

$$I_a(s) = \frac{s(J_m s + B_m) \, \theta_m(s)}{K_t}$$

Substituting into the first equation:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{s(J_m s + B_m) \, \theta_m(s)}{K_t} + K_b s \, \theta_m(s)$$

Factoring:

$$V_a(s) = s \, \theta_m(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J_m s + B_m)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$V_a(s) = \frac{s \, \theta_m(s) \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t \right]}{K_t}$$

**Final transfer function** (boxed result — 1 mark for the final answer):

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t \right]}$$

**(7 marks breakdown):** 1 mark for assumptions, 2 marks for time-domain equations, 2 marks for Laplace transform, 1 mark for substitution and algebraic manipulation, 1 mark for the final boxed result.

---

**Part (b) — Stepper Motor Calculations (7 marks):**

Given: 2-phase ($m = 2$), 50 rotor teeth ($N_r = 50$).

**(i) Step Angle (2 marks):**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

**(ii) Steps per Revolution (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**(iii) Shaft Displacement (3 marks):**

**Full-step mode:** Each pulse = 3.6°.

$$\theta_{full} = 320 \times 3.6° = 1152°$$

Reducing mod 360°: $1152° = 3 \times 360° + 72°$ → **net displacement = 72°** in the original direction (3 full revolutions + 72°). **(1.5 marks)**

**1/8 Microstepping mode:** Each microstep = $3.6° / 8 = 0.45°$.

$$\theta_{micro} = 320 \times 0.45° = 144°$$

Net displacement: $144°$ (less than one revolution, no wrap-around). **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a hybrid stepper motor** with a neat block diagram. Compare it with the **variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC servo motor has the following parameters: $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 0.5 \, \text{H}$, $K_t = 0.05 \, \text{N·m/A}$, $K_b = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$, $J_m = 0.01 \, \text{kg·m}^2$, $B_m = 0.001 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$. Determine:
   (i) The motor's **mechanical time constant** $\tau_m$ and **gain** $K_m$ (neglecting $L_a$). **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The **steady-state error** for a unit step input in a unity-feedback position loop. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) Briefly explain how **PID control** improves the steady-state and transient response. **(2 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Hybrid Stepper Motor (7 marks):**

**Construction** (2.5 marks):
A hybrid stepper motor combines the features of both **PM** and **VR** stepper motors. It has:
- A **two-phase stator** with salient poles (typically 8 poles, each with 5 teeth) wound with concentrated coils.
- A **two-section rotor**, axially magnetized — one half is a North-pole magnet, the other is a South-pole magnet. Each rotor section has 50 fine teeth.
- The teeth on the two rotor halves are **mechanically offset by half a tooth pitch** to produce a smooth torque curve.

**Working Principle** (2.5 marks):
When Phase A is energized, the rotor section near Phase A aligns its teeth to minimize reluctance. Due to the half-pitch offset on the second rotor section, simultaneous energization of Phase B (90° electrically shifted) causes a smooth handoff. By sequencing the phase currents, the rotor rotates in **small discrete steps (typically 1.8° or 0.72°)** with **very high detent torque and holding torque**.

**Comparison with VR Stepper** (2 marks):

| Feature | Hybrid | VR |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor | Axially magnetized, toothed | Plain soft iron, toothed (no magnet) |
| Detent Torque | Yes (significant) | No (zero without excitation) |
| Step Angle | Very small (0.72°–1.8°) | Larger (typically 7.5°–15°) |
| Holding Torque | High | Moderate (depends on excitation only) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

---

**Part (b) — DC Servo Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 0.5 \, \text{H}$, $K_t = 0.05 \, \text{N·m/A}$, $K_b = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$, $J_m = 0.01 \, \text{kg·m}^2$, $B_m = 0.001 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$.

**(i) Mechanical Time Constant and Gain (neglecting $L_a$) — (3 marks):**

From the simplified transfer function:

$$\tau_m = \frac{R_a J_m}{R_a B_m + K_b K_t} = \frac{2 \times 0.01}{2 \times 0.001 + 0.05 \times 0.05}$$

$$\tau_m = \frac{0.02}{0.002 + 0.0025} = \frac{0.02}{0.0045} \approx 4.44 \text{ s}$$

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B_m + K_b K_t} = \frac{0.05}{0.0045} \approx 11.11 \text{ rad/(V·s)}$$

**(ii) Steady-State Error for Unit Step — (2 marks):**

The system is **Type-1** (one integrator in the open-loop path). For a **Type-1 unity-feedback system**, the steady-state error for a step input is:

$$e_{ss} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is independent of $K_m$** — *any* Type-1 system tracks a step input with zero error in steady state. This is a **fundamental control theory result** that KTU examiners love to test.

**(iii) PID Control — (2 marks):**

A **PID controller** has the form $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$:
- The **Proportional term** ($K_p$) increases the **loop gain**, reducing rise time and improving transient response.
- The **Integral term** ($K_i$) **eliminates steady-state error** (already zero for step, but crucial for ramp inputs).
- The **Derivative term** ($K_d$) provides **phase lead**, increasing damping and reducing overshoot.

The combined effect: **faster response, no overshoot, and zero steady-state error for a wide class of inputs**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $K_b K_t$ term** in the denominator of the DC servo transfer function — this is the **electromechanical coupling** and its omission loses 2–3 marks. The transfer function is NOT just $K_t / (s R_a J_m)$ — students frequently make this mistake.
> 2. **Confusing the units of $K_b$ and $K_t$** — in SI, $K_b$ is in V·s/rad and $K_t$ is in N·m/A, and they are **numerically equal** for an ideal motor: $K_t (\text{N·m/A}) = K_b (\text{V·s/rad})$.
> 3. **Forgetting the "mod 360°" reduction** in stepper motor problems — if a problem asks for shaft displacement, the answer should be reduced to within a single revolution unless otherwise stated.
> 4. **Stating "stepper motors have feedback"** — this is **wrong**. The stepper is intrinsically an open-loop device. Modern *closed-loop steppers* (with encoders) exist as a hybrid product, but the **classical stepper is open-loop**.
> 5. **Confusing step angle with resolution** — step angle is the angular increment of *one* step; resolution is the smallest *controllable* increment (improved by microstepping).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ **Servo Motor** = **closed-loop** rotary/linear actuator with built-in position feedback. Holds commanded position against disturbance.
- ✅ **Stepper Motor** = **open-loop** brushless actuator that converts electrical pulses into discrete angular steps. No feedback sensor required.
- ✅ The DC servo **transfer function** is $\dfrac{K_t}{s[(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_b K_t]}$ — *memorize the form*, not the expansion.
- ✅ The **mechanical time constant** $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J_m}{R_a B_m + K_b K_t}$ and **motor gain** $K_m = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a B_m + K_b K_t}$.
- ✅ The **step angle formula** is $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m N_r}$ where $m$ = number of phases and $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth.
- ✅ A **Type-1 servo system** has **zero steady-state error for a step input** — a high-yield KTU fact.
- ✅ **Holding torque** is the maximum static torque with the motor energized at standstill. **Detent torque** is the residual torque of PM/hybrid steppers *without* excitation.
- ✅ **Microstepping** multiplies the effective resolution by a factor $k$ (e.g., 1/16, 1/32).
- ✅ **Pull-out torque** of a stepper drops with speed: $T_{po}(f) = T_h / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}$.
- ✅ Servo motors dominate in **high-speed, high-precision** applications (CNC, robotics); stepper motors dominate in **low-cost, open-loop, moderate-speed** applications (3D printers, packaging).
- ✅ The **encoder resolution** with quadrature decoding is $\theta_{enc} = 360° / (4 \times N_{ppr})$.
- ✅ **Mid-frequency resonance** is a unique problem of stepper motors — solved by microstepping and anti-resonance algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ON/OFF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ON/OFF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-Off Controller (Two-Position / Bang-Bang Controller):** A discontinuous feedback control element whose corrective output assumes only **two discrete states** — fully **ON (U_H)** or fully **OFF (U_L)** — depending on whether the instantaneous error signal $e(t)$ lies above or below a defined reference threshold (the **set-point / desired value**).

It is the mathematically simplest, structurally cheapest, and historically oldest form of closed-loop control. The KTU 2024 syllabus (PCBRT601 – Module 3) categorizes it under *Process Controllers* and uses it as the **baseline reference** against which Proportional (P), Integral (I), Derivative (D), and full PID controllers are compared.

**The two fundamental forms are:**
1. **Ideal On-Off Controller (Zero Dead-Band)** — output flips the instant the error crosses zero.
2. **Practical On-Off Controller (with Differential Gap / Hysteresis)** — output changes state only after the error exceeds a pre-defined band of width $\pm \delta$, preventing infinite high-frequency chattering.

**Key Nomenclature used throughout this note:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $e(t)$ | Error signal: set-point minus measured variable | Depends on process |
| $u(t)$ | Controller output | Volts / % / Amps |
| $U_H$ | High output (ON state) | V or % |
| $U_L$ | Low output (OFF state) | V or % |
| $\delta$ | Half-width of dead-band | Same as $e(t)$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Home Thermostat

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy (Thermal Comfort System):**  
> Imagine a domestic geyser in a Kerala hostel room. The user sets a desired water temperature of **45 °C**.
> - When the water falls **below 45 °C**, the thermostat's bimetallic strip triggers the relay → heater is switched **fully ON** at 230 V AC.
> - When water crosses **45 °C**, the relay trips → heater is switched **fully OFF** (0 V).
> - In practice, the thermostat does not flip at exactly 45 °C; it switches ON at 43 °C and OFF at 47 °C, giving a **2 °C dead band** (hysteresis) to prevent rapid relay wear.

The output of the controller is *binary* — it is never at, say, 40 % or 70 %. It is either **0 %** or **100 %** of the actuator capacity. This is the defining characteristic of a two-position control system.

## 1.3 Where On-Off Control is Used (Industry Examples)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Industry-Connection Note:** During board exams, adding 1–2 real applications earns extra valuation credit.
> - Refrigerator compressor cycling
> - Domestic geyser / iron / oven thermostat
> - Water-pump starter in overhead tanks (level switch)
> - Pressure cut-out switch in air compressors
> - CNC machine's safety E-stop (Emergency-Stop relay)
> - Liquid-level control in a sump using float switches
> - Over-current / over-voltage protection relays
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Steps

The controller decision is performed every sampling instant (or continuously for analog implementations). The decision tree is:

1. **Measure** the process variable (PV): $y(t)$ — e.g., actual temperature, level, pressure.
2. **Compare** with the set-point (SP / $r(t)$).
3. **Compute the error:** 
$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$
4. **Apply the decision rule**:
   - If $e(t) > 0$ (PV is *below* SP) → output = $U_H$ (actuator fully energized).
   - If $e(t) < 0$ (PV is *above* SP) → output = $U_L$ (actuator de-energized).
5. **Actuator drives** the plant in the corrective direction.
6. **Loop repeats** until PV reaches set-point region.

## 2.2 Mathematical Models

### A. Ideal (Zero Dead-Band) On-Off Controller
$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_H, & e(t) > 0 \\ U_L, & e(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$
This version *never* holds a steady state; the relay chatters at theoretically infinite frequency around $e = 0$, which is **physically unrealizable**.

### B. Practical On-Off Controller with Differential Gap (Hysteresis)
$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_H, & e(t) > +\delta \\ U_L, & e(t) < -\delta \\ u(t^-), & \vert e(t) \vert \le \delta \end{cases}$$

The width of the dead band is:
$$DB = 2\delta$$

The **hysteresis loop area** (energy lost per cycle) is:
$$A_{hys} = 2\delta \cdot (U_H - U_L)$$

## 2.3 Performance Metrics for an On-Off Controller

| Metric | Definition | Typical Expression |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-state error band** | Maximum continuous deviation from SP | $\vert e \vert_{max} = \delta$ |
| **Oscillation period $T_o$** | Time between two consecutive ON-switches | Depends on plant time constant |
| **Duty cycle $D$** | Fraction of time output is ON | $D = \dfrac{t_{ON}}{t_{ON} + t_{OFF}}$ |
| **Chatter rate** | Relay switching frequency | $f_c = 1 / T_o$ |

## 2.4 The "Why" of the Dead Band — Engineering Necessity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without hysteresis, an ideal On-Off controller commands the actuator to switch **infinitely fast** as the PV hovers near the set-point. Real mechanical relays, solenoid valves, and contactors have **finite mechanical life** (typically $10^5$ to $10^7$ operations). A 1-second chatter would destroy the relay in a few days. Hence the dead band is an **engineering compromise** to extend actuator life at the cost of steady-state accuracy.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **minimum-must-know** set for KTU ESE / Module Test 3. Memorize every entry.

| Sl. | Concept | Governing Equation | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Can be positive or negative |
| 2 | Ideal ON-OFF law | $u(t) = U_H \cdot \text{sgn}(e)$ | $\text{sgn}(0)$ undefined |
| 3 | Practical ON-OFF law | $u(t) = U_H$ if $e > \delta$, $U_L$ if $e < -\delta$ | Inactive when $\vert e \vert \le \delta$ |
| 4 | Dead band | $DB = 2\delta$ | Engineering choice |
| 5 | Hysteresis loop area | $A_{hys} = 2\delta (U_H - U_L)$ | Energy dissipated / cycle |
| 6 | Maximum PV overshoot | $PV_{max} = SP + \delta$ | Single-side |
| 7 | Maximum PV undershoot | $PV_{min} = SP - \delta$ | Single-side |
| 8 | Steady-state error bound | $\vert e_{ss} \vert \le \delta$ | Always |
| 9 | Effective plant input average | $\bar{u} = D \cdot U_H + (1-D) \cdot U_L$ | $0 \le D \le 1$ |
| 10 | Switching frequency | $f_c = 1 / T_o$ | $T_o$ = oscillation period |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

On-Off control dominates **mass-produced domestic and industrial switching applications** where:
- The process variable is intrinsically oscillatory in nature (room temperature, water level).
- The plant is slow (large thermal / mechanical inertia), so chatter is naturally damped.
- The cost of the controller (typically a single IC or relay) is the dominant design driver.
- The system is non-critical (e.g., domestic geyser — a 2 °C band is acceptable).

Modern implementations use **Schmitt-trigger electronic comparators** (e.g., LM393, TL331) to realize the hysteresis electronically, completely replacing the electromechanical relay in low-power applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Control Law of the On-Off Controller

The two-position control law is a *signum-function* mapping from the error space to the output space.

**Step 1 — Start from the basic error definition**
$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define the signum function**
$$\text{sgn}(e) = \begin{cases} +1, & e > 0 \\ \;\;\, 0, & e = 0 \\ -1, & e < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Express the output in terms of signum and an offset**
$$u(t) = \frac{U_H + U_L}{2} + \frac{U_H - U_L}{2} \cdot \text{sgn}(e)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by defining the DC bias $U_0$ and amplitude $A$**
$$U_0 = \frac{U_H + U_L}{2}, \quad A = \frac{U_H - U_L}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back**
$$u(t) = U_0 + A \cdot \text{sgn}(e)$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form**
$$\boxed{u(t) = U_0 + A \cdot \text{sgn}\bigl(r(t) - y(t)\bigr)}$$

This is the **canonical ideal On-Off control law**. When the dead band is added, the signum function is replaced by a **Schmitt-trigger characteristic function** $f_{ST}(e)$, giving:
$$u(t) = U_0 + A \cdot f_{ST}\bigl(e(t), \delta\bigr)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Oscillation Period of a First-Order Plant under On-Off Control

Consider a first-order plant with transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

When driven by the On-Off output $u(t)$, its response $y(t)$ to a step of magnitude $U_H$ is:
$$y(t) = y(0) + K U_H \bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

Suppose the PV starts at $y(0) = SP - \delta$ (the ON threshold). Output switches to $U_H$. PV rises exponentially toward $K U_H$.

**Time to reach upper threshold $y = SP + \delta$:**
$$SP + \delta = (SP - \delta) + K U_H \bigl(1 - e^{-t_1/\tau}\bigr)$$

**Rearrange:**
$$2\delta = K U_H \bigl(1 - e^{-t_1/\tau}\bigr)$$

**Solve for $e^{-t_1/\tau}$:**
$$e^{-t_1/\tau} = 1 - \frac{2\delta}{K U_H}$$

**Take natural log of both sides:**
$$-\frac{t_1}{\tau} = \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{2\delta}{K U_H}\right)$$

**Rise time (ON-time, $t_{ON} = t_1$):**
$$\boxed{t_{ON} = -\tau \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{2\delta}{K U_H}\right)}$$

By **symmetry** (assuming the OFF state is $U_L = 0$ and we also account for a possible cooling/heating time constant $\tau_c$):
$$\boxed{t_{OFF} = -\tau_c \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{2\delta}{K (SP - 0)}\right) \approx \tau_c \ln\!\left(\frac{SP}{SP - 2\delta}\right)}$$

**Total oscillation period:**
$$\boxed{T_o = t_{ON} + t_{OFF}}$$

**Switching frequency:**
$$f_c = \frac{1}{T_o}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from the Derivations:** Increasing $\delta$ lengthens $T_o$ → reduces relay wear, but **increases** the steady-state error band. The designer trades off **actuator lifetime** against **control accuracy**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Power Delivered (Duty Cycle)

For a process running with periodic On-Off cycling, the time-averaged heat / energy delivered to the process is:
$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{T_o} \int_0^{T_o} u(t) \cdot y_{eq} \, dt = \frac{t_{ON} \cdot U_H + t_{OFF} \cdot U_L}{T_o}$$

**Assuming $U_L = 0$ for simplicity (heater fully shut off):**
$$\bar{P} = U_H \cdot \frac{t_{ON}}{T_o} = U_H \cdot D$$

where the **duty cycle** is:
$$\boxed{D = \frac{t_{ON}}{t_{ON} + t_{OFF}} = \frac{t_{ON}}{T_o}}$$

This is the principle behind **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) electronic control** — a refined, fixed-frequency descendant of the On-Off controller.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: On-Off vs Proportional vs PID

| Property | On-Off (Two-Position) | Proportional (P) | Full PID |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output levels | 2 (ON / OFF) | Continuous | Continuous |
| Steady-state error | Bounded by $\delta$ | Non-zero ($e_{ss} = r / (1+K_p K)$) | Zero (theoretically) |
| Stability | Stable but oscillatory | Tunable | Tunable |
| Cost | Lowest (1 comparator/relay) | Medium (op-amp / µC) | Highest (µC / PLC) |
| Tuning parameters | Dead-band $\delta$ | $K_p$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ |
| Actuator wear | High without dead band | Moderate | Low |
| Application complexity | Trivial | Moderate | High |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Stability Analysis of On-Off Loop (Describing Function)

The On-Off controller is a **non-linear** element. Linear stability methods fail. The **Describing Function (DF)** method of Kochenburger approximates the non-linearity by its first Fourier harmonic.

For a symmetric On-Off element with output levels $\pm A$ and dead band $\pm \delta$, the describing function is:
$$N(A_s) = \frac{4A}{\pi A_s} \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\delta}{A_s}\right)^2}, \quad A_s > \delta$$
where $A_s$ is the amplitude of the sinusoidal error input. For zero dead band ($\delta = 0$):
$$N(A_s) = \frac{4A}{\pi A_s}$$

The closed-loop system will have a **limit cycle** (sustained oscillation) when:
$$\boxed{1 + N(A_s) \cdot G(j\omega) = 0}$$

This equation is solved graphically on the **Nyquist plot** to predict the amplitude $A_s$ and frequency $\omega_o$ of the limit cycle. This is a frequent KTU Part-B (14-mark) question.

> [!NOTE]
> The complete graphical procedure: (1) Plot $-1/N(A_s)$ locus. (2) Plot $G(j\omega)$ locus. (3) Intersection = limit cycle point. (4) Read $A_s$ and $\omega_o$ from the intersection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an On-Off Control Loop

The Mermaid block diagram below maps the **complete signal flow** of a closed-loop two-position control system (e.g., thermal control of a geyser), as typically expected in KTU descriptive questions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    spA[Set Point r t] --> compA[Comparator Summing Junction]
    pvA[Process Variable y t] --> compA
    compA --> errSig[Error Signal e t equals r minus y]
    errSig --> ctrlA[On Off Controller with Dead Band delta]
    ctrlA --> actA[Actuator Relay or Heater]
    actA --> pltA[Plant Geyser Tank]
    pltA --> sensA[Sensor Thermocouple or RTD]
    sensA --> pvA
    ctrlA -.-> fbPath[Feedback Path]
    pltA -.-> fbPath
    classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2d8a3e,color:#000
    class spA,pvA src
    class compA,errSig,actA,pltA,sensA,fbPath proc
    class ctrlA ctrl
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic of the On-Off Controller (Schmitt-Trigger Equivalent)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA([Start of Each Sampling Cycle]) --> readPV[Read Process Variable y t from Sensor]
    readPV --> calcErr[Compute Error e t equals r t minus y t]
    calcErr --> checkHigh{Is e t greater than plus delta}
    checkHigh -- YES --> outH[Set Output u t equals U_H ON]
    checkHigh -- NO --> checkLow{Is e t less than minus delta}
    checkLow -- YES --> outL[Set Output u t equals U_L OFF]
    checkLow -- NO --> holdOut[Hold Previous Output u t equals u of t minus]
    outH --> drive[Drive Actuator]
    outL --> drive
    holdOut --> drive
    drive --> waitA[Wait for Sampling Period T_s]
    waitA --> startA
    classDef decision fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef startEnd fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    class checkHigh,checkLow decision
    class outH,outL,holdOut,drive,waitA,readPV,calcErr action
    class startA startEnd
```

## 4.3 Hysteresis Transfer Characteristic (Schmitt-Trigger Plot)

The plot below illustrates the **input–output mapping** of a practical On-Off controller with dead band. Note the rectangular hysteresis loop — output is **bistable** with a memory element.

```mermaid
graph LR
    q1[Quadrant I: Error increasing past plus delta] -->|output jumps to U_H| q2[Output at U_H state]
    q3[Quadrant II: Error decreasing past minus delta] -->|output jumps to U_L| q4[Output at U_L state]
    q2 -->|error in range minus delta to plus delta| q5[Output retained at last value]
    q4 -->|error in range minus delta to plus delta| q5
    classDef high fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef hold fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    class q1,q2 high
    class q3,q4 low
    class q5 hold
```

## 4.4 Typical Response Curve of PV vs Time under On-Off Control

```mermaid
graph LR
    tAxis[Time axis t] --> p1
    p1[PV rises exponentially from SP minus delta] --> p2[PV crosses SP]
    p2 --> p3[Output switches OFF at SP plus delta]
    p3 --> p4[PV decays exponentially]
    p4 --> p5[PV crosses SP again]
    p5 --> p6[Output switches ON at SP minus delta]
    p6 --> tAxis
    classDef phase fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    class p1,p2,p3,p4,p5,p6 phase
```
> **Reading aid for students:** The PV curve is a *continuous sawtooth-like* waveform oscillating symmetrically about the set-point. The horizontal distance between two consecutive peaks is $T_o$ (the oscillation period). The vertical distance peak-to-trough is $2\delta$ (the dead band).

## 4.5 Block Diagram Annotation Table

| Block | Realization in Physical Plant |
|---|---|
| Set Point Source | Operator knob, keypad, or digital preset in HMI |
| Comparator | Op-amp summing junction / digital subtraction in µC |
| On-Off Controller | Electromechanical relay, Schmitt trigger, or PLC ladder rung |
| Actuator | Heating coil, solenoid valve, contactor, motor starter |
| Plant | Thermal tank, water reservoir, pneumatic line |
| Sensor | RTD, thermocouple, float switch, pressure transducer |
| Feedback Path | Electrical wires from sensor to comparator input |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
> **Define an On-Off controller. List any two of its practical applications.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
An On-Off controller, also called a two-position or bang-bang controller, is a feedback controller whose output can assume only **two discrete values**, namely $U_H$ (ON) and $U_L$ (OFF), depending on whether the process variable is below or above the set-point. The transition between the two states occurs at the set-point, or within a defined differential gap / dead band of width $2\delta$. The controller law is $u(t) = U_H$ if $e > \delta$ and $u(t) = U_L$ if $e < -\delta$.

**Practical applications:**
1. **Domestic geyser / refrigerator thermostat** — switches heating/cooling compressor ON or OFF based on measured temperature.
2. **Overhead water tank level controller** — float switch drives the pump starter contactor to turn the motor ON or OFF.
3. *(Optional 3rd)* Pressure cut-out switch in a reciprocating air compressor.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
> **What is a 'dead band' in an On-Off controller? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**
The **dead band** (also called differential gap or hysteresis band) is a small zone of width $2\delta$ around the set-point within which the controller output is held constant at its previous value and is not allowed to switch. Mathematically, $\vert e(t) \vert \le \delta$ ⇒ $u(t) = u(t^-)$.

**It is necessary because:**
1. It **prevents excessive relay chattering** as the process variable oscillates near the set-point, which would otherwise cause **rapid mechanical wear** and premature failure of the actuator.
2. It allows the PV to enter and remain within a finite, predictable error band, giving the system a **bounded steady-state error** of $\pm \delta$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 / Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q. Module 3]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> (a) **Derive the mathematical model of an On-Off controller with dead band. Draw its input-output characteristic.** *(7 marks)*
> (b) **A thermal process is controlled by an On-Off controller with $U_H = 10$ V, $U_L = 0$ V, set-point $r = 100$ °C, and dead band $2\delta = 4$ °C. The plant is first-order with $K = 5$ °C/V and time constant $\tau = 60$ s. Calculate (i) the upper and lower switch points, (ii) the ON-time, and (iii) the steady-state PV error band.** *(7 marks)*

### Solution to Q3(a) — Model Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the error signal:**
$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

**Step 2 — State the two-position control law with dead band:**
$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_H, & e(t) > +\delta \\ U_L, & e(t) < -\delta \\ u(t^-), & \vert e(t) \vert \le \delta \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Express using the Schmitt-trigger function $f_{ST}$:**
$$u(t) = U_0 + A \cdot f_{ST}(e, \delta)$$
where $U_0 = (U_H + U_L)/2$ and $A = (U_H - U_L)/2$.

**Step 4 — Hysteresis loop characteristics:**
- The output stays at $U_H$ for $e > +\delta$.
- Switches to $U_L$ only when $e$ falls below $-\delta$.
- Returns to $U_H$ only when $e$ rises above $+\delta$.
- This produces a **rectangular hysteresis loop** of area $A_{hys} = 2\delta (U_H - U_L)$.

**Step 5 — Input-output characteristic plot:** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.) The plot is a rectangular loop with the upper branch at $U_H$ and the lower branch at $U_L$, separated horizontally by a width of $2\delta$.

**Step 6 — Mention the practical implementation:** Realized using a comparator with positive feedback (Schmitt trigger, e.g., LM393) or as a single rung of a PLC ladder program.

### Solution to Q3(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
$U_H = 10$ V, $U_L = 0$ V, $r = 100$ °C, $2\delta = 4$ °C, $K = 5$ °C/V, $\tau = 60$ s.

**(i) Upper and lower switch points:**
$$\text{Lower switch} = r - \delta = 100 - 2 = \mathbf{98 \text{ °C}}$$
$$\text{Upper switch} = r + \delta = 100 + 2 = \mathbf{102 \text{ °C}}$$

**(ii) ON-time calculation:**

Using the derived formula:
$$t_{ON} = -\tau \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{2\delta}{K U_H}\right)$$

Substitute the values:
$$t_{ON} = -60 \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{4}{5 \times 10}\right) = -60 \ln(1 - 0.08)$$

$$t_{ON} = -60 \ln(0.92) = -60 \times (-0.0834) = \mathbf{5.0 \text{ s}}$$

Wait — checking units: $2\delta / (K U_H) = 4 / 50 = 0.08$ (dimensionless, °C cancels). Correct.

**Refined calculation:** $t_{ON} = -60 \times (-0.08338) = 5.003 \approx \mathbf{5.0 \text{ s}}$.

**(iii) Steady-state PV error band:**
The PV oscillates between $98$ °C and $102$ °C, hence:
$$\vert e_{ss} \vert \le \delta = \pm \mathbf{2 \text{ °C}}$$

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> (a) **Explain the working of an On-Off controller with the help of a block diagram. Compare it with a Proportional controller.** *(7 marks)*
> (b) **For a first-order thermal plant with $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{20s + 1}$ driven by an On-Off controller ($U_H = 12$ V, $U_L = 0$ V, $\delta = 1$ °C, $r = 50$ °C), find the period of oscillation $T_o$ and the switching frequency $f_c$.** *(7 marks)*

### Solution to Q3B(a) — Comparison (7 marks)

**Block diagram working:** (See Mermaid Section 4.1.) The set-point is compared with the PV; the error is fed to the On-Off block whose output is either $U_H$ or $U_L$; this drives the actuator and hence the plant; the sensor closes the loop.

**Comparison table:**

| Aspect | On-Off | Proportional (P) |
|---|---|---|
| Output | 2 levels ($U_H, U_L$) | Continuous, $u = K_p e$ |
| Steady-state error | Bounded by $\delta$ | Non-zero, $= r/(1+K_p K)$ |
| Tuning | Dead band $\delta$ | Gain $K_p$ |
| Oscillation | Continuous | Settles to band |
| Actuator wear | High without $\delta$ | Low |
| Cost | Very low | Low–medium |
| Application | Thermostat, level switch | Process industries |

### Solution to Q3B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $G(s) = 2/(20s + 1)$, so $K = 2$ °C/V, $\tau = 20$ s. $U_H = 12$ V, $U_L = 0$ V, $\delta = 1$ °C, $r = 50$ °C.

**Step 1 — ON-time:**
$$t_{ON} = -\tau \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{2\delta}{K U_H}\right) = -20 \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{2}{24}\right)$$
$$= -20 \ln(1 - 0.0833) = -20 \ln(0.9167) = -20 \times (-0.0870) = \mathbf{1.74 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 2 — OFF-time:** Since $U_L = 0$, the plant cools. With negligible cooling, the decay is approximately symmetric; using the simplified symmetric form:
$$t_{OFF} \approx t_{ON} = \mathbf{1.74 \text{ s}}$$

(For an exact answer with a cooling constant $\tau_c$, apply $t_{OFF} = -\tau_c \ln(1 - 2\delta/(K \cdot r))$.)

**Step 3 — Oscillation period:**
$$T_o = t_{ON} + t_{OFF} = 1.74 + 1.74 = \mathbf{3.48 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 4 — Switching frequency:**
$$f_c = \frac{1}{T_o} = \frac{1}{3.48} = \mathbf{0.287 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $2\delta$ vs $\delta$ confusion** — Dead band is the *total* width $2\delta$, but the controller switches at $\pm\delta$. Writing $2\delta$ on the wrong side costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Not stating the holding condition** — When $\vert e \vert \le \delta$, students often forget to specify $u(t) = u(t^-)$ (memory). This loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign of the error** — Many students write $e = y - r$ instead of $e = r - y$. This inverts the action (direct-acting vs reverse-acting). Examiners specifically look for the correct convention.
> 4. **Skipping the describing function / limit cycle analysis** — KTU frequently asks the *stability* aspect. A derivation missing the $1 + N(A_s) G(j\omega) = 0$ condition loses up to 4 marks in Part B.
> 5. **Not drawing the hysteresis loop** — A free-hand rectangular loop in the answer booklet is worth 2 easy marks. Always include it.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert units** — When $K$ is in °C/V and $\delta$ in °C, the argument of $\ln$ must be dimensionless. Show the cancellation explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Points — Read Twice Before the Exam**

- ⭐ The On-Off controller is the **simplest, cheapest, and most widely deployed** feedback controller in the world.
- ⭐ Output has **only two levels**: $U_H$ (ON) and $U_L$ (OFF). Never anything in between.
- ⭐ The decision rule is based on the **error sign**: $e = r - y$.
- ⭐ The **dead band / differential gap** $2\delta$ is essential in practice to prevent relay chatter.
- ⭐ When $\vert e \vert \le \delta$, the output **holds its previous value** (memory property).
- ⭐ The **hysteresis loop** on the input–output plane is rectangular, with area $A_{hys} = 2\delta (U_H - U_L)$.
- ⭐ The PV under On-Off control **always oscillates** between $SP - \delta$ and $SP + \delta$.
- ⭐ Steady-state error is **bounded** by $\pm \delta$, never zero.
- ⭐ **Rise time** of a first-order plant step response: $t_{ON} = -\tau \ln\bigl(1 - 2\delta / (K U_H)\bigr)$.
- ⭐ **Oscillation period** $T_o = t_{ON} + t_{OFF}$ → switching frequency $f_c = 1/T_o$.
- ⭐ **Stability analysis** uses the **Describing Function** method (limit cycle from $1 + N G = 0$).
- ⭐ The On-Off controller is the conceptual ancestor of **PWM** (Pulse-Width Modulation).
- ⭐ Realizations: (i) Electromechanical relay + bimetallic strip, (ii) Op-amp Schmitt trigger (LM393), (iii) PLC ladder rung, (iv) Microcontroller `if-else` block.
- ⭐ Applications: thermostats, level switches, pressure cut-outs, refrigerator compressors, geysers, water-pump starters.
- ⭐ **Advantages:** simplicity, cost, reliability, no tuning required beyond $\delta$.
- ⭐ **Disadvantages:** continuous oscillation, actuator wear, inability to achieve tight control, energy loss in hysteresis loop.
- ⭐ **Comparison mantra for exam:** "ON-OFF = 2-state, bang-bang, relay, hysteresis, limit cycle."

> **End of Module 3 — ON/OFF Topic Notes**  
> *(Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PCBRT601 Mechatronics)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PI and PID Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PI and PID Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **PI and PID Controllers** are the **workhorses of industrial automation**, responsible for over **95% of all closed-loop control loops** in process industries worldwide.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Proportional-Integral (PI) Controller** is a feedback control mechanism whose output is a linear combination of the *proportional* of the instantaneous error and the *integral* of the past error over time. In the **Laplace (s) domain**, its transfer function is given by:

$$C_{PI}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$$

A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Controller** extends the PI by adding a *derivative* term that anticipates the *future* trend of the error, based on its current rate of change. Its transfer function is:

$$C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \cdot s$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = **Proportional Gain** (dimensionless or with system units)
- $K_i$ = **Integral Gain** (units of $1/\text{time}$)
- $K_d$ = **Derivative Gain** (units of $\text{time}$)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain exactly 60 km/h on a hilly road (this is your *setpoint*):**

| Controller Term | Driving Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional (P)** | The harder you press the accelerator, the further you are from 60 km/h. Proportional to **current** speed gap. | Reacts to **present error** $e(t)$ |
| **Integral (I)** | You notice you have been driving at 58 km/h for a long time, so you press the pedal a little harder to *eliminate* the persistent lag. | Accumulates **past errors** $\int e(t)\,dt$ |
| **Derivative (D)** | You see a steep hill coming, so you preemptively press the brake/accelerator based on how *fast* the slope changes. | Anticipates **future error** $\frac{de(t)}{dt}$ |

- A **PI Controller** is like a driver who corrects *current* speed and remembers *past* slowdowns — but is surprised by sudden hills.
- A **PID Controller** is like a smart driver who also *anticipates* terrain changes based on the rate of error change.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Form (Parallel vs. Standard / ISA Form)

Engineers often express the controller in a **dimensionless, normalized form** known as the **Standard Form** or **ISA Form**:

$$C(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} + T_d \, s \right)$$

Where:
- $T_i = \frac{K_p}{K_i}$ is the **Integral Time / Reset Time** (minutes/seconds)
- $T_d = \frac{K_d}{K_p}$ is the **Derivative Time** (minutes/seconds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** When the question states "**parallel form**" — use $K_p, K_i, K_d$ directly. When it states "**standard / series / ISA form**" — use $K_p, T_i, T_d$. Mixing them up is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Structure of the PI Controller

The PI controller adds a **pole at the origin** ($s = 0$) and a **zero** on the negative real axis. The transfer function can be rewritten in pole-zero form:

$$C_{PI}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p \, s + K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p \left( s + \frac{K_i}{K_p} \right)}{s}$$

This means the PI controller introduces:
- A **pole at the origin** → guarantees **Type-1 system** → **zero steady-state error to a step input**
- A **zero at $s = -\frac{K_i}{K_p} = -\frac{1}{T_i}$** → shapes the transient response

### 2.2 Mathematical Structure of the PID Controller (Ideal Form)

The ideal (parallel) PID controller:

$$C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

Factored numerator analysis:
- **Two zeros** on the negative real axis (stable zeros if $K_p, K_i, K_d > 0$)
- **One pole at the origin** → **Type-1 system** → eliminates steady-state error

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a pole at the origin?** The integrator $\frac{1}{s}$ creates *infinite DC gain* ($C(0) = \infty$). Any constant error produces a continuously growing control signal until the error vanishes — this is the *mathematical reason* PI/PID eliminate steady-state offset.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Effect of Each Term

| Term | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase $K_p$** | Decreases ↑ | Increases ↓ | Small change | Decreases (but not zero) | Degrades |
| **Increase $K_i$** | Decreases | Increases | Increases | **Eliminated → 0** | Degrades (oscillates) |
| **Increase $K_d$** | Minor | **Decreases** | **Decreases** | No effect | **Improves** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PI Controllers**: Temperature control in furnaces, level control in tanks, flow control in pipelines.
- **PID Controllers**: DC motor speed/position, robotic arm joint control, drone flight controllers, CNC machine tools, HVAC systems, autonomous vehicle steering.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{PI}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$ | PI Controller (Parallel Form) | V/V (gain) |
| 2 | $C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID Controller (Parallel Form) | V/V (gain) |
| 3 | $C(s) = K_p\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | Standard / ISA Form | V/V (gain) |
| 4 | $T_i = \frac{K_p}{K_i}$ | Integral Time (Reset Time) | seconds |
| 5 | $T_d = \frac{K_d}{K_p}$ | Derivative Time | seconds |
| 6 | $K_u, P_u$ (Z-N) | Ultimate Gain & Ultimate Period (Ziegler-Nichols) | —, s |
| 7 | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$ (P) | Z-N Tuning (P-only) | — |
| 8 | $K_p = 0.45 K_u, T_i = \frac{P_u}{1.2}$ (PI) | Z-N Tuning (PI) | —, s |
| 9 | $K_p = 0.6 K_u, T_i = 0.5 P_u, T_d = 0.125 P_u$ (PID) | Z-N Tuning (PID) | —, s, s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** When writing $\vert K_p \vert$ or $\vert e(t) \vert$ in tables/derivations, **always use the LaTeX vertical bar `\vert`** (not the pipe `|`) to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Time-Domain Equation to Transfer Function

**Starting Point — Time Domain Output of a PID Controller:**

The control signal $u(t)$ in time domain is the linear combination of the three terms:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \, \frac{d e(t)}{d t}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace Transform term by term.**

Using the standard Laplace transform pairs:
- $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \frac{E(s)}{s}$
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = s E(s) - e(0)$ (assuming zero initial conditions, $e(0) = 0$)

**Step 2 — Substitute the transformed terms back into the time-domain equation.**

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + K_i \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_d \cdot s \, E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the common term $E(s)$ (this is the linearity property of the Laplace transform).**

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s \right) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Define the transfer function as the ratio $\frac{U(s)}{E(s)}$.**

$$C_{PID}(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s$$

This is the **Parallel Form of the PID Controller** — the canonical engineering form.

**Step 5 — Convert to standard (ISA) form by factoring out $K_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{PID}(s) &= K_p \left( 1 + \frac{K_i}{K_p \cdot s} + \frac{K_d \, s}{K_p} \right) \\
&= K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{\left(\frac{K_p}{K_i}\right) s} + \left(\frac{K_d}{K_p}\right) s \right) \\
&= K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} + T_d \, s \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Express the PID as a rational polynomial in $s$ to identify poles and zeros.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{PID}(s) &= \frac{K_p \, s}{s} + \frac{K_i}{s} + \frac{K_d \, s^2}{s} \\
&= \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A **second-order numerator** (giving 2 zeros) over a **first-order denominator** (giving 1 pole at the origin). The pole at the origin is what gives the controller its infinite DC gain, eliminating steady-state error.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: PI Controller Steady-State Error Elimination

**Starting Point — Unity Feedback System with a First-Order Plant.**

Consider a plant $G(s) = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}$ controlled by a PI controller. The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{C_{PI}(s) \, G(s)}{1 + C_{PI}(s) \, G(s)}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the PI transfer function and the plant.**

$$C_{PI}(s) \, G(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{(K_p s + K_i)}{s (\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the error transfer function $E(s)/R(s) = \frac{1}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} &= \frac{1}{1 + \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s(\tau s + 1)}} \\
&= \frac{s(\tau s + 1)}{s(\tau s + 1) + K_p s + K_i} \\
&= \frac{\tau s^2 + s}{\tau s^2 + s(1 + K_p) + K_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem to find steady-state error for a unit step input $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$.**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{\tau s^2 + s}{\tau s^2 + s(1 + K_p) + K_i} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify and evaluate the limit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{\tau s^2 + s}{\tau s^2 + s(1 + K_p) + K_i} \\
&= \frac{\tau (0)^2 + 0}{\tau (0)^2 + 0 + K_i} \\
&= \frac{0}{K_i} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Because $K_i \neq 0$, the steady-state error to a unit step is **exactly zero**. This is the *primary engineering justification* for using a PI controller instead of a pure P controller.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The $K_i/s$ term keeps "accumulating" corrective action as long as *any* error exists. It will not stop until error = 0. The integrator acts like a "memory" of all past errors.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each Term Affects Closed-Loop Poles

For a second-order plant $G(s) = \frac{\omega_n^2}{s(s + 2\zeta \omega_n)}$ with a PID controller:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Closed-Loop Char. Eq.:} \quad & s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 + C(s) \cdot G(s) = 0 \\
\text{With PID:} \quad & s^3 + 2\zeta\omega_n s^2 + \omega_n^2(1 + K_d) s + K_p \omega_n^2 s + K_i \omega_n^2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

| Term Added | Characteristic Equation Change | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **+ $K_p$** | $+ K_p \omega_n^2 s$ | Adds damping; **shifts poles left** |
| **+ $K_i$** | $+ K_i \omega_n^2$ | Adds a new pole; **Type-1 system** |
| **+ $K_d$** | $+ K_d \omega_n^2 s$ | Increases effective damping; **reduces overshoot** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: PID Controller in a Closed-Loop System

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete signal flow of a PID controller in a unity-feedback closed-loop control system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R s] --> SUM1{"Sum Junction +"}
    SUM1 --> EC[Error E s]
    EC --> P[Proportional Kp]
    EC --> I[Integral Ki over s]
    EC --> D[Derivative Kd times s]
    P --> SUM2{"Sum Junction +"}
    I --> SUM2
    D --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> U[Control Signal U s]
    U --> PL[Plant G s]
    PL --> Y[Output Y s]
    Y --> FB[Feedback Path H s equals 1]
    FB --> SUM1
    classDef controller fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signal fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class P,I,D controller
    class PL plant
    class R,Y,U,EC,FB signal
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Parallel vs. Standard Form Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E[Error E s] --> A
    subgraph SF [STANDARD ISA FORM]
        A[Kp] --> B[Multiplier]
        A1[1 divided by Ti times s] --> B
        A2[Td times s] --> B
    end
    B --> C[Control Signal U s]
    
    E2[Error E s] --> AA
    subgraph PF [PARALLEL FORM]
        AA[Kp] --> BB[Sum]
        AA1[Ki divided by s] --> BB
        AA2[Kd times s] --> BB
    end
    BB --> CC[Control Signal U s]
    
    classDef stdform fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef parform fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px
    class SF stdform
    class PF parform
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow: How Each Term Contributes to Output Over Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TI [TRANSIENT BEHAVIOR]
        T1[At t equals 0] --> T2[Error is Large]
        T2 --> T3[P term provides Strong Initial Push]
        T3 --> T4[I term builds up Slowly]
        T4 --> T5[D term suppresses Sudden Changes]
        T5 --> T6[Output Settles at Setpoint]
    end
    
    subgraph SS [STEADY STATE]
        S1[Error equals 0] --> S2[P term equals 0]
        S2 --> S3[I term holds the Load]
        S3 --> S4[D term equals 0]
    end
    
    TI --> SS
    
    classDef transient fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef steady fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px
    class TI transient
    class SS steady
```

### 4.4 Pole-Zero Map of Ideal PID Controller (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PZ [POLE ZERO MAP of C PID s]
        P1[POLE at Origin s equals 0]:::pole
        Z1[ZERO Z1 on Negative Real Axis]:::zero
        Z2[ZERO Z2 on Negative Real Axis]:::zero
    end
    
    classDef pole fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px
    classDef zero fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Map:** The "X" at the origin represents the pole (integrator), and the two "O" marks are the zeros contributed by the proportional and derivative actions. The zeros *attract* root locus branches, shaping the transient response.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a PI controller. List its main advantages over a pure P controller.*

**Model Answer:**
A **Proportional-Integral (PI) Controller** is a feedback controller whose output is the sum of a term proportional to the instantaneous error and a term proportional to the integral of the error over time. Its transfer function is:

$$C_{PI}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$$

**Advantages over P controller:**
1. **Eliminates steady-state error** to step inputs (because of the integrator's infinite DC gain).
2. Provides **zero offset** in regulatory processes (level, temperature, flow).
3. Slightly improves **transient response** by introducing a zero in the root locus.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the derivative term in a PID controller? Why is it not used alone?*

**Model Answer:**
The **derivative term** $K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ produces a control action proportional to the *rate of change* of error. Its role is to **anticipate future error** and apply corrective damping *before* the error grows large, thereby reducing overshoot and improving stability.

**Why it cannot be used alone:**
1. A pure derivative controller $C(s) = K_d s$ has **zero gain at DC** ($C(0) = 0$). It cannot eliminate steady-state error.
2. It amplifies **high-frequency noise** (since $\vert j\omega \vert$ grows with frequency), making the system susceptible to sensor noise.
3. It produces **no control action for constant errors** — if error is constant, derivative is zero.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a parallel-form PID controller from its time-domain equation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A unity-feedback system has a plant $G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)}$. A PI controller with $K_p = 1$ and $K_i = 5$ is used. Determine:
  (i) The closed-loop transfer function
  (ii) The steady-state error for a unit step input
  (iii) The type of the system **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Starting from the time-domain control law:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Applying the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s E(s)$$

Factoring $E(s)$:

$$U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)$$

Therefore, the transfer function is:

$$C_{PID}(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

This can be rewritten as:

$$C_{PID}(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**(i) Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

The open-loop transfer function is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(s) &= C_{PI}(s) G(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+2)} \\
&= \left(\frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+2)} \\
&= \frac{10(K_p s + K_i)}{s^2 (s+2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $K_p = 1, K_i = 5$:

$$L(s) = \frac{10(s + 5)}{s^2 (s+2)}$$

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{10(s+5)}{s^2(s+2) + 10(s+5)}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$s^3 + 2s^2 + 10s + 50$$

So:

$$T(s) = \frac{10(s+5)}{s^3 + 2s^2 + 10s + 50}$$

**(ii) Steady-State Error for Unit Step:**

The error transfer function is:

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{s^2(s+2)}{s^2(s+2) + 10(s+5)}$$

Using the Final Value Theorem with $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{s^2(s+2)}{s^2(s+2) + 10(s+5)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \\
&= \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s^2(s+2)}{s^2(s+2) + 10(s+5)} \\
&= \frac{0}{0 + 50} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**$e_{ss} = 0$** (steady-state error is eliminated).

**(iii) Type of the System:**

Counting the number of pure integrators (poles at origin) in the open-loop transfer function $L(s) = \frac{10(s+5)}{s^2(s+2)}$:

There are **2 poles at the origin** → **Type-2 system**.

A Type-2 system has zero steady-state error for step and ramp inputs, and finite error for parabolic inputs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Alternate]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop PID control system. State the function of each term ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) and their effects on the time response. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The Ziegler-Nichols ultimate cycle method gives $K_u = 8$ and $P_u = 60$ s for a process. Calculate the PID controller settings using Z-N tuning rules. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

*(Block Diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the diagram.)*

**Working of Closed-Loop PID Control:**

1. The **reference input** $R(s)$ (desired value, e.g., 60 °C) is compared with the **measured output** $Y(s)$ (sensor feedback, e.g., 58 °C).
2. The **error** $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ is fed to the PID controller.
3. The PID controller computes three parallel actions:
   - **Proportional:** $u_P(t) = K_p e(t)$ → reacts to *present* error
   - **Integral:** $u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$ → accumulates *past* errors
   - **Derivative:** $u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ → predicts *future* error trend
4. The total control signal $u(t) = u_P + u_I + u_D$ drives the **actuator/plant**.
5. The plant output $Y(s)$ is sensed and fed back, closing the loop.

**Effects of Each Term on Time Response:**

| Term | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p \uparrow$ | Decreases | Increases | Slight increase | Decreases (not zero) |
| $K_i \uparrow$ | Decreases | Increases | Increases | **Eliminates** |
| $K_d \uparrow$ | Minor | **Decreases** | **Decreases** | No effect |

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $K_u = 8$ (ultimate gain), $P_u = 60$ s (ultimate period).

**Z-N PID Tuning Formulas:**

$$
K_p = 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8
$$

$$
T_i = 0.5 \, P_u = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_d = 0.125 \, P_u = 0.125 \times 60 = 7.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Converting to parallel form** (if required by the question):

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{30} = 0.16$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 4.8 \times 7.5 = 36$$

**Final PID Settings (Parallel Form):**

$$\boxed{K_p = 4.8, \quad K_i = 0.16 \text{ s}^{-1}, \quad K_d = 36 \text{ s}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Form Confusion:** Mixing up $K_p, T_i, T_d$ (standard form) with $K_p, K_i, K_d$ (parallel form) is the **#1 mistake** in PID problems. Always re-read the question stem.
> 2. **Steady-State Error Calculation:** Students often forget the **Final Value Theorem** requires checking system stability first (poles in LHP). The integrand check is implicit but expected.
> 3. **Z-N Table Memorization:** Forgetting whether to use 0.45 or 0.6 for $K_p$ in PI vs PID is a common 2-mark loss. **Memory aid:** P-only uses 0.5, PI uses 0.45, PID uses 0.6.
> 4. **Units of $K_i, K_d$:** Always write units in final answers. $K_i$ has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$, $K_d$ has units of s.
> 5. **Derivative Noise:** In real systems, pure derivative is replaced by $K_d \cdot \frac{s}{\tau_f s + 1}$ (filtered derivative). Mention this in design questions for *bonus marks*.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PI Controller:** $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$ → eliminates steady-state error, adds a zero and a pole at origin.
- [x] **PID Controller:** $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ → adds derivative action, improves damping, anticipates error.
- [x] **Standard (ISA) Form:** $K_p(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s)$ where $T_i = K_p/K_i$ and $T_d = K_d/K_p$.
- [x] **P-action:** Reduces rise time, decreases (but does not eliminate) steady-state error.
- [x] **I-action:** Eliminates steady-state error; can cause oscillations and integrator windup.
- [x] **D-action:** Reduces overshoot and settling time; amplifies noise; cannot be used alone.
- [x] **Z-N Ultimate Cycle (PID):** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
- [x] **Z-N Ultimate Cycle (PI):** $K_p = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = P_u/1.2$, $T_d = 0$.
- [x] **Type-N System:** Number of pure integrators (poles at origin) in the open-loop transfer function.
- [x] **PI → Type-1 System → Zero SSE to step input.**
- [x] **Final Value Theorem for SSE:** $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$, valid only if all closed-loop poles are in LHP.
- [x] **Derivative Filter (Real PID):** $\frac{K_d s}{1 + \tau_f s}$ to avoid amplifying sensor noise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Controller** is an electronic control system that uses a digital computer, microprocessor, microcontroller, or Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) as its central processing element to execute control algorithms on discretized (sampled) input signals and generate discrete control commands for actuating physical processes. Unlike analog controllers that process continuous-time signals using op-amps, resistors, and capacitors, digital controllers process **quantized, time-sampled** data streams.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus (PCBRT601, Module 3)**, digital controllers are studied as the computational "brain" of a mechatronic system, interfacing the digital decision-making domain with the analog physical world through **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converters)** and **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converters)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
