## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). List any four advantages of PLC over conventional relay-based control systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a digitally operating, solid-state, industrially hardened computer that uses a programmable memory to store user-defined instructions for implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic, in order to control industrial machines and processes in real time.

**Advantages of PLC over Relay Logic (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**

1. **Easy to modify** — Program changes replace rewiring of relays.
2. **High reliability** — Solid-state, no moving parts in the logic; MTBF > 100,000 hours.
3. **Compact size** — One PLC replaces hundreds of relays and timers.
4. **Built-in diagnostics** — Self-checking and I/O status indicators simplify troubleshooting.
5. **Lower long-term cost** — Less maintenance, faster changeover.
6. **Communication capability** — Easy integration with SCADA, HMI, and other PLCs via networks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a robot. How is it calculated using Gruebler's equation for a planar mechanism?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is the *number of independent motions* (rotations or translations) the manipulator can perform in 3D space. It represents the dimension of the robot's task space.

> **Gruebler's Equation for Planar Mechanisms (2 marks):**
>
> $$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
>
> Where:
> - $M$ = mobility / number of DOF of the mechanism
> - $n$ = total number of links (including the fixed ground link)
> - $j_1$ = number of **1-DOF (lower) pairs** (e.g., revolute, prismatic joints)
> - $j_2$ = number of **2-DOF (higher) pairs** (e.g., cam-follower contact)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, usually with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**  *(Choose A or B)*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a PLC. Describe the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the working of a PLC using the scan cycle concept. A PLC takes 2 ms for input scan, 8 ms for program scan, 2 ms for output scan, and 2 ms for housekeeping. Calculate the scan time and the maximum frequency of input signal that the PLC can detect. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) PLC Architecture (7 marks):**

A PLC consists of **five major functional blocks**:

| # | Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power Supply** | Converts 230 V AC mains to +5 V DC (logic) and +24 V DC (sensor supply). Provides isolation and surge protection. | 1 |
| 2 | **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** | Contains the microprocessor, ALU, registers, and timing circuits. Executes the operating system and the user program. | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Memory (ROM, RAM, EEPROM)** | ROM stores the OS; RAM stores the user program (battery-backed); EEPROM/Flash stores the final program permanently. | 1.5 |
| 4 | **Input/Output (I/O) Modules** | Interface between the CPU and field devices. Input modules accept signals from sensors/buttons; output modules drive contactors/valves/lights. | 2 |
| 5 | **Programming Device** | PC or handheld console used to write, edit, upload, and download the ladder logic program. Communicates via RS-232/485, USB, or Ethernet. | 1 |

**Block diagram (1 mark — textual representation acceptable in exam):**

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │              PROGRAMMING DEVICE (PC)                │
   └───────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                           │ (Communication port)
   ┌──────────────┐   ┌────▼─────┐   ┌─────────────────┐
   │   INPUT      │   │   CPU    │   │     OUTPUT       │
   │   MODULE     ◄───►  + ALU   ◄───►    MODULE        │
   │              │   │          │   │                  │
   └──────┬───────┘   └────┬─────┘   └────────┬─────────┘
          │                │                   │
   ┌──────▼───────┐   ┌────▼─────┐   ┌────────▼─────────┐
   │  ROM/EPROM   │   │  RAM     │   │    EEPROM/Flash   │
   │  (OS)        │   │ (User    │   │   (Final user     │
   │              │   │  program)│   │    program)       │
   └──────────────┘   └──────────┘   └───────────────────┘
                           ▲
                  ┌────────┴────────┐
                  │  POWER SUPPLY   │
                  │  230V AC→5V/24V │
                  └─────────────────┘
```

**(b) PLC Scan Cycle and Calculations (7 marks):**

**Working — Scan Cycle Concept (3 marks):**

The PLC operates in an **endless, cyclic manner** called the *scan cycle*. Each cycle consists of **four sequential phases**:

1. **Input Scan** — All physical input devices are read and their ON/OFF states are stored in the **Input Image Table (IIT)** in memory.
2. **Program Scan** — The CPU executes the user ladder program **rung by rung (top-to-bottom, left-to-right)**, using values from the IIT (not live inputs).
3. **Output Scan** — The computed results are written into the **Output Image Table (OIT)** and then transferred to the actual physical output terminals.
4. **Housekeeping** — Internal diagnostics, communication with the programming device, and timer/counter updates are handled.

**Calculation — Scan Time (2 marks):**

$$T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$T_{scan} = 2 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} = 14 \text{ ms}$$

**Calculation — Maximum Detectable Frequency (2 marks):**

By the Nyquist sampling criterion, the input must be sampled at least twice per cycle to be reliably detected:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.014} = \frac{1}{0.028} \approx 35.71 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A):**
> 1. If you write the scan cycle as "inputs → outputs → program," the order is **wrong** and you'll lose 1 mark.
> 2. The Nyquist formula is $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2T}$, not $\dfrac{1}{T}$. Using the wrong formula gives **~70 Hz** and you lose 1 mark.
> 3. Always **show units** (ms, Hz). Unitless answers are penalized.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**  *(Choose A or B)*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) Define an industrial robot as per the ISO/RIA standard. With neat sketches, explain any FOUR types of robot configurations based on coordinate system. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define (i) payload, (ii) reach, (iii) repeatability, and (iv) accuracy. A cylindrical robot has a vertical travel of 1.5 m and the horizontal arm can extend from 0.4 m to 1.8 m, rotating through 240°. Calculate the workspace volume. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Industrial Robot Definition and Types (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
As per **ISO 8373:2021 / RIA**, an **Industrial Robot** is *"an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

The three key qualifier words: **(i) automatically controlled, (ii) reprogrammable, (iii) multipurpose** — without any of these, the definition is incomplete.

**Robot Configurations (6 marks — 1.5 marks each, four types):**

**1. Cartesian Robot (PPP)**
- Three **prismatic (linear sliding)** joints, mutually perpendicular.
- Workspace = **rectangular cuboid**.
- Sketch:
```
   Z↑
   |   ┌──────────────┐
   |   │              │
   |   │    WORK      │──→ Y
   |   │   ENVELOPE   │
   |   └──────────────┘
   └──────────────────→ X
```
- Applications: CNC, 3D printing, pick-and-place, palletizing.

**2. Cylindrical Robot (RPP)**
- One **revolute** joint (base rotation about vertical axis) + two **prismatic** joints (radial arm and vertical column).
- Workspace = **hollow cylindrical sector**.
- Applications: Machine tending, assembly, coating.

**3. Spherical (Polar) Robot (RRP)**
- Two **revolute** joints + one **prismatic** joint.
- Workspace = **partial sphere** (bounded by two concentric spheres and a vertical angle).
- Applications: Welding, painting, material handling.

**4. Articulated (Revolute) Robot (RRR)**
- All three primary joints are **revolute**, mimicking a human arm (waist–shoulder–elbow).
- Workspace = **sphere-like**, large but with a dead zone at the base.
- Applications: Welding, painting, complex assembly — **most popular industrial robot type**.

**(b) Specifications and Workspace Volume (7 marks):**

**Definitions (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**

- **(i) Payload:** Maximum weight (in kg) the robot can carry at the wrist, including the end-effector, while maintaining rated performance.
- **(ii) Reach:** Maximum distance (in m) from the center of the robot's base to the center point of the end-effector (or wrist) when the arm is fully extended.
- **(iii) Repeatability:** The ability of the robot to return to a *previously taught* position consistently, expressed as ± deviation (in mm). Determined by mechanical errors and quantified as the **3-sigma deviation**.
- **(iv) Accuracy:** The ability of the robot to reach a *mathematically computed* position based on its kinematic model. Determined by calibration errors and is generally **less than** repeatability.

**Workspace Volume Calculation (5 marks):**

Given:
- Vertical travel: $h = 1.5$ m
- Horizontal arm extension: from $r_{min} = 0.4$ m to $r_{max} = 1.8$ m
- Angular rotation: $\theta = 240°$

**Step 1:** Convert angle to radians

$$\theta = 240° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \text{ rad} \approx 4.1888 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2:** Write the formula for volume of a cylindrical sector

$$V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \theta \cdot \left( R^2 - r^2 \right) \cdot h$$

**Step 3:** Compute the squared terms

$$R^2 = 1.8^2 = 3.24 \text{ m}^2$$
$$r^2 = 0.4^2 = 0.16 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R^2 - r^2 = 3.24 - 0.16 = 3.08 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and solve

$$V = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.1888 \times 3.08 \times 1.5$$

$$V = 0.5 \times 4.1888 \times 4.62$$

$$V = 0.5 \times 19.352$$

$$V \approx 9.68 \text{ m}^3$$

**Final Result:** Workspace volume $\approx 9.68 \text{ m}^3$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** is the #1 error. If you use 240 in the formula, you get a wrong answer and lose 2 marks.
> 2. Many students forget to square the radii first and end up with linear units (m) instead of volume (m³). The examiner will specifically check for **m³** in the unit.
> 3. In the definition, "Repeatability is ±0.05 mm" is acceptable; "Repeatability is 0.05 mm" (without ±) is **incomplete** and loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. Listing the *three key qualifier words* (automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose) in the ISO definition is **worth 1 mark** by itself. Forgetting them costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Additional Concept-Trigger Questions for Quick Practice

**Q3. (Conceptual — 2 marks)**
*What is the difference between PLC and PC?*

**Quick Answer:**
- A **PLC** is designed for *real-time, harsh industrial environments*, uses ladder logic, and has deterministic scan-based execution.
- A **PC** is a *general-purpose office computer* with a non-real-time OS (Windows/Linux) that is *not* suited for direct plant-floor control.

**Q4. (Conceptual — 2 marks)**
*Name the four basic types of end effectors used in industrial robots.*

**Quick Answer:** (1) Mechanical grippers, (2) Vacuum grippers, (3) Magnetic grippers, (4) Tool-type end effectors (welding torch, spray gun, drilling spindle).

**Q5. (Numerical — 3 marks)**
*A 4-axis SCARA robot has 5 moving links and 5 revolute joints. Calculate its planar DOF using Gruebler's equation.*

**Quick Answer:**
$M = 3(5 - 1) - 2(5) - 0 = 12 - 10 = 2$ DOF (in-plane); the 4th axis (vertical prismatic) is handled separately.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- [ ] **PLC = "industrial computer"** — solid-state, real-time, ladder-logic-programmed, harsh-environment-ready.
- [ ] **Five PLC blocks:** Power Supply, CPU, Memory (ROM/RAM/EEPROM), I/O Module, Programming Device.
- [ ] **Scan cycle order (NEVER forget):** **Input → Program → Output → Housekeeping** (then repeat forever).
- [ ] **Scan time equation:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$.
- [ ] **Max input frequency (Nyquist):** $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 T_{scan}}$.
- [ ] **Ladder logic** symbols: NO = —| |—, NC = —|/ |—, Coil = —( )—.
- [ ] **Series contacts = AND** (·), **Parallel contacts = OR** (+).
- [ ] **Industrial robot definition** (ISO 8373): automatically controlled + reprogrammable + multipurpose + ≥3 axes.
- [ ] **Robot anatomy:** Base → Link → Joint → Link → Joint → ... → Wrist → End-effector.
- [ ] **DOF** = number of independent movements. 6 DOF = full 3D dexterity.
- [ ] **Gruebler's equation (planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [ ] **Robot types (acronym "CCSSAD"):** **C**artesian, **C**ylindrical, **S**pherical, **S**CARA, **A**rticulated, **D**elta.
- [ ] **Workspace volumes:** Cartesian = cuboid, Cylindrical = hollow cylinder sector, Spherical = partial sphere.
- [ ] **Cylindrical workspace formula:** $V = \dfrac{1}{2} \theta (R^2 - r^2) h$ — *always convert θ to radians.*
- [ ] **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy:** Repeatability = statistical consistency of return to a taught point; Accuracy = ability to reach a computed point.
- [ ] **Repeatability formula:** $R = \bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$ (3-sigma band = 99.73% confidence).
- [ ] **Four end-effector types:** Mechanical, Vacuum, Magnetic, Tool (welding/painting/drilling).
- [ ] **PLC origin:** 1968, Dick Morley, GM Hydramatic Division, MODICON 084.
- [ ] **KTU 14-mark question pattern:** Two sub-parts × 7 marks = architecture/scan cycle OR robot types/workspace.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Golden Tip for KTU 2024:**
> When answering a PLC scan-cycle question, **always draw the four blocks in a closed loop** and label them in order. When answering a robot-type question, **always include the workspace shape (with units)** — this alone can earn you 2 extra marks compared to peers who only name the type.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Logic Controller: Basic PLC Structure, I/O Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controller: Basic PLC Structure, I/O Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, microprocessor-based, industrial-grade digital computing device designed specifically for the reliable and deterministic control of manufacturing processes and automated machinery in harsh industrial environments. It executes user-defined logical, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations on discrete and continuous input signals to drive output actuators, following a cyclic **scan-based execution philosophy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Definition Statement (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> *"A PLC is a specialized digital computer used for the automation of industrial electromechanical processes. It monitors inputs, makes decisions based on a stored program, and controls outputs to automate a machine or process."*

The **Basic PLC Structure** refers to the canonical hardware architecture consisting of a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, a **Memory Subsystem**, an **Input Interface Module**, an **Output Interface Module**, a **Power Supply Unit**, and a **Programming/Communication Interface**.

**I/O Processing** refers to the systematic, cyclic method by which the PLC acquires real-world input signals, interprets them in accordance with the user logic, and updates the output signals—governed by a deterministic **scan cycle** measured in milliseconds.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **traffic police officer** standing at a busy four-way intersection. Your job is straightforward but repetitive:

- **Inputs** → You constantly *look* at the traffic (cars, pedestrians, light sensors).
- **Processor (Brain)** → Your *mind* applies the traffic rules you were taught ("if North-South is green AND no emergency vehicle is present, then keep it green; otherwise, switch").
- **Memory** → The *rulebook* of traffic laws stored in your head, plus a small notebook of the current state ("North light is currently GREEN, timer has 12 seconds left").
- **Output** → Your *hand* presses the buttons to switch the lights, change signal displays, and ring the pedestrian bell.
- **Power Supply** → The coffee, food, and energy keeping you alert at the post.
- **Programming Device** → The *training officer* who originally taught you the rules and can update them.

A PLC does *exactly* this, but with extreme speed (a typical **scan time** is **5–20 ms**), infinite repetition, and zero fatigue. Instead of eyes, it uses electrical sensors; instead of hands, it uses contactors and solenoid valves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Use:** PLCs are the invisible "brains" inside car assembly lines, bottling plants, escalators, traffic signals, elevators, and even roller coasters—anywhere deterministic, rugged, real-time control is required.

---

### 1.3 Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CPU** | Central Processing Unit — the decision-maker |
| **Scan Cycle** | One full loop of Input → Program → Output |
| **Scan Time** | Time taken for one complete scan, typically **10–50 ms** |
| **Discrete I/O** | ON/OFF signals (1 bit), e.g., limit switches, solenoids |
| **Analog I/O** | Continuous variable signals (0–10 V, 4–20 mA) |
| **Ladder Logic (LAD)** | The most common PLC programming language |
| **I/O Module** | The interface between CPU and field devices |
| **Isolated I/O** | I/O electrically separated from CPU using opto-couplers |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Building Blocks of a PLC (Detailed Breakdown)

#### 2.1.1 Power Supply Unit (PSU)
- Converts mains AC (**230 V AC, 50 Hz** in India / Kerala) into regulated DC voltages—typically **+5 V DC** (logic circuits), **+24 V DC** (sensor power), and sometimes **±15 V DC** (analog circuits).
- Must be highly stable, immune to voltage spikes, and capable of withstanding brown-outs. Often includes a **backup battery** (lithium, ~**3.6 V DC**) for retaining RAM contents during power failure.
- Battery retention time: typically **2–5 years** at 25 °C.

#### 2.1.2 Processor (CPU)
- The "brain" of the PLC, comprising:
  - **Microprocessor** (8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit depending on PLC class)
  - **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — performs AND, OR, NOT, comparison, math
  - **Control Unit** — fetches, decodes, and executes instructions
  - **System ROM** — stores the firmware (operating system, scan engine)
- Handles the **scan cycle** (input read, logic solve, output write) relentlessly.
- Modern PLCs embed the CPU in a **System-on-Chip (SoC)** for compactness and reliability.

#### 2.1.3 Memory Subsystem
Divided into two major regions:
- **System Memory (ROM/Flash)** — holds the OS, scan engine, and fixed functions.
- **User Memory (RAM + Battery Backup)** — holds the user's ladder program, data tables, timers, counters.
- Memory is organized as:
  - **Program File** — the user logic
  - **Data File** — input image table, output image table, timer/counter registers, integer storage, bit storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** PLC memory is **non-volatile** in the sense that battery-backed RAM retains data even when mains power is off. This is critical for resuming the process state after a blackout.

#### 2.1.4 Input Module (I in I/O)
- Receives signals from field devices: pushbuttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors, temperature transmitters, pressure transducers.
- Performs:
  1. **Signal conditioning** (filtering, debouncing)
  2. **Electrical isolation** (using **opto-isolators** to protect CPU from surges)
  3. **Voltage level translation** (e.g., 24 V field → 5 V logic)
  4. **Multiplexing** (many inputs share the CPU's input port via analog multiplexers)
- Two wiring standards:
  - **Sinking (NPN)** — current *flows into* the PLC input
  - **Sourcing (PNP)** — current *flows out of* the PLC input
- **Discrete Input Voltages:** 24 V DC, 110 V AC, 230 V AC.

#### 2.1.5 Output Module (O in I/O)
- Drives actuators: relay coils, solenoid valves, contactors, motor starters, indicator lamps, variable frequency drives (VFDs).
- Three major output types:
  - **Relay Output** — handles AC and DC, slow (~10 ms), but rugged. Contact rating up to **2 A at 230 V AC**.
  - **Transistor Output (DC only)** — fast (~0.5 ms), for DC loads, sourcing or sinking variants.
  - **Triac Output (AC only)** — fast switching of AC loads, used for motor and heater control.
- Like inputs, outputs are **opto-isolated** and protected with **fuses**, **MOVs (Metal Oxide Varistors)**, and **RC snubbers**.

#### 2.1.6 Programming & Communication Interface
- Modern PLCs use:
  - **USB / Ethernet / RS-232 / RS-485** ports
  - **Industrial Fieldbuses** — PROFIBUS, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/RTU, EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet, CANopen
- The programming device (laptop with vendor software) downloads the ladder program to the PLC.
- HMI (Human-Machine Interface) panels also connect here for operator interaction.

---

### 2.2 I/O Processing — The Scan Cycle

The PLC operates on a deterministic, **cyclic scan architecture**. Every scan cycle consists of **three mandatory phases**, with optional housekeeping phases (communication, diagnostics).

#### Phase 1: Input Scan (Read Phase)
- The CPU reads the **status of all physical inputs** and stores their states in a dedicated region of memory called the **Input Image Table (IIT)** or **Process Input Image (PII)**.
- During this phase, the CPU does NOT execute the user program. Inputs are sampled *once*, in a single batch.
- This guarantees a *consistent* snapshot of the process at the start of program execution.

#### Phase 2: Program Scan (Execute Phase)
- The CPU executes the user program **one instruction at a time**, sequentially, from top to bottom (and left to right within each ladder rung).
- The program reads from the **Input Image Table** (not directly from the physical inputs) and writes results to the **Output Image Table (OIT)** or **Process Output Image (POI)**.
- This decoupling is what makes the scan deterministic.

#### Phase 3: Output Scan (Write Phase)
- After the program finishes, the CPU copies the **Output Image Table** to the physical output modules in one batch, energizing or de-energizing connected loads.
- The cycle then repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Consequence:** The **scan time** directly limits the maximum frequency of input signal changes that the PLC can reliably detect. Any input pulse shorter than one scan time can be *missed* — this is the well-known **missed-pulse problem**.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Scan Time | $T_{scan}$ | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$ | ms | Sum of all four phases |
| Program Execution Time | $T_{prog}$ | $T_{prog} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | ms | Sum over all $n$ instructions |
| Average Scan Time | $\bar{T}_{scan}$ | $\bar{T}_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} T_{scan,j}$ | ms | Across $N$ cycles |
| Max Detectable Pulse Width | $W_{max}$ | $W_{max} \geq T_{scan}$ | ms | Any pulse shorter is missed |
| I/O Throughput | $R_{I/O}$ | $R_{I/O} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | scans/s | Inverse of scan time |
| Resolution of Analog Input | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n}}$ | V or mA | $n$ = ADC bits, $FS$ = full scale |
| Memory Retention | $t_{ret}$ | typically **2–5 years** | years | For lithium battery backup |
| Opto-isolation Voltage | $V_{iso}$ | typically **1500–2500 V RMS** | V | Between field and logic |
| Relay Output Response | $t_{relay}$ | approx **10 ms** | ms | On/Off mechanical delay |
| Transistor Output Response | $t_{trans}$ | approx **0.5 ms** | ms | Solid-state switching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention in Tables:** The notation $Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n}}$ should be read as "$Q$ equals FS divided by 2 to the power n". The pipe symbol `\vert` is reserved in tables for divisibility only, never for absolute value.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Welding robots, paint shops, conveyor sequencing — controlled by distributed PLCs networked via PROFINET.
- **Water Treatment:** PLCs monitor pH, turbidity, flow, and automatically dose chemicals.
- **Power Plants:** PLCs trip circuit breakers in milliseconds when faults are detected.
- **Smart Buildings:** HVAC, lighting, and access control systems use PLCs for energy-efficient operation.
- **Food & Beverage:** Bottle filling lines require precise timing — handled by PLC counters and timers with millisecond resolution.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Scan Time Composition

The total scan time of a PLC is the sum of the time spent in each phase of the scan cycle. We derive this from first principles.

**Step 1 — Identify the four canonical phases.**

A complete PLC scan cycle comprises the following time intervals:

- $T_{in}$ — time required to read all physical input channels and load them into the Input Image Table
- $T_{prog}$ — time required to execute the user program (ladder logic)
- $T_{out}$ — time required to write the Output Image Table to the physical output modules
- $T_{comm}$ — time consumed by background communication and housekeeping tasks

**Step 2 — Express the total as a sum.**

Since these four phases occur sequentially in a non-overlapping manner, the total scan time is simply their arithmetic sum.

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}
$$

**Step 3 — Note the units and typical magnitudes.**

All terms are in **milliseconds (ms)**. Typical industrial values:

- $T_{in} \approx 1 \text{ to } 3 \text{ ms}$ (for 16–32 discrete inputs)
- $T_{prog} \approx 2 \text{ to } 10 \text{ ms}$ (depends on program length)
- $T_{out} \approx 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ ms}$
- $T_{comm} \approx 1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ ms}$

Hence, for a mid-size PLC, $T_{scan} \approx 5$ to **$20 \text{ ms}$**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Program Execution Time from Instruction Count

**Step 1 — Decompose program execution into individual instructions.**

Let the user program contain $n$ instructions. Each instruction $i$ takes a specific execution time $t_i$ that depends on its type (contact, coil, timer, math, comparison, etc.).

**Step 2 — Form the summation.**

Since instructions are executed serially (one after another), the total program execution time is:

$$
T_{prog} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \cdots + t_n
$$

**Step 3 — Sample numerical illustration.**

Consider a simple program with 5 contacts, 3 coils, and 1 TON timer.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prog} &= 5 \cdot t_{contact} + 3 \cdot t_{coil} + 1 \cdot t_{TON} \\
&= 5(0.5) + 3(0.5) + 1(50.0) \\
&= 2.5 + 1.5 + 50.0 \\
&= 54.0 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

where typical instruction times are $t_{contact} = 0.5 \ \mu s$, $t_{coil} = 0.5 \ \mu s$, and $t_{TON} = 50 \ \mu s$ for a small PLC.

**Step 4 — Compute the total scan time.**

Adding the other phases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm} \\
&= 2 + 0.054 + 1 + 3 \\
&= 6.054 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Detectable Input Pulse Width

**Step 1 — The "missed pulse" problem.**

If an input signal transitions from LOW to HIGH and back to LOW within a single scan interval, the Input Scan phase may either:

- See the input as LOW both before and after the pulse (if the pulse occurred *between* two scans)
- See the input as HIGH in one scan and LOW in the next (catching the trailing edge)

**Step 2 — The condition for reliable detection.**

For the PLC to detect a pulse, the pulse width $W$ must be greater than or equal to the scan time:

$$
W \geq T_{scan}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example.**

If $T_{scan} = 10$ ms, then any pulse shorter than 10 ms may be missed. To reliably detect a 5 ms pulse, one would need a scan time:

$$
T_{scan} \leq W = 5 \ \text{ms}
$$

This often requires **high-speed counter (HSC) modules** or **interrupt-driven I/O**, which bypass the normal scan.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Analog Input Resolution (Quantization Step)

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**

An analog input must be digitized by an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) of $n$-bit resolution over a full-scale range $FS$ (e.g., $0$ to $10$ V or $4$ to $20$ mA).

**Step 2 — Express the number of quantization levels.**

A binary $n$-bit ADC produces $2^{n}$ distinct output codes (from $0$ to $2^{n} - 1$).

**Step 3 — Derive the resolution (size of one Least Significant Bit).**

$$
Q = \frac{FS}{2^{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For a 12-bit ADC with $FS = 10$ V:

$$
Q = \frac{10 \ \text{V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10 \ \text{V}}{4096} \approx 2.44 \ \text{mV per bit}
$$

For a 16-bit ADC with $FS = 20$ mA$:

$$
Q = \frac{20 \ \text{mA}}{2^{16}} = \frac{20 \ \text{mA}}{65536} \approx 0.305 \ \mu A \ \text{per bit}
$$

This explains why **high-precision processes (e.g., pharmaceutical dosing)** demand 16-bit or higher ADC modules.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Output Module Types

| Feature | Relay Output | Transistor Output | Triac Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load type | AC and DC | DC only | AC only |
| Switching speed | ~**10 ms** (slow) | ~**0.5 ms** (fast) | ~**1 ms** (fast) |
| Electrical isolation | Mechanical (galvanic) | Opto-isolated | Opto-isolated |
| Contact life | ~100,000 cycles | Unlimited (no moving part) | Unlimited |
| Leakage current | None | Small (< 1 mA) | Small (< 2 mA) |
| Surge protection | Built-in MOV recommended | Required (flyback diode) | Required (RC snubber) |
| Typical application | General-purpose, motor starters | High-speed solenoids, DC valves | Heaters, AC lamps |
| Voltage rating | up to 250 V AC, 30 V DC | up to 30 V DC | up to 400 V AC |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Input Wiring Topologies

| Feature | Sinking Input (NPN) | Sourcing Input (PNP) |
|---|---|---|
| Current flow | Into the PLC input terminal | Out of the PLC input terminal |
| Sensor type | NPN sensor (common positive) | PNP sensor (common negative) |
| Common terminal | Positive (+V) supply | Negative (0 V) supply |
| Compatibility | Common in Japan, parts of Asia | Common in Europe, USA |
| Industrial safety | Less preferred (positive common) | More preferred (negative common) |
| Example sensor | Autonics, Omron NPN series | Sick, Pepperl+Fuchs PNP series |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Basic PLC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> B[Power Supply Unit]
    B --> C[CPU and Memory]
    B --> D[Input Module]
    B --> E[Output Module]
    F[Field Sensors] --> D
    D -->|Opto Isolation| C
    C -->|Opto Isolation| E
    E --> G[Field Actuators]
    H[Programming Device PC] -->|USB or Ethernet| C
    I[HMI Panel] -->|Industrial Bus| C
    J[Battery Backup] --> C
    classDef source fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#1F4E79
    classDef cpufill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#C55A11,color:#000
    classDef iofill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    classDef fieldfill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    class A,F,G,H,I,J source
    class B,C cpufill
    class D,E iofill
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — PLC Scan Cycle (Cyclic Execution)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start of Scan Cycle] --> P1[Read Physical Inputs]
    P1 --> P2[Store to Input Image Table]
    P2 --> P3[Execute User Ladder Program]
    P3 --> P4[Update Output Image Table]
    P4 --> P5[Write to Physical Outputs]
    P5 --> P6[Background Comm and Diagnostics]
    P6 --> P7[Wait for Watchdog Tick]
    P7 --> S1
    classDef io fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    classDef prog fill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#C55A11,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    classDef bg fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    class P1,P2 io
    class P3,P4 prog
    class P5 out
    class P6,P7 bg
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Flow of I/O Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS[Field Signal 24V or 4-20mA] --> SC[Signal Conditioning Filter]
    SC --> ISO[Opto Isolator 1500V RMS]
    ISO --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer]
    MUX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter n-bit]
    ADC --> IIT[Input Image Table RAM]
    IIT --> CPU[CPU Executes Ladder Logic]
    CPU --> OIT[Output Image Table RAM]
    OIT --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter]
    DAC --> ISO2[Opto Isolator]
    ISO2 --> DRV[Output Driver Relay or Transistor or Triac]
    DRV --> FL[Field Load Solenoid or Motor or Lamp]
    classDef fieldfill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    classDef isol fill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#C55A11,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    class FS,FL fieldfill
    class SC,ISO,ISO2 isol
    class MUX,ADC,DAC,CPU,IIT,OIT core
    class DRV out
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Architecture — Memory Organization in PLC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MEM[PLC Memory Subsystem] --> SYS[System Memory ROM or Flash]
    MEM --> USR[User Memory RAM Battery Backed]
    SYS --> OS[Operating System and Scan Engine]
    SYS --> FIRM[Firmware and System Functions]
    USR --> PROG[Program File Ladder Logic]
    USR --> DATA[Data File]
    DATA --> IIT2[Input Image Table IIT]
    DATA --> OIT2[Output Image Table OIT]
    DATA --> TIM[Timer Registers T0 to T255]
    DATA --> CTR[Counter Registers C0 to C255]
    DATA --> INT[Integer Storage N7:0 etc]
    DATA --> BIN[Bit Storage B3:0 etc]
    classDef sysfill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    classDef userfill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#C55A11,color:#000
    class SYS,OS,FIRM sysfill
    class USR,PROG userfill
    class DATA,IIT2,OIT2,TIM,CTR,INT,BIN data
```

### 4.5 Sequence Diagram — Input Program Output Cycle Timing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Field Sensors
    participant I as Input Module
    participant IIT as Input Image Table
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant OIT as Output Image Table
    participant O as Output Module
    participant L as Field Loads
    Note over S,L: Phase 1 Input Scan
    S->>I: Real time analog or discrete signal
    I->>IIT: Latch all input states
    Note over IIT: Snapshot of process state
    Note over S,L: Phase 2 Program Scan
    CPU->>IIT: Read input bits
    CPU->>CPU: Execute ladder rungs serially
    CPU->>OIT: Write computed output bits
    Note over S,L: Phase 3 Output Scan
    OIT->>O: Transfer all output states
    O->>L: Energize or de-energize actuators
    Note over S,L: Housekeeping Communication
    CPU->>CPU: Handle comm diagnostics watchdog
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). List any four main components of a PLC.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a microprocessor-based, ruggedized digital computer designed for the deterministic real-time control of industrial processes in harsh environments. It uses a stored user program to make logical decisions based on input signals and produces output signals to drive actuators.

The four main components of a PLC are:

1. **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** — converts AC mains to regulated DC voltages for internal circuits.
2. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — the brain that executes the user program and manages I/O.
3. **Memory** — stores the operating system, user program, and process data.
4. **Input/Output (I/O) Modules** — interface the CPU with field devices; provide signal conditioning and electrical isolation.

*(Additional optional components: Programming device, Communication interface, HMI.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the term "scan time" of a PLC. What are the three main phases of a PLC scan cycle?*

**Model Answer:**

**Scan time** is the total time taken by a PLC to complete one full cycle of reading all inputs, executing the user program once, and updating all outputs. It is measured in milliseconds (typically **5 to 20 ms** for mid-range PLCs) and is the fundamental metric of PLC responsiveness.

The three main phases of a PLC scan cycle are:

1. **Input Scan (Read Phase):** The CPU reads the status of all physical input channels and stores them in the **Input Image Table (IIT)**.
2. **Program Scan (Execute Phase):** The CPU executes the user ladder logic instruction by instruction, reading from the IIT and writing to the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
3. **Output Scan (Write Phase):** The CPU transfers the contents of the OIT to the physical output modules, energizing or de-energizing connected loads.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the Input Image Table with the physical input.** Many students write "the program reads from the input" — INCORRECT. The program reads from the *Input Image Table*, which is a memory snapshot. The actual physical input is read only once, during the Input Scan phase. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Listing only 3–4 PLC components and stopping.** The expected minimum is **six** components for full marks.
> 3. **Writing "PLC stands for Programmable Logic Computer."** This is a frequently repeated wrong answer. It is **Controller**, not Computer. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Failing to mention opto-isolation** when describing I/O modules. This is a key KTU buzzword.
> 5. **Conflating scan time with program execution time.** Scan time = input + program + output + communication.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic structure of a PLC** and describe the function of each block.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Describe in detail the **three phases of PLC I/O processing**, highlighting the role of the Input Image Table and Output Image Table.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Basic Structure of a PLC (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram:**

```
[AC Mains] → [Power Supply] → [CPU & Memory]
                                   ↕
                          [Input Module] ← [Field Sensors]
                          [Output Module] → [Field Actuators]
                                   ↕
                          [Programming Device / HMI]
                          [Battery Backup]
```

**Function of each block:**

1. **Power Supply Unit (1 Mark):**
   Converts 230 V AC mains to regulated DC voltages (**+5 V, +24 V, ±15 V**) required by the CPU, memory, and I/O modules. Includes a backup battery for memory retention.

2. **Central Processing Unit (1.5 Marks):**
   Contains the microprocessor, ALU, and control unit. Executes the user program in a cyclic scan, manages I/O, handles communication, and updates the watchdog timer. It is the decision-making core.

3. **Memory (1 Mark):**
   Divided into System Memory (ROM/Flash — holds OS) and User Memory (RAM — holds program and data). Data includes Input Image Table, Output Image Table, timer/counter registers, and bit storage.

4. **Input Module (1 Mark):**
   Receives signals from field devices, performs signal conditioning, provides **opto-isolation** (typically **1500 V RMS**), and translates field voltages to logic levels. Supports both discrete (24 V DC, 110 V AC) and analog (0–10 V, 4–20 mA) inputs.

5. **Output Module (1 Mark):**
   Drives actuators via relay, transistor, or triac outputs. Provides isolation and surge protection. Energizes loads based on values in the Output Image Table.

6. **Programming & Communication Interface (0.5 Marks):**
   USB/Ethernet/serial ports for downloading programs and connecting to HMIs and fieldbuses (Modbus, PROFINET, etc.).

7. **Battery Backup (0.5 Marks):**
   Lithium battery (~**3.6 V**) preserves RAM contents for **2–5 years** during power outage.

**Common wiring configurations:** *Sinking (NPN)* — current flows into the input; *Sourcing (PNP)* — current flows out of the input.

---

**(b) Three Phases of I/O Processing (7 Marks)**

The PLC executes a **cyclic scan** that perpetually repeats three phases:

**Phase 1: Input Scan (2.5 Marks)**
- At the start of every cycle, the CPU reads the *current state* of all physical input channels (whether each input is 0 or 1).
- These states are written into a dedicated memory region called the **Input Image Table (IIT)** or Process Input Image (PII).
- The IIT is **updated only once per scan**, providing a *consistent* snapshot of the process for the program to work with.
- During this phase, the user program is *not* executing.

**Phase 2: Program Scan (2.5 Marks)**
- The CPU executes the user's ladder logic *serially*, rung by rung, top to bottom.
- The program **reads input states from the IIT** (not directly from the physical inputs) and performs logical, arithmetic, timing, and counting operations.
- Computed output values are written to the **Output Image Table (OIT)**, NOT to the physical outputs yet.

**Phase 3: Output Scan (2 Marks)**
- After the entire program has been executed, the CPU *copies* the OIT contents to the physical output modules in one batch.
- This energizes or de-energizes the connected actuators (relays, solenoids, lamps, motor starters).

**Why this architecture?**
- It guarantees **deterministic** and **repeatable** behavior — the program always sees a consistent set of inputs and produces a consistent set of outputs in a fixed time.
- **Total scan time:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$, typically **5 to 20 ms**.
- **Limitation:** Inputs are sampled only once per scan, so very fast pulses (shorter than $T_{scan}$) may be missed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the different **types of PLC input and output modules**, with their characteristics and applications.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A PLC has a program execution time of 8 ms, input scan time of 2 ms, output scan time of 1 ms, and communication overhead of 3 ms. Compute the **total scan time** and discuss its significance in detecting input pulses. What is the **maximum reliable pulse frequency** that this PLC can detect for a 50% duty-cycle signal?

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Types of PLC I/O Modules (7 Marks)**

**Input Module Types (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Discrete (Digital) Input Module — DC Voltage (1 Mark)**
   - Accepts ON/OFF signals at 24 V DC, 12 V DC, or 5 V DC.
   - Used with limit switches, pushbuttons, proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors.
   - Typically uses opto-isolators for protection; sinking (NPN) or sourcing (PNP) wiring.

2. **Discrete Input Module — AC Voltage (0.5 Marks)**
   - Accepts 110 V AC or 230 V AC signals.
   - Used for line-voltage applications like mains contactor auxiliary contacts.

3. **Analog Input Module (1 Mark)**
   - Accepts continuous variable signals: 0–10 V, ±10 V, 4–20 mA, 0–20 mA.
   - Uses a multi-channel ADC (12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit).
   - Used for temperature (RTD/thermocouple), pressure, flow, level transmitters.

4. **Specialty Input Modules (1 Mark)**
   - **High-Speed Counter (HSC):** Detects pulses faster than scan time.
   - **Thermocouple / RTD Module:** Built-in linearization and cold-junction compensation.
   - **Encoder / Quadrature Input:** For position and speed feedback.

**Output Module Types (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Relay Output Module (1 Mark)**
   - Electromechanical contacts handle both AC and DC loads.
   - Contact rating up to **2 A at 230 V AC**.
   - Slow response (~**10 ms**); limited contact life (~100,000 cycles).
   - Best for: general-purpose motor starters, indicator lamps, solenoid valves.

2. **Transistor Output Module (1 Mark)**
   - Solid-state DC switching, response ~**0.5 ms**.
   - Sourcing (PNP) or sinking (NPN) variants.
   - Requires flyback diode protection for inductive loads.
   - Best for: high-speed DC solenoids, LED indicators, small DC motors.

3. **Triac Output Module (0.5 Marks)**
   - Solid-state AC switching, response ~**1 ms**.
   - Best for: heaters, AC lamps, soft-start motor control.

4. **Analog Output Module (1 Mark)**
   - Produces 0–10 V or 4–20 mA using a multi-channel DAC.
   - Used to drive variable frequency drives (VFDs), proportional valves, dampers.

---

**(b) Scan Time Calculation & Pulse Detection (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — List the given data.**

$$
T_{in} = 2 \ \text{ms}, \quad T_{prog} = 8 \ \text{ms}, \quad T_{out} = 1 \ \text{ms}, \quad T_{comm} = 3 \ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the total scan time formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm} \\
&= 2 + 8 + 1 + 3 \\
&= 14 \ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum detectable pulse width.**

For reliable detection:

$$
W_{min} = T_{scan} = 14 \ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum reliable pulse frequency for 50% duty cycle.**

For a 50% duty cycle, the pulse width equals half the period:

$$
T_{period} = 2 \cdot W_{min} = 2 \cdot 14 = 28 \ \text{ms}
$$

Therefore, the maximum frequency is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{period}} \\
&= \frac{1}{28 \ \text{ms}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.028 \ \text{s}} \\
&\approx 35.71 \ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Significance and engineering interpretation.**

- Any pulse train with frequency **above 35.71 Hz** cannot be reliably detected by the standard scan cycle; pulses may be **missed entirely** (the **missed-pulse problem**).
- For such high-frequency signals, a **High-Speed Counter (HSC) module** is mandatory — these operate *independently* of the scan cycle and can count pulses in the kHz range.
- This is critical in applications like **flow metering** (turbine flowmeters), **encoder-based position sensing**, and **event counting**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month Exam Cram — Essential Takeaways:**

- [x] **PLC** = Programmable Logic **Controller** (NOT Computer). Solid-state, microprocessor-based, industrial-grade.
- [x] **Six building blocks:** Power Supply, CPU, Memory, Input Module, Output Module, Programming/Communication Interface.
- [x] **Power Supply voltages:** +5 V, +24 V, ±15 V DC derived from 230 V AC mains.
- [x] **Battery backup:** ~3.6 V lithium; retains RAM for 2–5 years.
- [x] **I/O modules must be opto-isolated** to protect CPU from field-side surges (1500–2500 V RMS isolation).
- [x] **Three output types:** Relay (~10 ms, AC+DC), Transistor (~0.5 ms, DC only), Triac (~1 ms, AC only).
- [x] **Two input wiring standards:** Sinking (NPN) and Sourcing (PNP). Sourcing (PNP) is preferred in Europe for safety.
- [x] **Three scan phases:** Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan. Plus background Communication.
- [x] **Input Image Table (IIT)** and **Output Image Table (OIT)** are the *only* memory regions the user program reads from / writes to.
- [x] **Scan time formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$ — typically 5–20 ms.
- [x] **Missed-pulse rule:** $W \geq T_{scan}$ — any pulse shorter than scan time may be lost.
- [x] **Analog resolution:** $Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n}}$; 12-bit ADC over 10 V = **2.44 mV per LSB**.
- [x] **Most common programming language:** **Ladder Logic (LAD)** — graphical, resembling electrical relay schematics.
- [x] **Industrial fieldbuses:** PROFINET, PROFIBUS, Modbus TCP/RTU, EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet, CANopen.
- [x] **High-Speed Counters (HSC)** bypass the scan cycle for sub-millisecond pulse detection.
- [x] **Watchdog timer** resets the CPU if the scan hangs — critical for fail-safe operation.
- [x] **Common KTU buzzwords:** "deterministic," "opto-isolated," "scan cycle," "image table," "sinking vs. sourcing," "NPN vs. PNP," "ladder logic."
- [x] **Avoid in answers:** saying "Computer" instead of "Controller"; confusing scan time with program time; omitting the IIT/OIT concept.
- [x] **Practice problem (must-do):** Given $T_{in}$, $T_{prog}$, $T_{out}$, $T_{comm}$, compute $T_{scan}$ and $f_{max}$ for 50% duty cycle — appears in almost every KTU exam.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Robotics: Basic Concepts, Robot Anatomy, Robotics and Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Robotics: Basic Concepts, Robot Anatomy, Robotics and Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Industrial Robot

The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** defines an industrial robot as:

> *"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

The **Robotic Industries Association (RIA)** offers a complementary definition:

> *"An industrial robot is a reprogrammable multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The word **"reprogrammable"** is the cornerstone of the robot definition. A hard-wired, single-purpose machine is NOT a robot — it is merely an automated machine. Robots are distinguished by their ability to execute a *new program* without physical modification.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intelligent Human Arm" Model

Imagine extending your own arm to pick up a glass of water from a table. To perform this seemingly simple task, your body executes a precise chain of events:

| Human Body Component | Robot Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Bones & joints | **Manipulator (Links & Joints)** | Provides structural skeleton & allows movement |
| Muscles | **Actuators** (Hydraulic / Pneumatic / Electric) | Produces the force/torque required for motion |
| Hand & fingers | **End Effector** (Gripper, Tool, Welder) | Performs the actual useful work on the object |
| Eyes, skin, ears | **Sensors** (Vision, Force, Proximity) | Feeds back information about the environment |
| Brain & nervous system | **Controller & Software** | Plans motion, processes sensor data, executes commands |
| Heart & circulatory system | **Power Supply & Transmission** | Delivers energy (electricity / fluid) to all subsystems |

### The Intuitive Workflow
1. You **decide** where to grasp (Controller plans trajectory).
2. Your **brain commands** muscles to contract (Controller sends signals to actuators).
3. Your **joints rotate** in coordinated fashion (Manipulator moves).
4. Your **hand opens, closes, and applies just enough force** (End Effector executes task).
5. Your **eyes monitor** the glass — if slipping, you tighten grip (Sensor feedback loop).

> [!NOTE]
> A robot is, quite literally, a **mechatronic emulation of the human arm**, with each biological function mapped to an engineering subsystem. The Mechatronics essence (the synergy of Mechanical + Electrical + Electronics + Computer + Control Engineering) is embodied in every robot.

---

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Performance Metrics of Robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five metrics appear in nearly every KTU board exam question on robotics. Memorize the definitions precisely.

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent rotational or translational axes along which the robot (or its end effector) can be positioned and oriented. Most industrial robots have **4 to 6 DOF**.
- **Payload Capacity:** The maximum weight the robot can manipulate while maintaining rated performance, typically ranging from **3 kg to 1000+ kg**.
- **Reach:** The maximum distance from the robot's base to the end of the wrist — defines the working volume.
- **Repeatability:** The robot's ability to return to a previously taught position under identical conditions. Modern industrial robots achieve repeatability of **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**.
- **Accuracy:** The absolute deviation between a commanded position and the actual attained position — typically **larger** than repeatability (≈ 1 to 10 times worse).

> [!NOTE]
> **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy.** A robot may consistently return to the *same* point (high repeatability) but that point may not be the *target* point (low accuracy). Repeatability is statistically quantifiable; accuracy depends on calibration.

---

## 1.4 Automation vs. Robotics — Clearing the Confusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automation** is the broader concept — the use of machines, control systems, and information technologies to perform tasks with minimal human intervention. **Robotics** is a *subset* of automation that specifically involves programmable, multi-axis physical agents.

| Aspect | Automation | Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Broad (includes conveyors, PLCs, process control) | Narrow (focused on reprogrammable manipulators) |
| Reprogrammability | Not required (e.g., fixed automation) | Essential (defining property) |
| Decision-making | Often rule-based, static | Can be sensor-driven, adaptive |
| Mobility | Usually fixed | Can be mobile (AGV, AMR) |
| Human Form Factor | Not required | Often anthropomorphic (but not mandatory) |

### Three Hierarchical Levels of Automation (per KTU Module 4)

1. **Fixed Automation** — Hard-wired for a single task (e.g., car-body welding transfer lines). Highest throughput, lowest flexibility.
2. **Programmable Automation** — Reconfigured by reprogramming for batch production of different parts (e.g., CNC machines, industrial robots). Medium flexibility.
3. **Flexible Automation** — Real-time reconfiguration with minimal downtime; handles mixed-model production (e.g., FMS — Flexible Manufacturing Systems). Highest flexibility.

---

## 1.5 Historical Context & Industrial Significance

- **1954:** George Devol filed the first patent for a programmable robot (Unimate).
- **1961:** Unimate installed at GM's Inland Fisher Guide plant in New Jersey — the first industrial robot.
- **1970s–80s:** Introduction of electric servo-driven robots (e.g., PUMA, SCARA).
- **2000s–Present:** Collaborative robots (cobots), AI-integrated vision systems, and Industry 4.0 paradigms.

**Industrial applications dominating global robot deployment:**

- **Material Handling** (~30%) — Pick-and-place, palletizing
- **Welding** (~25%) — Spot welding, arc welding
- **Assembly** (~15%) — Electronics, automotive sub-assemblies
- **Painting & Dispensing** (~10%)
- **Machining & Inspection** (~20%)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Robot Anatomy — Detailed Subsystem Breakdown

A complete industrial robot comprises **six fundamental subsystems** working in closed-loop harmony.

### 2.1.1 The Manipulator (Mechanical Unit / Arm)

The manipulator is the kinematic chain of **rigid links** connected by **joints** that physically positions and orients the end effector.

**Two principal joint types:**

| Joint Type | Symbol | Motion | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | R | Rotation about an axis | Shoulder, elbow, wrist joints |
| **Prismatic (P)** | P | Linear translation along an axis | In/out extension, vertical lift |

**Notation:** The joint sequence is read from base to wrist. A *RRR* manipulator has three revolute joints; a *PPR* has two prismatic followed by one revolute.

### 2.1.2 The End Effector (EOAT — End of Arm Tooling)

The specialized tooling mounted on the robot's wrist flange (typically ISO 9409-1 mechanical interface) that interacts with the workpiece.

**Two broad categories:**
- **Grippers (Mechanical contact):**
  - Mechanical jaw grippers (2-finger, 3-finger angular, parallel)
  - Vacuum grippers (suction cups)
  - Magnetic grippers (electromagnets, permanent magnets)
  - Pneumatic grippers
- **Tools (Non-contact or specialized):**
  - Arc welding torches
  - Spot welding guns
  - Spray painting nozzles
  - Drilling spindles, deburring tools
  - Waterjet/laser cutters

### 2.1.3 Actuators — The "Muscles"

| Actuator Type | Energy Source | Advantages | Disadvantages | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized fluid (oil) | High force/power, self-lubricating, fast response | Leakage, noisy, requires pump & reservoir | Heavy payload (e.g., die-casting, forging) |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air | Clean, simple, cheap, fast | Low force, hard to control precisely | Pick-and-place, small parts handling |
| **Electric (Servo)** | Electric motors (DC/AC servo, stepper) | High precision, clean, programmable | Lower power density, heat dissipation | Most modern industrial robots (KUKA, FANUC, ABB) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** When asked to "select an actuator for a spray-painting robot," the answer is **hydraulic or pneumatic** (explosion-proof electrical safety is critical for solvent vapors). For **precision assembly**, the answer is always **electric servo**.

### 2.1.4 Sensors — Internal (Proprioceptive) and External (Exteroceptive)

| Sensor | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoders (optical, magnetic)** | Internal | Joint position & velocity feedback |
| **Tachometers / Resolvers** | Internal | Speed and angular position |
| **Strain gauges / Load cells** | Internal | Force/torque measurement at wrist |
| **Vision cameras (2D, 3D)** | External | Object recognition, inspection, guidance |
| **Proximity (inductive, capacitive, ultrasonic)** | External | Detect nearby obstacles / parts |
| **Tactile / Pressure sensor arrays** | External | Grasp feedback, slip detection |

### 2.1.5 The Controller — The Robot's "Brain"

The controller houses the **CPU, memory, I/O modules, and communication interfaces** that execute the user program, interpolate trajectories, and close servo loops on each axis.

**Modern robot controllers support:**
- Real-time trajectory planning (PTP, Linear, Circular interpolation)
- Multiple programming languages (e.g., RAPID for ABB, KRL for KUKA, KAREL for FANUC)
- Fieldbus protocols: EtherCAT, PROFINET, DeviceNet
- Digital twin integration for simulation (e.g., ABB RobotStudio, FANUC ROBOGUIDE)

### 2.1.6 Power Supply & Transmission

- **Electric power conditioning** for servo drives (typically 400–480 V AC, 3-phase).
- **Hydraulic/Pneumatic supply** includes pumps, compressors, reservoirs, filters, regulators, and lubricators (FRL unit).

---

## 2.2 Robot Reference Coordinate Frames

Every robot motion is described in **three distinct frames**, each critical for KTU numerical and conceptual questions.

1. **World Frame (Base Frame) {B}** — Fixed to the robot base; the global reference for the entire cell.
2. **Joint Frame {J}** — Attached at each joint; describes rotation (θᵢ) or translation (dᵢ) relative to the previous link.
3. **Tool Frame (End Effector Frame) {T}** — Attached at the end-effector tip; describes the pose of the tool relative to the workpiece.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pose** = **Position** (x, y, z) + **Orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw). Six degrees of freedom (3 position + 3 orientation) are needed for *arbitrary* pose in 3D space.

---

## 2.3 Six Robot Kinematic Configurations (Asimov & McCorkle Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most heavily-tested table in Module 4. KTU 2024 board papers have appeared every year asking for a comparison.

| Configuration | Joint Sequence | Work Envelope Shape | Typical Applications | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian / Gantry (PPP)** | P–P–P | Rectangular box | Pick-and-place, CNC loading | High accuracy, large volume, simple kinematics | Requires large floor space, slow, limited reach |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | R–P–P | Cylindrical volume | Machine tending, foundry | Robust, simple, larger reach than Cartesian | Lateral reach limited |
| **Spherical / Polar (RRP)** | R–R–P | Spherical sector | Welding, die-casting | Good reach, high payload capability | Mechanical complexity, lower accuracy |
| **Articulated / Revolute (RRR)** | R–R–R | Irregular sphere (most dexterous) | Assembly, painting, welding | Most versatile, mimics human arm | Most complex kinematics & control |
| **SCARA (RRP)** | R–R–P (selective compliance) | Horizontal cylinder | PCB assembly, vertical insertion | Fast, precise, high stiffness vertically | Limited to vertical-axis insertion tasks |
| **Parallel / Delta** | Multiple parallel chains | Dome / shallow cone | High-speed pick-and-place (food, pharma) | Extremely high speed, high stiffness | Limited workspace, complex design |

### 2.3.1 SCARA — Special Note

> **SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm.** Designed for vertical (Z-axis) assembly tasks. It is **stiff in the vertical direction** (carries heavy loads vertically) but **compliant in the horizontal XY plane** (allows slight passive lateral compliance for peg-in-hole insertion). Patented in 1978 by Hiroshi Makino (Yamanashi University, Japan).

---

## 2.4 Workspace / Work Envelope Geometry

The **work envelope** is the 3D region reachable by the robot's end effector (or wrist center point). For KTU problems, students must derive envelope equations for each configuration.

**Reach parameters:**
- **r₁:** Length of first link (typically upper arm or shoulder offset)
- **r₂:** Length of second link (forearm)
- **d₃:** Vertical prismatic reach (if any)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** In the tables below, the vertical bar `|` has been replaced by `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do NOT use raw `|` for absolute value.

### Table A: Kinematic Chain & DOF Formulas

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom (Grübler-Kutzbach, planar) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = lower pairs, $j_2$ = higher pairs |
| Degrees of Freedom (spatial) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | For 3D mechanisms |
| Joint displacement (Revolute) | $\theta_i$ | Rotational angle about joint axis $i$ |
| Joint displacement (Prismatic) | $d_i$ | Linear translation along joint axis $i$ |
| Tool pose in base frame | ${}^{B}T_{T} = {}^{B}T_{1} \cdot {}^{1}T_{2} \cdot {}^{2}T_{3} \cdots {}^{n-1}T_{T}$ | Forward kinematics chain of homogeneous transforms |

### Table B: Work Envelope Equations

| Configuration | Envelope Equation |
|---|---|
| **Cartesian (PPP)** | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ (rectangular box volume) |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | $V = \pi \cdot R^2 \cdot h$ where $R$ = max radial reach, $h$ = vertical prismatic stroke |
| **Spherical (RRP)** | $V = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R^2 h$ (spherical sector) |
| **Articulated (RRR)** | Approximate outer reach: $R_{max} = r_1 + r_2$, inner dead-zone: $R_{min} = \vert r_1 - r_2 \vert$ |
| **SCARA (RRP)** | $R_{min} = \vert r_1 - r_2 \vert$, $R_{max} = r_1 + r_2$, height = vertical prismatic stroke |

### Table C: Robot Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeatability | — | $\pm 0.02 \text{ to } \pm 0.1$ | mm |
| Accuracy | — | $\pm 0.1 \text{ to } \pm 2.0$ | mm |
| Resolution | — | $\text{Min} \leq 0.001$ | mm |
| Payload | $W_{max}$ | $3 \text{ to } 1000+$ | kg |
| Maximum linear speed | $v_{max}$ | $1 \text{ to } 10$ | m/s |

### Table D: Forward Kinematics (DH Parameters — Summary)

| DH Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Link length | $a_i$ | Distance along $x_i$ from $z_{i-1}$ to $z_i$ |
| Link twist | $\alpha_i$ | Angle about $x_i$ from $z_{i-1}$ to $z_i$ |
| Link offset | $d_i$ | Distance along $z_{i-1}$ from $x_{i-1}$ to $x_i$ |
| Joint angle | $\theta_i$ | Angle about $z_{i-1}$ from $x_{i-1}$ to $x_i$ |

$${}^{i-1}A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forward Kinematics (FK)** is used in **offline programming & simulation** — engineers can verify trajectories before physical execution.
- **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** is used in **real-time motion control** — given a desired tool pose, the controller computes the joint angles $\theta_1 \dots \theta_n$.
- **Jacobian-based velocity analysis** governs **singularity detection** — configurations where the robot loses one or more DOF (end-effector cannot move in a particular direction).
- **Work envelope calculations** determine **factory cell layout** — where to place conveyors, fixtures, and safety fences.
- **Repeatability specifications** drive **selection** for precision assembly (electronics, medical device manufacturing).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Work Envelope Volume for a Cylindrical Configuration (RPP)

> **Problem Statement:** A cylindrical robot has a maximum radial reach of $R = 1.5$ m and a vertical prismatic stroke of $h = 2.0$ m. The base rotation $\theta$ sweeps $300°$ (from $-150°$ to $+150°$). Compute the total reachable work envelope volume.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric shape.**

A cylindrical robot's reachable workspace is bounded by:
- A **horizontal rotation** $\theta$ about the base (Z-axis).
- A **radial prismatic** extension $r$ from the base axis.
- A **vertical prismatic** lift $z$ from the base plane.

The full 3D envelope is therefore a **hollow cylindrical sector** (a wedge of an annulus extruded vertically).

**Step 2 — Define the angular extent.**

The base rotation sweeps an angle of:
$$\Delta\theta = 300° = 300 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{5\pi}{3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Set up the volume integral.**

For a vertical slice at angle $\theta$ with thickness $r \, dr \, d\theta$, the volumetric element over the full radial and vertical extents is:

$$dV = r \, dr \, d\theta \cdot dz$$

The total volume is obtained by integrating over all three ranges:

$$V = \int_{0}^{h} \int_{0}^{R} \int_{-\Delta\theta/2}^{\Delta\theta/2} r \, d\theta \, dr \, dz$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration sequentially.**

First, integrate over $\theta$ (constant limits):

$$\int_{-\Delta\theta/2}^{\Delta\theta/2} d\theta = \Delta\theta = \frac{5\pi}{3}$$

Then integrate over $r$:

$$\int_{0}^{R} r \, dr = \frac{R^2}{2} = \frac{(1.5)^2}{2} = \frac{2.25}{2} = 1.125 \text{ m}^2$$

Then integrate over $z$:

$$\int_{0}^{h} dz = h = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the results.**

$$V = \Delta\theta \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} \cdot h = \frac{5\pi}{3} \cdot 1.125 \cdot 2.0$$

$$V = \frac{5\pi}{3} \cdot 2.25 = \frac{11.25 \pi}{3} = 3.75 \pi$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerical value.**

$$V = 3.75 \times \pi \approx 3.75 \times 3.14159 \approx 11.78 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** If the rotation swept a full $360°$ ($2\pi$ rad), the envelope would be a complete cylinder of volume $\pi R^2 h = \pi (1.5)^2 (2.0) = 4.5\pi \approx 14.14 \text{ m}^3$. Since our angular sweep is $5\pi/3$, which is $5/6$ of $2\pi$, we expect $5/6 \times 14.14 \approx 11.78 \text{ m}^3$. ✓ Matches!

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DOF of a Planar Articulated (RRR) Robot Using Grübler-Kutzbach Criterion

> **Problem Statement:** A planar robot arm has 4 links (including ground) connected by 3 revolute joints. Verify its DOF using the Grübler-Kutzbach formula.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (planar case).**

For planar mechanisms with only lower-pair joints:
$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints: revolute, prismatic)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints: cam-follower, gear contact)

**Step 2 — Count the components.**

- Links: $n = 4$ (ground + link 1 + link 2 + end-effector link)
- Revolute joints: $j_1 = 3$ (shoulder, elbow, wrist)
- Higher pairs: $j_2 = 0$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 0$$

$$M = 3 \cdot 3 - 6$$

$$M = 9 - 6 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

The manipulator has **3 DOF** — it can position its end-effector anywhere in the 2D plane (x, y) but cannot independently control orientation in 3D space. To gain a third orientation DOF (in 3D), three more revolute joints are added at the wrist, giving a 6-DOF articulated robot.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Position of End Effector for a 2-Link Planar RR Robot

> **Problem Statement:** A 2-link planar robot has link lengths $L_1 = 1.0$ m and $L_2 = 0.8$ m. The shoulder joint is at $\theta_1 = 30°$ and the elbow joint at $\theta_2 = 45°$ (relative to link 1). Compute the Cartesian coordinates $(x, y)$ of the end effector.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Establish the coordinate system.**

Place the base of link 1 at the origin. The shoulder rotates by $\theta_1$ from the X-axis; the elbow rotates by an additional $\theta_2$ relative to link 1's extension.

**Step 2 — Compute the position of joint 2 (elbow).**

$$x_2 = L_1 \cos\theta_1$$
$$x_2 = 1.0 \cdot \cos 30° = 1.0 \cdot 0.8660 = 0.8660 \text{ m}$$
$$y_2 = L_1 \sin\theta_1 = 1.0 \cdot \sin 30° = 1.0 \cdot 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the absolute angle of link 2.**

The absolute angle of link 2 with respect to the X-axis is:
$$\phi_2 = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 30° + 45° = 75°$$

**Step 4 — Compute the end-effector position.**

The end effector is at:
$$x_e = x_2 + L_2 \cos\phi_2$$
$$x_e = 0.8660 + 0.8 \cdot \cos 75°$$
$$x_e = 0.8660 + 0.8 \cdot 0.2588$$
$$x_e = 0.8660 + 0.2071 = 1.0731 \text{ m}$$

$$y_e = y_2 + L_2 \sin\phi_2$$
$$y_e = 0.5 + 0.8 \cdot \sin 75°$$
$$y_e = 0.5 + 0.8 \cdot 0.9659$$
$$y_e = 0.5 + 0.7727 = 1.2727 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — General compact form (Forward Kinematics).**

This result generalizes to the famous 2-link planar forward kinematics equations:

$$x_e = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y_e = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The compact FK equations above are derived by summing the link vectors $\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2$, where each link vector is its length times the unit vector along its direction. This vector-sum approach extends naturally to 3D via the Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameterization.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Robot Specification Trade-off Analysis

| Configuration | Reach-to-Footprint Ratio | Stiffness | Speed | Cost Index (relative) | Maintenance Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (PPP) | Low | Highest | Medium | 1.0× | Low |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | Medium | High | Medium | 1.1× | Low |
| Spherical (RRP) | Medium-High | Medium | Medium | 1.3× | Medium |
| Articulated (RRR) | **Highest** | Medium-Low | Medium | **1.6×** | **High** |
| SCARA (RRP) | Medium | High (vertical) | **Highest** | 1.4× | Medium |
| Parallel/Delta | Low (workspace) | **Highest** | **Highest** | 2.0× | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The articulated robot dominates global market share (~60% of all installed units) precisely because of its **anthropomorphic versatility** — it can replicate nearly any human-arm motion. SCARA dominates PCB assembly because of its unparalleled speed-to-precision ratio in the vertical (Z) axis.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Robot

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **complete functional architecture** of an industrial robot, mapping every subsystem and its interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER["Power Supply & Energy Source"]
        PS["Electric Mains 400V AC 3-Phase"]
        HYD["Hydraulic Pump + Reservoir"]
        PNE["Compressed Air FRL Unit"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["Controller — Robot Brain"]
        CPU["Central Processing Unit"]
        MEM["Memory — Program Storage"]
        IO["Digital and Analog IO Modules"]
        FB["Fieldbus — EtherCAT / PROFINET"]
        SW["Software — RAPID / KRL / KAREL"]
    end

    subgraph SENS["Sensor Suite"]
        ENC["Encoders — Position Feedback"]
        TAC["Tachometers — Velocity Feedback"]
        VISION["2D 3D Vision Cameras"]
        FTS["Force Torque Sensor at Wrist"]
        PROX["Proximity and Tactile Sensors"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["Actuator Bank"]
        A1["Servo Motor — Axis 1 Base Rotation"]
        A2["Servo Motor — Axis 2 Shoulder"]
        A3["Servo Motor — Axis 3 Elbow"]
        A4["Servo Motor — Axis 4 Wrist Roll"]
        A5["Servo Motor — Axis 5 Wrist Pitch"]
        A6["Servo Motor — Axis 6 Wrist Yaw"]
    end

    subgraph MAN["Manipulator — Mechanical Arm"]
        L1["Link 1 — Base Column"]
        L2["Link 2 — Upper Arm"]
        L3["Link 3 — Forearm"]
        L4["Link 4 — Wrist Assembly"]
    end

    subgraph EOAT["End of Arm Tooling EOAT"]
        GRIP["Gripper — 2 Finger or Vacuum"]
        TOOL["Specialized Tool — Welder Cutter"]
    end

    subgraph WRK["Workpiece and Environment"]
        PART["Workpiece / Part to be Manipulated"]
        CELL["Surrounding Cell — Conveyors Fixtures"]
    end

    PS --> A1
    PS --> A2
    PS --> A3
    HYD --> A4
    PNE --> A5

    CPU -->|Command Signals| A1
    CPU -->|Command Signals| A2
    CPU -->|Command Signals| A3
    CPU -->|Command Signals| A4
    CPU -->|Command Signals| A5
    CPU -->|Command Signals| A6

    A1 --> L1
    A2 --> L2
    A3 --> L3
    A4 --> L4

    L4 --> EOAT

    ENC -.->|Position Feedback| CPU
    TAC -.->|Velocity Feedback| CPU
    VISION -.->|Object Recognition| CPU
    FTS -.->|Force Feedback| CPU
    PROX -.->|Collision Avoidance| CPU

    SW --> CPU
    FB --> CPU
    MEM --> CPU
    IO --> CPU

    EOAT <-->|Manipulate| PART
    L1 -.->|Operates Within| CELL
```

## 4.2 Robot Configuration Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Application Requirement Identified"]
    Q1{"Primary Task Type?"}
    Q2{"Required Workspace Shape?"}
    Q3{"DOF and Dexterity Required?"}
    Q4{"Speed vs Precision Priority?"}

    CART["Cartesian PPP Configuration"]
    CYL["Cylindrical RPP Configuration"]
    SPH["Spherical RRP Configuration"]
    ART["Articulated RRR Configuration"]
    SCA["SCARA RRP Configuration"]
    DEL["Delta Parallel Configuration"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Pick and Place Horizontal Transfer| Q2
    Q1 -->|Vertical Assembly Insertion| SCA
    Q1 -->|Heavy Welding Painting| Q3
    Q1 -->|Ultra Fast Sorting| DEL

    Q2 -->|Rectangular Box| CART
    Q2 -->|Cylindrical Volume| CYL

    Q3 -->|High Dexterity Multi Plane| ART
    Q3 -->|Moderate Reach Solid Volume| SPH
```

---

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WORLD["World Frame B Fixed to Robot Base Origin"]
    JOINT1["Joint Frame 1 — Base Rotation about Z"]
    JOINT2["Joint Frame 2 — Shoulder Rotation"]
    JOINT3["Joint Frame 3 — Elbow Rotation"]
    JOINT4["Joint Frame 4 — Wrist Roll"]
    JOINT5["Joint Frame 5 — Wrist Pitch"]
    JOINT6["Joint Frame 6 — Wrist Yaw"]
    TOOL["Tool Frame T — At End Effector Tip"]

    WORLD --> JOINT1 --> JOINT2 --> JOINT3 --> JOINT4 --> JOINT5 --> JOINT6 --> TOOL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pose Transformation:** Each transition between adjacent frames is governed by a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix that includes rotation and translation. The complete tool pose in world coordinates is the product of all six matrices: ${}^{B}T_{T} = {}^{B}A_{1} \cdot {}^{1}A_{2} \cdot {}^{2}A_{3} \cdot {}^{3}A_{4} \cdot {}^{4}A_{5} \cdot {}^{5}A_{6}$.

---

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Joint Servo Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF["Reference Joint Angle theta_d"]
    SUM["Comparator Summing Junction"]
    AMP["Servo Amplifier"]
    MOT["Servo Motor Actuator"]
    ARM["Robot Link Physical Plant"]
    ENC["Encoder Position Sensor"]
    FBK["Feedback Signal theta_actual"]

    REF --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error Signal e_t| AMP
    AMP --> MOT
    MOT --> ARM
    ARM --> ENC
    ENC --> FBK
    FBK --> SUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic **unity-feedback single-axis servo loop**. Every joint of an industrial robot has its own independent servo loop running in the controller. Multi-axis synchronization is achieved by the controller issuing synchronized setpoints at sampling rates of typically **1–4 kHz**.

---
