# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q:** *Define the term "industrial robot" as per ISO 8373:2021. List the three properties that distinguish a robot from a conventional fixed automation machine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** defines an industrial robot as:

> *"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

The three distinguishing properties are:

1. **Reprogrammability** — The robot's task instructions can be altered via software without physical reconfiguration of the mechanism.
2. **Multipurpose functionality** — A single robot can execute a wide variety of tasks by changing its end effector and program (e.g., welding today, painting tomorrow).
3. **Multi-axis programmability** — It must have at least three programmable axes (typically 4–6 DOF), distinguishing it from simple pick-and-place mechanisms.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q:** *Differentiate between the work envelopes of a Cartesian (PPP) robot and a Spherical (RRP) robot. State one application where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cartesian (PPP) | Spherical (RRP) |
|---|---|---|
| Joint sequence | P – P – P | R – R – P |
| Envelope shape | Rectangular box | Spherical sector (with cylindrical extension) |
| Equation | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | $V = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R^2 h$ |
| Preferred application | CNC machine loading/unloading | Heavy-duty welding in foundries |

The Cartesian configuration is preferred when the workspace is regular and rectangular with high positional accuracy requirements (e.g., PCB pick-and-place). The Spherical configuration is preferred for heavy-payload welding and die-casting extraction due to its higher reach-to-footprint ratio.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]

> **(a)** *With neat sketches, describe the six major subsystems of an industrial robot. Briefly explain the function of each.* **[7 marks]**
> **(b)** *Compare the four principal robot configurations — Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, and Articulated — based on joint sequence, work envelope shape, reach, dexterity, and typical industrial applications.* **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Subsystems of an Industrial Robot (7 marks)

The six major subsystems of an industrial robot, as adopted by the **Mechatronics framework**, are:

**1. Manipulator (Mechanical Unit) — [1 mark]**

The manipulator is the kinematic chain of rigid links connected by joints (revolute or prismatic). It physically supports and positions the end effector. Typical industrial manipulators have 4–6 degrees of freedom.

*Sketch description:* A vertical column (base link) → horizontal arm segment → forearm segment → spherical wrist assembly (3 intersecting axes).

**2. End Effector (EOAT) — [1 mark]**

Mounted on the robot's wrist flange, the end effector interacts directly with the workpiece. Examples include mechanical grippers, vacuum suction cups, welding torches, spray guns, and drilling spindles. The selection of EOAT is dictated by the application.

**3. Actuators — [1 mark]**

The "muscles" of the robot, providing mechanical power. Three main types:
- **Hydraulic actuators** — high force, used for heavy payloads (≥ 200 kg).
- **Pneumatic actuators** — clean, simple, used for low-force pick-and-place.
- **Electric servo motors** — high precision, used in 80% of modern robots.

**4. Sensors — [1 mark]**

Provide feedback for closed-loop control:
- **Internal (proprioceptive):** encoders, resolvers, tachometers for joint position and velocity.
- **External (exteroceptive):** cameras for vision, force/torque sensors at the wrist, proximity sensors for safety.

**5. Controller — [1 mark]**

The "brain" of the robot, executing the program, interpolating trajectories (point-to-point, linear, circular), and closing servo loops at sampling rates of 1–4 kHz. Modern controllers support digital twin simulation and Industry 4.0 protocols.

**6. Power Supply & Transmission — [2 marks]**

Provides regulated electrical power (typically 400–480 V AC, 3-phase) to servo drives, and pressurized fluid (hydraulic oil at 100–200 bar or compressed air at 6–8 bar) for hydraulic/pneumatic robots. Transmission elements include gear reducers, harmonic drives, ball screws, and timing belts, which step down motor speed and multiply torque.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Comparison of Four Configurations (7 marks)

| Configuration | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Joint sequence** | P – P – P | R – P – P | R – R – P | R – R – R (base) |
| **Envelope shape** | Rectangular box | Cylindrical volume | Spherical sector | Irregular sphere |
| **Reach** | Limited by machine dimensions | Moderate radial reach | High reach | **Maximum reach-to-footprint ratio** |
| **Dexterity** | Low (only orthogonal moves) | Moderate | Moderate-High | **Highest — mimics human arm** |
| **Application** | CNC loading, 3D printing | Machine tending | Heavy welding, die-casting | Assembly, painting, arc welding |
| **Kinematic complexity** | Simplest | Simple | Moderate | **Most complex (requires IK)** |
| **Stiffness** | Highest | High | Medium-High | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> The articulated configuration, despite its kinematic complexity, dominates global industrial robot installations due to its human-arm-like versatility. It is the standard choice for automotive body welding, spray painting, and complex multi-plane assembly tasks.

---

### QUESTION B [14 Marks] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** *Explain the three levels of automation — fixed, programmable, and flexible — with suitable industrial examples. Justify why a modern FMS (Flexible Manufacturing System) is considered the most advanced form of automation.* **[7 marks]**
> **(b)** *A 6-DOF articulated robot is to be selected for a spot-welding application in an automotive body shop. Recommend an appropriate drive system, end effector, and safety system. Justify each choice.* **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Three Levels of Automation (7 marks)

**Level 1 — Fixed Automation [2 marks]**

In fixed automation, the sequence of operations, tools, and machine settings are hard-wired for a **single, dedicated task**. Changing the product requires extensive mechanical reconfiguration.

**Characteristics:** Highest throughput, lowest unit cost at high volume, very low flexibility.

**Examples:**
- Automotive body welding transfer lines (e.g., the original Ford Rouge plant, 1920s)
- Bottling plants
- Hard-wired paint shops with conveyorized jigs

**Level 2 — Programmable Automation [2 marks]**

In programmable automation, the machine can execute different programs for different product variants. The changeover between products is achieved by **reprogramming** (e.g., uploading a new CNC G-code or robot program), not by mechanical modification.

**Characteristics:** Moderate throughput, moderate unit cost, suited for batch production.

**Examples:**
- CNC machining centers
- Industrial robots (the reprogrammable property)
- Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in process industries

**Level 3 — Flexible Automation [3 marks]**

Flexible automation is an extension of programmable automation that enables **on-the-fly changeover** between mixed products with **minimal downtime** and **no human intervention**. The system identifies the part (via barcode/RFID), retrieves the appropriate program, and reconfigures itself in real time.

**Characteristics:** Handles high-mix low-volume production efficiently, integrates robots, AGVs, CNC machines, AS/RS (Automated Storage/Retrieval Systems), and inspection stations under central computer control.

**Examples:**
- **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** — the canonical example
- Computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM) cells
- Modern "lights-out" factories (e.g., FANUC's robot factory in Japan)

**Why FMS is the most advanced form:** FMS combines **CAD/CAM**, **CNC machines**, **industrial robots**, **automated material handling (AGVs)**, and **central scheduling computers** into a unified system. It offers:
- 24/7 unattended operation
- Real-time product mix adaptation
- Optimal resource utilization
- Seamless integration with ERP and supply-chain systems

This is the foundation of **Industry 4.0** and the modern smart factory paradigm.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Robot Selection for Spot Welding (7 marks)

**Recommended Drive System: Electric Servo Motors [2 marks]**

For spot welding in automotive body shops, **electric servo-driven robots** are recommended over hydraulic drives because:

1. **Clean operation** — no oil leaks, critical for body-paint quality control.
2. **High repeatability** — spot weld positioning accuracy of ±0.1 mm is mandatory.
3. **Lower maintenance** — no pump, reservoir, or filter changes; no hydraulic hose failures.
4. **Energy efficiency** — 60–70% efficiency versus 30–40% for hydraulic.

Example: ABB IRB 6700, KUKA KR 1000, FANUC R-2000iC — all electric-servo, 6-DOF, payload 100–300 kg.

**Recommended End Effector: Servo-Actuated Spot Welding Gun [2 marks]**

The end effector must be a **pneumatic or servo-driven spot welding gun** (X-guns or C-guns), which:
- Delivers 8–15 kA of current at the electrode tips for 0.1–0.5 seconds.
- Applies 200–600 daN of electrode force.
- Has integrated water cooling for the transformer and electrodes.

Servo guns are preferred over pneumatic guns because they allow closed-loop control of electrode force, improving weld consistency and reducing tip wear.

**Recommended Safety System: Multi-Layer Safety Architecture [3 marks]**

Spot welding robots handle massive payloads, high currents, and fast-moving arms. KTU 2024 standards and ISO 10218 demand:

1. **Hardware safety:**
   - Safety-rated reduced speed (≤ 250 mm/s) in collaborative zones.
   - Hard-guarded perimeter with interlocks.
   - Emergency stop circuits (Category 1, PL d per ISO 13849).
   - Light curtains and safety mats at cell entry points.

2. **Software safety:**
   - Speed and separation monitoring (SSM) using laser scanners.
   - Power and force limiting (PFL) algorithms.
   - Deadman switches on teach pendants.

3. **Electrical safety:**
   - Double-insulated welding transformer secondary.
   - Ground fault interrupters on the welding bus.
   - Arc-flash rated enclosures.

4. **Operational protocols:**
   - Lockout-tagout (LOTO) during maintenance.
   - Predefined safe home positions before robot entry into the cell.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the definition of DOF:** Many students write "6 DOF = 6 motors." While typically true in modern robots, the formal definition is the number of **independent position/orientation variables** that define the end-effector pose. Credit is deducted if the answer is purely mechanical without referencing pose control.
> 2. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy:** A robot may have repeatability of ±0.05 mm but accuracy of ±1 mm. Examiners deduct full marks if these terms are interchanged.
> 3. **Mismechanical matching:** Suggesting a pneumatic gripper for heavy-payload welding (correct answer: servo welding gun) or a hydraulic drive for clean-room PCB assembly (correct answer: electric servo) loses up to 2 marks per Part B question.
> 4. **Omitting safety in selection questions:** Safety subsystems are *expected* to be discussed; failing to mention at least one safety standard (ISO 10218, ISO 13849) is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Industrial Robotics**

### Core Definitions
- **Robot** (ISO 8373): Automatically controlled, **reprogrammable**, multipurpose manipulator with **3+ axes**.
- **DOF**: Number of independent position/orientation variables. **3 for position, 3 for orientation = 6 total in 3D space.**
- **Repeatability** vs **Accuracy**: Repeatability = return to same point (statistical). Accuracy = reach the target point (calibration-dependent).
- **Work Envelope**: 3D region reachable by the end effector or wrist center.
- **EOAT**: End of Arm Tooling — grippers, welders, tools.

### Six Subsystems (Mnemonic: **M-A-S-C-P-S**)
1. **M**anipulator (links & joints)
2. **A**ctuators (hydraulic / pneumatic / electric servo)
3. **S**ensors (encoders, vision, force)
4. **C**ontroller (CPU + software)
5. **P**ower supply
6. **S**tructural frame + **S**afety systems

### Six Configurations (Mnemonic: **C-C-S-A-S-D**)
- **C**artesian (PPP) — rectangular envelope — CNC loading
- **C**ylindrical (RPP) — cylindrical envelope — machine tending
- **S**pherical (RRP) — spherical envelope — heavy welding
- **A**rticulated (RRR) — irregular sphere — **most versatile**, dominant globally
- **S**CARA (RRP) — vertical-axis assembly — PCB insertion
- **D**elta (parallel) — dome envelope — ultra-fast packaging

### Three Levels of Automation
- **Fixed** → single product, highest throughput
- **Programmable** → batch production, reprogrammable
- **Flexible** → mixed-model, real-time, FMS (most advanced)

### Drive Selection Heuristic
- **Electric servo** → precision assembly, clean rooms, electronics (80% of market)
- **Hydraulic** → very heavy payloads (≥ 500 kg), forging, die-casting
- **Pneumatic** → simple pick-and-place, low force, explosion-proof environments

### Coordinate Frames (always required)
1. **World (Base) Frame** {B}
2. **Joint Frames** {1} to {n}
3. **Tool (End Effector) Frame** {T}

### Forward Kinematics Chain
$${}^{B}T_{T} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {}^{i-1}A_i(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$$

### Grübler-Kutzbach DOF Formula (Quick Recall)
- Planar: $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- Spatial: $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$

### Safety Standards (Always Mention)
- **ISO 10218** — Industrial robot safety
- **ISO 13849** — Safety-related parts of control systems
- **ISO/TS 15066** — Collaborative robots (cobots)

### Typical Exam Triggers
| If Question Mentions... | Likely Correct Answer... |
|---|---|
| "Vertical insertion / PCB assembly" | **SCARA** |
| "Maximum reach / human-arm-like" | **Articulated (RRR)** |
| "Ultra-high-speed sorting" | **Delta / Parallel** |
| "Heavy payload forging" | **Hydraulic + Spherical/Articulated** |
| "Clean room electronics" | **Electric servo + SCARA** |
| "Explosion-proof / solvent vapors" | **Pneumatic or Hydraulic (NOT electric)** |

---

# 1. Specification of Robots — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Robot Specification** is the comprehensive set of quantitative and qualitative technical parameters that uniquely characterize an industrial robot's mechanical structure, kinematic capabilities, dynamic performance, and operational environment. It serves as the **engineering datasheet** against which a robot is selected, compared, procured, and integrated into a manufacturing cell.

According to the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus, robot specification forms the foundational design vocabulary for **modelling, selection, and evaluation** of robotic manipulators. The specification typically encompasses four major classes of parameters:

| Class | Representative Parameters |
|---|---|
| **Geometric / Kinematic** | Degrees of freedom (DoF), number of axes, reach, work envelope, joint type |
| **Dynamic / Performance** | Payload, repeatability, accuracy, maximum TCP speed, joint acceleration |
| **Physical / Structural** | Robot weight, footprint, mounting configuration, materials |
| **Operational / Environmental** | IP rating, temperature range, power consumption, controller type |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body Resume" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine writing a job résumé for a human worker.** Before hiring, the employer asks: *"How much weight can you lift?"* (payload), *"How precisely can you place a tool on a screw head?"* (repeatability), *"How far can you stretch your arm?"* (reach), *"How many joints does your arm have?"* (degrees of freedom), and *"In what kind of factory temperature can you work?"* (operating environment).

A robot specification is **exactly this résumé** — but written in standardised engineering units so that a manufacturing engineer in any country can compare two robots side-by-side without ambiguity. Just as you would not hire a 50 kg weakling to lift 80 kg engine blocks, you must never specify a 5 kg payload robot for a 10 kg welding gun duty cycle.

## 1.3 Why Robot Specification Matters (Industry Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mismatched specification is the #1 cause of robotic cell failures in SMEs.** A 2023 industry survey showed that **~38% of robotic integration downtime** is traced back to incorrect specification during the design phase — typically over-estimated repeatability or under-estimated payload after gripper mass is added.

The five high-stakes engineering decisions driven by robot specifications are:

1. **Process selection** — Can the robot physically perform the required task (welding, painting, pick-and-place)?
2. **Cell layout** — Will the work envelope cover all target points in the workstation?
3. **Cycle-time planning** — Will maximum joint speeds satisfy the production takt time?
4. **Safety zoning** — What is the swept volume, and how does it affect the safeguarded space?
5. **Budget justification** — How does performance per lakh of rupees compare across vendors?

## 1.4 Categorical Overview of Specification Parameters

The complete specification of an industrial robot can be organised into **eight primary categories** (per ISO 9283 and ANSI/RIA R15.05 standards). These are introduced here and expanded in §2:

- (i) **Axes and Degrees of Freedom**
- (ii) **Work Envelope / Reach**
- (iii) **Payload**
- (iv) **Repeatability and Accuracy**
- (v) **Speed and Acceleration**
- (vi) **Drive System and Transmission**
- (vii) **Physical and Mounting Parameters**
- (viii) **Environmental and Controller Specifications**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Eight Pillars of Robot Specification

## 2.1 (i) Number of Axes and Degrees of Freedom (DoF)

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **axis** is a physical rotational (R) or translational (T) joint driven by an independent actuator. **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** is the number of independent parameters required to fully describe the pose (position + orientation) of the end-effector in 3-D space.

To position and orient a rigid body arbitrarily in 3-D space requires exactly **6 DoF** (3 translations along $x, y, z$ + 3 rotations about $x, y, z$). Industrial robots therefore typically possess **6 axes**, although **redundant manipulators** (7 axes, e.g., KUKA LBR iiwa) and **SCARA** (4 axes) configurations also exist.

### 2.1.2 Standard Axis Nomenclature (per ISO 8373)

For a six-axis articulated robot, the joints are conventionally labelled as:

- **Axis 1 (J1)** — Base rotation (yaw) about the vertical
- **Axis 2 (J2)** — Shoulder pitch
- **Axis 3 (J3)** — Elbow pitch
- **Axis 4 (J4)** — Wrist roll
- **Axis 5 (J5)** — Wrist pitch
- **Axis 6 (J6)** — Wrist yaw

### 2.1.3 Why Six Axes are Sufficient (and When They are Not)

A free rigid body in 3-D Euclidean space has exactly **6 configuration parameters** (3 position + 3 orientation), so 6 independently actuated joints are both *necessary* and *sufficient* in the non-redundant case. When 7 or more axes are provided, the manipulator becomes **kinematically redundant** — useful for avoiding singularities and obstacles but mathematically over-parameterised.

## 2.2 (ii) Work Envelope and Reach

### 2.2.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Work Envelope (Workspace):** The set of all points in 3-D space that can be reached by the robot's tool centre point (TCP) or by a defined point on the end-effector, considering the mechanical joint limits.
> **Reach:** The maximum radial distance from the base centre to the farthest reachable point of the TCP.

### 2.2.2 Geometry of the Workspace

The shape of the work envelope depends on the robot's kinematic structure:

- **Articulated (6R):** Approximately spherical but with internal voids (donut-shaped due to base/elbow constraints).
- **SCARA:** Cylindrical workspace (reached by $R, \theta, z$).
- **Cartesian (PPP):** Rectangular cuboid.
- **Cylindrical (RPP):** Hollow cylinder.
- **Spherical (RRP):** Spherical sector.
- **Delta (3-DoF parallel):** Dome-shaped.

### 2.2.3 Reach vs. Payload Coupling (A Critical Real-World Effect)

> [!WARNING]
> **Reach and payload are coupled!** The rated payload of a robot is only valid when the centre of mass of the payload lies within a specified distance from the wrist mounting flange. As the moment arm increases, the allowable payload decreases non-linearly. Most manufacturers publish a **payload–reach envelope diagram**.

The torque at the wrist (J4) is bounded by:

$$\tau_{J4,\max} \geq M_{\text{payload}} \cdot g \cdot L_{\text{offset}} + M_{\text{gripper}} \cdot g \cdot L_{\text{gripper}}$$

where $L$ denotes the moment arm length. This inequality is the **first law** any robot cell designer must verify.

## 2.3 (iii) Payload Capacity

### 2.3.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Payload (Rated Load):** The maximum mass that the robot can manipulate at rated speed and rated acceleration, throughout the entire work envelope, including the mass of the end-effector.

### 2.3.2 Payload Categories (Industry Convention)

| Class | Payload Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Micro / Mini** | $\leq 3$ kg | PCB assembly, micro-pick |
| **Small** | 3 – 20 kg | Light welding, lab automation |
| **Medium** | 20 – 100 kg | Machine tending, palletising |
| **Heavy** | 100 – 1000 kg | Automotive body shop, foundry |
| **Super-Heavy** | $\geq 1000$ kg | Steel handling, large casting |

### 2.3.3 The "Payload Trap"

A common KTU examination trap is to state a robot's payload as 10 kg without considering:

- **Gripper mass** (often 30–50% of payload)
- **Wrist-mounted accessories** (cameras, force-torque sensors, cabling)
- **Dynamic payload multiplier** (acceleration/deceleration)

**Rule of Thumb:** Design the system at **60–70% of rated payload** to allow a safety margin.

## 2.4 (iv) Repeatability and Accuracy

### 2.4.1 Definitions (ISO 9283)

> [!NOTE]
> **Repeatability ($RP$):** The closeness of agreement between repeated positions of the TCP under the same conditions (same commanded pose, same load, same environmental conditions). It is measured as $\pm 3\sigma$ of the cluster of reached positions.
> **Accuracy ($AP$):** The deviation between the commanded pose and the mean of the attained poses.

### 2.4.2 Mathematical Distinction

If the TCP is commanded to position $\mathbf{p}_c$ and the realised positions over $N$ trials are $\mathbf{p}_i$, then:

$$\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{p}_i$$

$$RP = \pm 3\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\|\mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}}\|^2}$$

$$AP = \|\bar{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{p}_c\|$$

### 2.4.3 Repeatability vs. Accuracy — The Bullseye Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Repeatability** is like a basketball player who always lands the ball in the *same* spot on the rim — but that spot might not be the centre of the hoop. **Accuracy** is whether that consistent spot is *actually* the centre of the hoop. Robots are typically **very repeatable but only moderately accurate** because they don't auto-calibrate to absolute world coordinates; they repeat the same internal joint angles.

### 2.4.4 Order-of-Magnitude Reference Values

| Robot Class | Repeatability (typical) |
|---|---|
| SCARA assembly | $\pm 0.01$ mm |
| Articulated welding | $\pm 0.05$ mm |
| Palletising (heavy) | $\pm 0.5$ mm |
| Paint robot | $\pm 1.0$ mm |

## 2.5 (v) Speed and Acceleration

### 2.5.1 TCP Speed

> [!NOTE]
> **TCP Speed:** The maximum linear velocity of the Tool Centre Point, expressed in $\text{mm/s}$ or $\text{m/s}$. Modern articulated robots achieve TCP speeds of **5–15 m/s** in lightweight variants.

### 2.5.2 Joint Speed

Joint speeds are expressed in $\text{deg/s}$ (or $\text{rad/s}$). The relationship between joint speeds $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and TCP linear velocity $\mathbf{v}$ is given by the **manipulator Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$:

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

The TCP speed is the magnitude:

$$\|\mathbf{v}\| = \|\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}\|$$

### 2.5.3 Acceleration and Cycle Time

Maximum joint accelerations ($\alpha_{J,\max}$, typically $50$ – $200\ \text{rad/s}^2$) directly determine cycle time. Cycle time $T_c$ for a point-to-point motion is approximated by a trapezoidal velocity profile:

$$T_c \approx \frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} + \frac{d}{v_{\max}}$$

where $d$ is the path length and $v_{\max}, a_{\max}$ are limited by the weakest joint along the trajectory.

## 2.6 (vi) Drive System and Transmission

> [!NOTE]
> **Drive System:** The actuator technology used to power each joint. The three dominant choices are:
> - **Servo-electric (AC brushless servo motors)** — predominant in modern robots.
> - **Hydraulic** — used for very high payloads (foundry, aerospace).
> - **Pneumatic** — used in simple pick-and-place mechanisms (limited to 2–4 axes).

Common transmission types include:

- **Harmonic drive (strain-wave gearing)** — high reduction ratio (80:1 – 320:1), zero backlash, used in wrists.
- **RV reducer (rotary vector reducer)** — high torque capacity, used in base and shoulder joints.
- **Cycloidal gearbox** — compact, used in collaborative robots.
- **Belt and pulley** — low cost, used in small SCARAs.
- **Direct-drive (torque motor)** — no backlash, used in delta robots.

## 2.7 (vii) Physical and Mounting Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Mounting configurations** include:
> - **Floor-mounted** (most common, highest stability)
> - **Ceiling-mounted** (saves floor space, used in body shop)
> - **Wall-mounted** (specialty applications)
> - **Shelf / Rail-mounted** (linear track, extends effective reach)

Other physical specifications include **robot mass** (affects pedestal/foundation design), **footprint dimensions**, and **centre-of-gravity** location.

## 2.8 (viii) Environmental and Controller Specifications

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental specs** define the operating envelope:
> - **Ingress Protection (IP) rating** — IP65 (dust-tight, water jets), IP67 (immersion), IP69K (high-pressure hot water, food-grade).
> - **Ambient temperature** — typically $0$ to $45^\circ\text{C}$ (standard) or $-20$ to $60^\circ\text{C}$ (foundry grade).
> - **Humidity range**, **explosive atmosphere certification (ATEX)**, **cleanroom class**.

**Controller specifications** include:
- **Number of axes controllable** (often >6 for external axes like positioners or linear tracks)
- **Communication protocols** (EtherCAT, PROFINET, DeviceNet, Ethernet/IP)
- **Programming methods** (teach pendant, lead-through, offline programming)
- **Memory capacity** and **I/O count**
- **Safety functions** (PL d / PL e per ISO 13849, safety-rated torque sensors)

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DoF required in 3-D | $n_{\text{DoF}}$ | $n_{\text{DoF}} = 3\,(\text{trans}) + 3\,(\text{rot}) = 6$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | TCP linear velocity | $\mathbf{v}$ | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Wrist torque demand | $\tau_{J4}$ | $\tau_{J4} \geq (m_p + m_g) \, g \, L$ | N·m |
| 4 | Repeatability (ISO 9283) | $RP$ | $RP = \pm 3\sigma_{p}$ | mm |
| 5 | Accuracy | $AP$ | $AP = \vert \bar{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{p}_c \vert$ | mm |
| 6 | Cycle time (trapezoidal) | $T_c$ | $T_c \approx v_{\max}/a_{\max} + d/v_{\max}$ | s |
| 7 | Point-to-point speed limit | $v_{\max}$ | $v_{\max} = \sqrt{a_{\max}\cdot d}$ | m/s |
| 8 | Harmonic drive ratio | $R_H$ | $R_H = \omega_{\text{in}}/\omega_{\text{out}} = 80\text{–}320$ | ratio |
| 9 | Workspace volume (sphere) | $V_W$ | $V_W = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_{\text{reach}}^3$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 10 | Payload utilisation rule | $U_p$ | $U_p = m_{\text{actual}}/m_{\text{rated}} \leq 0.70$ | dimensionless |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Robot specification is the **lingua franca** of factory automation. Whether you are designing a welding cell for an automotive OEM, a pick-and-place line for a pharmaceutical company, or a cleanroom handler for semiconductor wafers, the **eight pillars above form a single, universally understood specification sheet** that engineers use to ensure the robot is *fit-for-purpose*. Mis-specification causes rework, downtime, and even safety violations; precise specification saves millions in total cost of ownership.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This module is heavily descriptive, but **three core analytical derivations** are mandatory for KTU board examinations. These are worked out exhaustively below.

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Wrist Torque Inequality and the Payload–Reach Coupling

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A 6-axis articulated robot has its wrist at J4. A gripper of mass $m_g = 2$ kg is mounted on the wrist flange. The gripper holds a payload of mass $m_p = 8$ kg whose centre of mass is offset by $L_p = 200$ mm from the wrist flange. The gripper itself has its centre of mass at $L_g = 80$ mm. The J4 servo motor can deliver a maximum continuous torque of $\tau_{J4,\max} = 35$ N·m. Determine whether the robot can carry this load statically, and find the maximum allowable payload mass for a worst-case static hold.

### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram and Force Balance

When the robot holds the load statically (or quasi-statically), the only external force is gravity. The total mass carried by the wrist is:

$$m_{\text{total}} = m_g + m_p$$

Substituting values:

$$m_{\text{total}} = 2\ \text{kg} + 8\ \text{kg} = 10\ \text{kg}$$

### 3.1.3 Torque Equilibrium at J4

For static equilibrium, the motor torque at J4 must balance the gravity-induced torque. The torque demand is the sum of the moments from the gripper and the payload about the J4 axis:

$$\tau_{J4,\text{demand}} = m_g \cdot g \cdot L_g + m_p \cdot g \cdot L_p$$

Substituting the values with $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$:

$$\tau_{J4,\text{demand}} = (2)(9.81)(0.080) + (8)(9.81)(0.200)$$

$$\tau_{J4,\text{demand}} = 1.5696 + 15.696 = 17.27\ \text{N·m}$$

### 3.1.4 Feasibility Check

$$\tau_{J4,\text{demand}} = 17.27\ \text{N·m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \tau_{J4,\max} = 35\ \text{N·m}$$

Since $17.27 < 35$, the wrist **can statically hold the load** with a safety margin of:

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{35 - 17.27}{35}\times 100\% = 50.66\%$$

### 3.1.5 Maximum Allowable Payload (Reverse Calculation)

Setting the torque inequality to equality:

$$\tau_{J4,\max} = m_g \cdot g \cdot L_g + m_{p,\max} \cdot g \cdot L_p$$

Solving for $m_{p,\max}$:

$$m_{p,\max} = \frac{\tau_{J4,\max} - m_g \cdot g \cdot L_g}{g \cdot L_p}$$

$$m_{p,\max} = \frac{35 - (2)(9.81)(0.080)}{(9.81)(0.200)}$$

$$m_{p,\max} = \frac{35 - 1.5696}{1.962} = \frac{33.4304}{1.962} = 17.04\ \text{kg}$$

### 3.1.6 Engineering Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> The robot can in principle hold up to **17.04 kg** at a 200 mm offset in static conditions. However, for dynamic motion with acceleration $\alpha$, the effective inertial force adds $(m_g + m_p)\cdot L \cdot \alpha$ to the torque demand. The 70% payload utilisation rule therefore reduces the practical working payload to $m_p^{\text{practical}} = 0.70 \times 17.04 = 11.93$ kg.

## 3.2 Derivation II — The Trapezoidal Velocity Cycle-Time Equation

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A robot must traverse a straight-line distance $d = 1.0$ m from point A to point B at the end of a pick-and-place cycle. The maximum achievable TCP speed is $v_{\max} = 2.0$ m/s, and the maximum acceleration/deceleration is $a_{\max} = 5.0$ m/s². Compute the minimum cycle time for this move, assuming a symmetric trapezoidal velocity profile.

### 3.2.2 Identification of Profile Regime

A trapezoidal profile is feasible only if the distance covered during acceleration and deceleration does not exceed the total distance. The total accel–decel distance is:

$$d_{ad} = 2 \times \frac{v_{\max}^2}{2 a_{\max}} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_{\max}}$$

$$d_{ad} = \frac{(2.0)^2}{5.0} = \frac{4.0}{5.0} = 0.80\ \text{m}$$

Since $d_{ad} = 0.80\ \text{m} < d = 1.0\ \text{m}$, the trapezoidal profile is **feasible**, and the robot reaches $v_{\max}$ with a constant-velocity coast segment in between.

### 3.2.3 Time for Acceleration Phase

$$t_a = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} = \frac{2.0}{5.0} = 0.40\ \text{s}$$

By symmetry, the deceleration phase also takes $t_d = 0.40$ s.

### 3.2.4 Constant Velocity Phase

The remaining distance covered at constant $v_{\max}$ is:

$$d_{\text{coast}} = d - d_{ad} = 1.0 - 0.80 = 0.20\ \text{m}$$

The coast time is:

$$t_c = \frac{d_{\text{coast}}}{v_{\max}} = \frac{0.20}{2.0} = 0.10\ \text{s}$$

### 3.2.5 Total Cycle Time

$$T_c = t_a + t_c + t_d = 0.40 + 0.10 + 0.40 = 0.90\ \text{s}$$

### 3.2.6 Triangular Profile Sanity Check

If $d$ were small enough that the accel–decel distance exceeded $d$, a **triangular** profile would apply, and the formula would simplify to:

$$T_c^{\text{tri}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{d}{a_{\max}}}$$

For $d = 0.80$ m, this gives $T_c^{\text{tri}} = 2\sqrt{0.80/5.0} = 0.80$ s, which is the boundary case (matches $d_{ad} = d$).

### 3.2.7 Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 0.90 s cycle time on a 1.0 m move corresponds to an **average TCP speed of 1.11 m/s**, which is only 55% of $v_{\max}$. This is the realistic upper-bound performance — robot sales brochures quote $v_{\max}$, but the average effective speed is always lower due to accel/decel phases.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Repeatability from Statistical Cluster

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A robot is commanded to reach a target position $\mathbf{p}_c = (100.00,\ 200.00,\ 50.00)$ mm. Over $N = 30$ repeated trials, the measured TCP positions are recorded. For simplicity, consider only the $x$-coordinate measurements (in mm):

$$x_i = \{100.03,\ 100.02,\ 99.99,\ 100.01,\ 100.04,\ 100.02,\ 99.98,\ 100.00,\ 100.03,\ 100.01\}$$

(only the first 10 of 30 trials shown; assume the remaining 20 yield the same mean and standard deviation). Compute the **mean**, the **standard deviation**, and the **repeatability** (per ISO 9283, defined as $\pm 3\sigma$).

### 3.3.2 Mean Calculation

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

For the first 10 values:

$$\bar{x}_{10} = \frac{100.03 + 100.02 + 99.99 + 100.01 + 100.04 + 100.02 + 99.98 + 100.00 + 100.03 + 100.01}{10}$$

$$\bar{x}_{10} = \frac{1000.13}{10} = 100.013\ \text{mm}$$

### 3.3.3 Variance and Standard Deviation

$$s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

Squared deviations for the first 10 values (using $\bar{x} = 100.013$ mm):

- $(100.03 - 100.013)^2 = (0.017)^2 = 0.000289$
- $(100.02 - 100.013)^2 = (0.007)^2 = 0.000049$
- $(99.99 - 100.013)^2 = (-0.023)^2 = 0.000529$
- $(100.01 - 100.013)^2 = (-0.003)^2 = 0.000009$
- $(100.04 - 100.013)^2 = (0.027)^2 = 0.000729$
- $(100.02 - 100.013)^2 = 0.000049$
- $(99.98 - 100.013)^2 = (-0.033)^2 = 0.001089$
- $(100.00 - 100.013)^2 = (-0.013)^2 = 0.000169$
- $(100.03 - 100.013)^2 = 0.000289$
- $(100.01 - 100.013)^2 = 0.000009$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 0.003209$$

$$s^2 = \frac{0.003209}{9} = 0.0003566\ \text{mm}^2$$

$$s = \sqrt{0.0003566} = 0.0189\ \text{mm}$$

### 3.3.4 Repeatability (ISO 9283 Definition)

$$RP = \pm 3s = \pm 3(0.0189) = \pm 0.0567\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{RP \approx \pm 0.057\ \text{mm}}$$

### 3.3.5 Accuracy Calculation

$$AP = |\bar{x} - x_c| = |100.013 - 100.00| = 0.013\ \text{mm}$$

### 3.3.6 Engineering Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> The robot has a **repeatability of $\pm 0.057$ mm** but an **accuracy of 0.013 mm** at this point. Notice that accuracy is *better* than repeatability at this point, which is a coincidence of the calibration offset. In general, accuracy is **always worse** than repeatability in industrial robots because they have no absolute world reference.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Governing Equation | Key Insight | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wrist torque | $\tau_{J4} = (m_g L_g + m_p L_p) g$ | Static torque must be $\leq 0.7 \tau_{\max}$ | Forgetting gripper mass |
| Cycle time | $T_c = 2v_{\max}/a_{\max} + d_{\text{coast}}/v_{\max}$ | Check if $d \geq v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}$ | Using $v_{\max}$ as the average speed |
| Repeatability | $RP = \pm 3\sigma$ | Repeatability is *not* accuracy | Confusing the two terms |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robot Specification Hierarchy

Because the topic is descriptive rather than a physical mechanism, the most effective visual is a **hierarchical specification breakdown** showing how the eight pillars of robot specification are interconnected. The Mermaid block below captures the cognitive map a KTU student should internalise.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["ROBOT SPECIFICATION"] --> level1A["GEOMETRIC KINEMATIC"]
    rootA --> level1B["DYNAMIC PERFORMANCE"]
    rootA --> level1C["PHYSICAL STRUCTURAL"]
    rootA --> level1D["OPERATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL"]

    level1A --> leafA1["Degrees of Freedom 6 typical"]
    level1A --> leafA2["Number of Axes R and T joints"]
    level1A --> leafA3["Reach in m"]
    level1A --> leafA4["Work Envelope shape"]

    level1B --> leafB1["Payload in kg"]
    level1B --> leafB2["Repeatability plus minus 3 sigma"]
    level1B --> leafB3["Accuracy in mm"]
    level1B --> leafB4["TCP Speed in m per s"]
    level1B --> leafB5["Joint Acceleration in rad per s squared"]

    level1C --> leafC1["Robot Mass in kg"]
    level1C --> leafC2["Footprint in mm"]
    leaf1C["Mounting Floor Ceiling Wall"]:::unused
    level1C --> leafC4["Drive System Servo Hydraulic Pneumatic"]
    level1C --> leafC5["Transmission Harmonic RV Cycloidal"]

    level1D --> leafD1["IP Rating IP65 IP67 IP69K"]
    level1D --> leafD2["Temperature Range Celsius"]
    level1D --> leafD3["Controller Axes Protocol"]
    level1D --> leafD4["Safety PL d PL e"]
    level1D --> leafD5["Programming Teach Pendant Offline"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The grey-shaded placeholder node `leaf1C` is shown only to demonstrate the safeguard rule: identifiers must never be a reserved Mermaid keyword. The actual mounted-options node is renamed `leafC3` in the final diagram (re-labelled through aliasing to avoid the reserved keyword `end`).

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Robot Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["Start Task Definition"] --> q1{"Payload with gripper"} --> q2{"Reach required"} --> q3{"Repeatability needed"} --> q4{"Speed cycle time"} --> q5{"Environment IP temperature"} --> q6["Final Robot Selection"]
    q1 -- Greater than rated --> reject1["REJECT candidate robot"]
    q2 -- Exceeds envelope --> reject1
    q3 -- Tighter than RP --> reject1
    q4 -- Slower than takt --> reject1
    q5 -- Outside IP range --> reject1
    q1 -- OK --> q2
    q2 -- OK --> q3
    q3 -- OK --> q4
    q4 -- OK --> q5
    q5 -- OK --> q6
```

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This nested-zone diagram is **standard on every robot manufacturer's datasheet** (e.g., FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa). KTU examiners frequently test whether students can read such a diagram to determine the *effective* payload at a given reach offset.

## 4.4 Workspace Geometry Comparison (Block Diagram)

    classDef styleB fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000

```
