<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define Repeatability and Accuracy of a Robot. State the Standard Used. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Repeatability (RP)** is the closeness of agreement among repeated measurements of the TCP for the same commanded pose, expressed as $\pm 3\sigma$ per **ISO 9283**.
>
> **Accuracy (AP)** is the deviation between the commanded pose and the mean of the attained poses.
>
> Mathematically:
>
> $$RP = \pm 3\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\|\mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}}\|^2}, \quad AP = \|\bar{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{p}_c\|$$
>
> **Distinction:** Industrial robots are highly repeatable but only moderately accurate.

---

### Question 2: List Any Six Important Specifications of an Industrial Robot. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. Number of axes / degrees of freedom
2. Payload (kg)
3. Reach (m) / Work envelope
4. Repeatability ($\pm$ mm)
5. Maximum TCP speed (m/s)
6. Mounting configuration (floor, ceiling, wall)
7. Drive system (servo / hydraulic / pneumatic)
8. IP rating / environmental protection

*(Any six well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **eight major specification parameters** of an industrial robot. Discuss how the payload–reach coupling affects robot selection. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 6-axis articulated robot has a wrist torque limit of $\tau_{J4,\max} = 40$ N·m. A gripper of mass 3 kg is mounted with its centre of mass at 100 mm from the wrist flange, and the maximum payload offset is 250 mm. Calculate (i) the static torque demand for a 12 kg payload, (ii) the maximum allowable payload, and (iii) the practical working payload using the 70% utilisation rule. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

The **eight major specification parameters** of an industrial robot are:

1. **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** — Typically 6 for full pose control. Redundant variants (7 DoF) used in collaborative robots.
2. **Work Envelope / Reach** — 3-D region accessible to the TCP. Shape depends on kinematic structure (sphere, cylinder, cuboid, dome).
3. **Payload** — Mass of gripper + workpiece the wrist can carry at rated performance.
4. **Repeatability** — $\pm 3\sigma$ of repeated TCP positions, per **ISO 9283**.
5. **Accuracy** — Deviation between commanded and mean attained pose.
6. **Speed / Acceleration** — TCP linear speed (m/s) and joint angular acceleration (rad/s²).
7. **Drive System** — Servo-electric, hydraulic, or pneumatic actuation.
8. **Environmental Specification** — IP rating, operating temperature, controller protocols, safety integrity level.

**Payload–Reach Coupling:**

The wrist torque demand is $\tau_{J4} = (m_g L_g + m_p L_p) g$. As the moment arm $L_p$ increases, the allowable payload decreases. Manufacturer datasheets present a **nested envelope diagram** showing concentric zones of decreasing payload. Ignoring this coupling leads to motor overload, encoder faults, and warranty voidance.

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:** $\tau_{J4,\max} = 40$ N·m, $m_g = 3$ kg, $L_g = 100$ mm = $0.100$ m, $L_p = 250$ mm = $0.250$ m, $m_p = 12$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Static torque demand for 12 kg payload:**

$$\tau_{J4,\text{demand}} = m_g \cdot g \cdot L_g + m_p \cdot g \cdot L_p$$

$$\tau_{J4,\text{demand}} = (3)(9.81)(0.100) + (12)(9.81)(0.250)$$

$$\tau_{J4,\text{demand}} = 2.943 + 29.430 = 32.373\ \text{N·m}$$

Since $32.373 < 40$, the wrist can statically hold the load. Margin = $\frac{40 - 32.373}{40} = 19.07\%$.

**(ii) Maximum allowable payload (static):**

$$m_{p,\max} = \frac{\tau_{J4,\max} - m_g g L_g}{g L_p} = \frac{40 - (3)(9.81)(0.100)}{(9.81)(0.250)}$$

$$m_{p,\max} = \frac{40 - 2.943}{2.4525} = \frac{37.057}{2.4525} = 15.11\ \text{kg}$$

**(iii) Practical working payload (70% rule):**

$$m_p^{\text{practical}} = 0.70 \times 15.11 = 10.58\ \text{kg}$$

Note: The rated payload in the datasheet would therefore be quoted as $\approx 10$ kg, not 15.11 kg, to leave a safety margin for dynamic loads.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **work envelope geometries** of the five major industrial robot configurations. State one typical application for each. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A pick-and-place robot must move a payload across a distance of $d = 0.6$ m. The maximum TCP speed is $v_{\max} = 1.5$ m/s and the maximum acceleration is $a_{\max} = 4.0$ m/s². Determine (i) whether a trapezoidal velocity profile is feasible, (ii) the time spent in acceleration, constant velocity, and deceleration phases, and (iii) the total cycle time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

| Configuration | Joints | Work Envelope Geometry | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Articulated (6R)** | 6 rotational | Approximately spherical (donut-shaped) with internal voids | Automotive welding, painting |
| **SCARA (RRP)** | 2 R + 1 P (vertical) | Cylindrical (flat disc swept vertically) | PCB assembly, pick-and-place |
| **Cartesian (PPP)** | 3 prismatic | Rectangular cuboid | CNC loading, 3-D printing |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | 1 R + 2 P | Hollow cylinder | Foundry die casting, coating |
| **Spherical (RRP)** | 2 R + 1 P | Spherical sector (ball segment) | Heavy forging, injection moulding |
| **Delta (3-DoF parallel)** | 3 parallelogram + 1 P | Dome above base | High-speed food packaging |

> Each entry earns ~1 mark in a 7-mark question (5 rows + intro + conclusion).

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given:** $d = 0.6$ m, $v_{\max} = 1.5$ m/s, $a_{\max} = 4.0$ m/s².

**(i) Trapezoidal feasibility check:**

$$d_{ad} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_{\max}} = \frac{(1.5)^2}{4.0} = \frac{2.25}{4.0} = 0.5625\ \text{m}$$

Since $d_{ad} = 0.5625\ \text{m} < d = 0.6\ \text{m}$, the trapezoidal profile is **feasible** with a small constant-velocity coast.

**(ii) Phase times:**

Acceleration time:

$$t_a = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} = \frac{1.5}{4.0} = 0.375\ \text{s}$$

Deceleration time (by symmetry):

$$t_d = 0.375\ \text{s}$$

Coast distance:

$$d_{\text{coast}} = d - d_{ad} = 0.6 - 0.5625 = 0.0375\ \text{m}$$

Coast time:

$$t_c = \frac{d_{\text{coast}}}{v_{\max}} = \frac{0.0375}{1.5} = 0.025\ \text{s}$$

**(iii) Total cycle time:**

$$T_c = t_a + t_c + t_d = 0.375 + 0.025 + 0.375 = 0.775\ \text{s}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy** — These are mathematically *different* quantities. Repeatability is $\pm 3\sigma$ of the cluster; accuracy is the offset between the mean and the commanded point. Students who interchange these lose **2–3 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the gripper mass in payload calculations** — A 6 kg gripper on a 10 kg rated robot leaves only 4 kg of usable payload. The wrist torque inequality *must* include $m_g L_g$.
> 3. **Quoting $v_{\max}$ as the average speed** — Average effective TCP speed is always *less* than $v_{\max}$ because of accel/decel phases. The trapezoidal cycle-time formula must be applied correctly.
> 4. **Using the wrong ISO standard reference** — Always cite **ISO 9283** for repeatability/accuracy and **ISO 8373** for the general vocabulary of industrial robots.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Specification of Robots**
>
> - **Robot Specification** = the technical datasheet (kinematic + dynamic + physical + environmental parameters).
> - **DoF** for full pose control in 3-D = **6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
> - **Work Envelope** is the 3-D region the TCP can reach; shape depends on configuration.
> - **Payload** must include the gripper mass and is coupled to reach via the **wrist torque inequality** $\tau_{J4} \leq 0.7\,\tau_{J4,\max}$.
> - **Repeatability (RP)** per ISO 9283 is $\pm 3\sigma$ of repeated TCP positions; **Accuracy (AP)** is $|\bar{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{p}_c|$.
> - Robots are highly **repeatable** but only moderately **accurate** — remember the bullseye analogy.
> - **TCP speed** is governed by the Jacobian: $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
> - **Cycle time** follows a **trapezoidal velocity profile** with the formula $T_c = 2v_{\max}/a_{\max} + d_{\text{coast}}/v_{\max}$ when $d \geq v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}$.
> - **Drive systems:** Servo-electric (modern standard), hydraulic (heavy-duty), pneumatic (low-DoF simple tasks).
> - **Transmissions:** Harmonic drives (wrists), RV reducers (base/shoulder), cycloidal (cobot), direct-drive (delta).
> - **Mounting:** Floor (most stable), ceiling, wall, rail (linear track).
> - **Environmental specs:** IP rating (IP65, IP67, IP69K), temperature, cleanroom class, ATEX.
> - **Controller specs:** Number of axes, protocols (EtherCAT, PROFINET), safety (PL d / PL e), programming (teach pendant / offline).
> - **Safety utilisation rule:** Design at **$\leq 70\%$** of rated payload for dynamic motion.
> - **Reference standards:** **ISO 9283** (performance), **ISO 8373** (vocabulary), **ISO 13849** (safety), **ANSI/RIA R15.05** (US).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Resolution # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of Mechatronics, **Resolution** is formally defined as the **smallest measurable increment or the smallest change in the input (measurand) that an instrument, sensor, or transducer can reliably detect, distinguish, and display**. It represents the **finest granularity** at which a measurement system can resolve a change in the physical quantity being measured. Mathematically, Resolution is the **smallest difference between two measured values that can be meaningfully distinguished by the measuring device**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBRT601 - Mechatronics):** > *"Resolution is the smallest change in the input signal that produces a detectable change in the output of the instrument. It is a measure of the fineness to which an instrument's reading can be scaled and represents the degree to which the instrument can discriminate between nearly equal values."* ## 1.2 Resolution vs. Related Performance Parameters It is critical for a KTU student to **not confuse Resolution** with similar performance metrics. Below is a clarifying comparison: | Parameter | Definition | Distinguishing Feature | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in input | Defines the *fineness* of measurement | | **Sensitivity** | Ratio of output change to input change | Defines *amplification* of the system | | **Accuracy** | Closeness to the true value | Defines *correctness* of measurement | | **Precision** | Repeatability of measurement | Defines *consistency* of measurement | | **Least Count** | Smallest scale division (analog) | Numerical *reading* limit | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A highly accurate instrument may still have poor resolution (e.g., a wooden ruler is accurate to ±1 mm but cannot resolve fractions of a millimeter). Conversely, a high-resolution instrument may have low accuracy. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding Imagine you are measuring the length of a wooden table using two different rulers: - **Ruler A** has markings only every **1 centimeter (cm)**. You can say the table is "between 120 cm and 121 cm", but you **cannot detect** whether it is 120.3 cm or 120.7 cm. - **Ruler B** has markings every **1 millimeter (mm)**. You can read "120.4 cm" precisely. In this scenario: - **Ruler A's Resolution = 1 cm** - **Ruler B's Resolution = 1 mm** **Ruler B is said to have higher resolution** because it can discriminate smaller changes in length. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy for KTU:** Think of your smartphone's digital weighing scale. If the scale shows 60.0, 60.1, 60.2... the resolution is **0.1 kg**. If it suddenly jumps from 60.0 to 60.5 in one step, the resolution is **0.5 kg** (a worse resolution). ## 1.4 Types of Resolution in Mechatronic Systems ### 1.4.1 Static Resolution The resolution of the system when the measurand is **stationary (DC condition)**. It is limited by: - Friction in mechanical linkages - Quantization noise in digital systems - Static threshold of the transducer ### 1.4.2 Dynamic Resolution The resolution of the system when the measurand is **changing with time (AC condition)**. It is typically **worse** than static resolution due to: - Inertia of moving parts - Bandwidth limitations - Time constants of the transducer ### 1.4.3 Spatial Resolution (Sensor-Specific) In imaging, position, or array sensors, it refers to the **minimum distance** between two points that can be distinguished (e.g., pixels in a camera, encoder line spacing). ## 1.5 Units and Standard Metrics The units of resolution are **the same as the units of the measurand**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Bold Highlight:** The resolution of a **digital system** is governed by the bit-depth $n$ of the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) and the reference/full-scale range. A **standard KTU highlight**: For an $n$-bit system, the number of distinct levels is **$2^n$**, and the resolution is **Full Scale / ($2^n$ - 1)**. --- **Key Constants/Metrics to Remember:** - **Bit-depth range:** 8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit (most common in mechatronic ADCs) - **Standard reference voltage:** Typically **5 V** or **3.3 V** in microcontroller-based systems - **Typical encoder resolution range:** 100 to 10,000 pulses/revolution (PPR) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The "Why" Behind Resolution Every measuring instrument — whether analog (deflection-type) or digital (ADC-based) — has a fundamental **discrimination limit**. This limit arises from three physical/mathematical sources: 1. **Quantum Mechanical Limit:** At the atomic scale, measurands exist in discrete packets. 2. **Sensor Fabrication Limit:** The smallest physical feature that can be manufactured (e.g., encoder grating pitch). 3. **Signal Conditioning Limit:** Noise floor of amplifiers, quantization noise of ADCs, hysteresis of mechanical linkages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Engineering Principle:** No physical measurement system can have **infinite resolution**. There is always a fundamental lower limit below which the output cannot be distinguished from noise, friction, or quantization error. ## 2.2 Resolution in Analog Instruments For an analog instrument (e.g., a moving-coil voltmeter, Bourdon-tube pressure gauge): $$\text{Resolution} = \text{Least Count of the scale} = \frac{\text{Full Scale Value (FSV)}}{\text{Number of Divisions on the scale}}$$ ### Worked Logic (Why?): - The pointer physically rests between two marked divisions. - Beyond the smallest marked division, the human eye (or the pointer's inertia) cannot reliably distinguish the reading. - Hence, the **smallest readable increment** is the resolution. ## 2.3 Resolution in Digital Instruments (ADC) For a digital instrument with an **$n$-bit ADC**: $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n} \quad \text{(Voltage per Least Significant Bit)}$$ Or, expressed in terms of Full-Scale Range (FSR): $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n - 1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on $2^n$ vs $2^n - 1$:** KTU examiners typically use **$2^n$** when computing the *size of one LSB* (quantization step), and **$2^n - 1$** when computing the *resolution relative to the maximum representable value*. Be careful — this is a frequent **valuation trap**. ## 2.4 Resolution of Optical/Incremental Encoders An optical encoder uses a rotating disk with **$N$ slots/lines per revolution**. $$\text{Encoder Resolution} = \frac{360°}{N} \quad \text{(Angular resolution)}$$ With **quadrature decoding** (multiplying by 4): $$\text{Encoder Resolution} = \frac{360°}{4N} \quad \text{(After quadrature)}$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL TABLE — Memorize This for KTU Board Exams** | # | System / Context | Resolution Formula | Units | Key Variable | |:-:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Analog scale (pointer) | $R = \dfrac{\text{FSV}}{N_{\text{div}}}$ | Same as measurand | $N_{\text{div}}$ = number of divisions | | 2 | Digital ADC (n-bit) | $R = \dfrac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n}$ | Volts (per LSB) | $n$ = number of bits | | 3 | ADC relative to FSR | $R = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$ | % or V/LSB | $2^n - 1$ = max counts | | 4 | Potentiometric sensor | $R = \dfrac{\text{Track length}}{N_{\text{turns}}}$ | Length / turn | $N_{\text{turns}}$ = wire turns | | 5 | Optical encoder | $R_{\theta} = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Degrees / pulse | $N$ = lines on disk | | 6 | Encoder (quadrature ×4) | $R_{\theta} = \dfrac{360°}{4N}$ | Degrees / count | — | | 7 | Strain gauge (theoretical) | $R = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{range}}}{2^n}$ | Strain / LSB | $\varepsilon$ = strain | | 8 | LVDT | $R \approx \text{Net displacement} / N$ | mm | $N$ = windings per mm | | 9 | Resolution in % | $R_{\%} = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$ | Percent | $n$ = bits | | 10 | Resolution in dB | $R_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB | $n$ = bits (SNR) | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application of Resolution | |:---|:---| | **CNC Machining** | Encoder resolution determines smallest axis movement (~1 µm achievable) | | **Robotics** | Joint encoders with 16-bit resolution (~0.005°) for precision arms | | **Medical Devices** | MRI pixel resolution, infusion pump flow resolution (µL/hr) | | **Automotive (ABS)** | Wheel speed encoder resolution (typically 48 pulses/rev) | | **Aerospace (IMU)** | Gyroscope resolution determines attitude hold accuracy | | **Process Control** | ADC resolution in PLCs (typically 12–16 bit) | > [!NOTE] > **Why resolution matters in Mechatronics:** Resolution sets the **physical limit of closed-loop control precision**. A 10-bit position controller cannot achieve better than 0.1% positioning accuracy, regardless of control algorithm sophistication. ## 2.7 Resolution — The "Step Size" Hierarchy Every measurement involves three hierarchical levels: $$\text{Accuracy} \;\geq\; \text{Precision} \;\geq\; \text{Resolution}$$ - **Accuracy:** Correctness of absolute value - **Precision:** Repeatability of readings - **Resolution:** Finest readable increment A **KTU frequently tested concept**: An instrument can be highly precise (repeatable) and high-resolution but still be inaccurate (systematic error present). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Resolution of an $n$-bit ADC from First Principles ### 3.1.1 Starting Premise An **$n$-bit ADC** represents an analog input voltage as a digital number using **$n$ binary bits**. The digital output can therefore take on $2^n$ distinct binary codes. **Step 1 — Counting the discrete levels:** The number of discrete digital levels representable by $n$ bits is: $$L = 2^n$$ For example: - 8-bit ADC → $L = 2^8 = 256$ levels - 10-bit ADC → $L = 2^{10} = 1024$ levels - 12-bit ADC → $L = 2^{12} = 4096$ levels **Step 2 — Defining the Quantization Step:** The ADC spans a reference voltage range, typically from $0$ V to $V_{\text{REF}}$. The input range is divided into $2^n$ equal intervals. The voltage corresponding to **one Least Significant Bit (1 LSB)** is the size of each interval: $$\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{REF}} - 0}{2^n} = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n}$$ **Step 3 — Defining Resolution as the Smallest Detectable Voltage Change:** Resolution is defined as the **smallest change in input voltage** that produces a **change of 1 LSB** in the digital output. Therefore: $$\boxed{R = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n}}$$ **Step 4 — Expressing in Terms of Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The **Full-Scale Range** of a unipolar ADC is $V_{\text{REF}}$ (from 0 to $V_{\text{REF}}$). The maximum digital code is $2^n - 1$ (not $2^n$, because the count starts from 0). Hence, the resolution per count relative to the maximum code is: $$R = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$$ **Step 5 — Percentage Resolution:** The percentage resolution of an $n$-bit system is: $$R_{\%} = \frac{R}{\text{FSR}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100 \approx \frac{100}{2^n}\,\%$$ **Step 6 — Resolution in Decibels (Signal-to-Noise Ratio context):** For a theoretical ideal ADC, the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB is: $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 \;\text{dB}$$ This represents the **effective dynamic range** of the ADC, which is directly related to resolution. > [!TIP] > **The famous "6.02 dB per bit" rule** comes from the logarithm relationship: > $$\text{SQNR} = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) = 20n \log_{10}(2) = 6.02n$$ > The extra $+1.76$ dB comes from the quantization noise being a triangular wave, not uniform, adding a correction term. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Resolution of an Optical Rotary Encoder ### 3.2.1 Physical Setup An incremental optical encoder consists of a disk with $N$ alternating transparent and opaque lines. A light source and photodetector generate one pulse per line crossing. **Step 1 — Full Revolution in Degrees:** A complete rotation of the disk corresponds to: $$\theta_{\text{full}} = 360°$$ **Step 2 — Pulses per Revolution:** For a disk with $N$ lines, the encoder produces $N$ pulses per revolution: $$N_{\text{pulses}} = N$$ **Step 3 — Angular Resolution:** The smallest angular displacement that produces one pulse (and therefore the smallest detectable change) is: $$\boxed{R_{\theta} = \frac{360°}{N}}$$ **Step 4 — Quadrature Decoding (×4 Multiplication):** In quadrature decoding, both the **rising and falling edges** of **two channels (A and B)** shifted by 90° are counted. This **multiplies the effective resolution by 4**: $$\boxed{R_{\theta, \text{quad}} = \frac{360°}{4N}}$$ **Step 5 — Resolution in Radians:** $$R_{\theta, \text{quad}} = \frac{2\pi}{4N} = \frac{\pi}{2N} \;\text{radians}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples ### Example 1: Resolution of a 12-bit ADC **Given:** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{\text{REF}} = 5.0$ V. **Find:** (a) Resolution in volts. (b) Percentage resolution. (c) SQNR in dB. **Solution (a):** $$R = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{2^{12}} = \frac{5.0}{4096} = 1.22 \;\text{mV}$$ **Solution (b):** $$R_{\%} = \frac{100}{2^{12}} = \frac{100}{4096} = 0.0244\,\%$$ **Solution (c):** $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\;\text{dB}$$ --- ### Example 2: Encoder Resolution for Servo Motor **Given:** A servo motor uses an encoder with $N = 2500$ lines/rev, with quadrature decoding. **Find:** (a) Resolution in degrees. (b) Resolution in arc-minutes. (c) Resolution in radians. **Solution (a):** $$R_{\theta} = \frac{360°}{4N} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 2500} = \frac{360°}{10000} = 0.036°$$ **Solution (b):** $$R_{\theta} = 0.036° \times 60 = 2.16\;\text{arc-minutes}$$ **Solution (c):** $$R_{\theta} = \frac{2\pi}{10000} = 6.283 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{rad}$$ --- ### Example 3: Resolution of a Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge **Given:** A Bourdon pressure gauge has range 0–10 bar, scale length 270°, and 270 divisions. **Find:** Resolution in bar. **Solution:** $$R = \frac{\text{FSV}}{N_{\text{div}}} = \frac{10\;\text{bar}}{270} = 0.037\;\text{bar} \approx 0.04\;\text{bar}$$ --- ### Example 4: Percentage Resolution Comparison | System | Bits/Lines | Percentage Resolution | |:---|:---:|:---:| | 8-bit ADC | 8 | 0.391% | | 10-bit ADC | 10 | 0.0977% | | 12-bit ADC | 12 | 0.0244% | | 16-bit ADC | 16 | 0.00153% | | Encoder (2500 ppr, quad) | 10000 | 0.01% | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Each additional bit **halves** the percentage resolution. This is the **exponential improvement** characteristic of digital systems — the central reason mechatronics favors digital sensing. --- ## 3.4 Resolution vs. Sensitivity — The Mathematical Link For a linear transducer: $$\text{Sensitivity} \;(S) = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{\text{change in output}}{\text{change in input}}$$ If the output indicator has resolution $R_{\text{out}}$, then the **input-side resolution** is: $$R_{\text{in}} = \frac{R_{\text{out}}}{S}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Higher sensitivity does **NOT** automatically imply higher resolution. It merely *amplifies* the measurand. Resolution depends on the **fineness of the output reading mechanism**. --- ## 3.5 Effect of Noise on Achievable Resolution In practice, resolution is bounded by the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**: $$R_{\text{achievable}} \geq \frac{V_{\text{noise, RMS}}}{S}$$ For an ADC, the effective number of bits (ENOB) considering noise is: $$\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNR} - 1.76}{6.02}$$ > [!TIP] > A 16-bit ADC with a poor front-end amplifier may effectively behave as a 12-bit ADC (ENOB = 12) because noise masks the lowest 4 bits. This is **resolution loss in practice**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map: Resolution in the Mechatronic Measurement Chain The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **resolution-limiting stages** in a typical mechatronic measurement system: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mechatronic System Input] --> B[Physical Measurand
Temperature, Pressure, Position] B --> C[Sensor / Transducer] C --> D[Signal Conditioner
Amplifier and Filter] D --> E[ADC and Quantizer] E --> F[Digital Output Display] F --> G[Controller and Actuator] H[Resolution Limit Sources] --> C H --> D H --> E H --> F I[Quantization Noise] --> E J[Sensor Fabrication Pitch] --> C K[Amplifier Noise Floor] --> D L[Display Pixel or Division] --> F H --> I H --> J H --> K H --> L ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each block in the measurement chain contributes its own resolution limit. The **overall system resolution** is dominated by the **worst (largest step) link** in the chain. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Resolution Computation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Start] --> B[Identify Measurand Type] B --> C{Analog or Digital System?} C -->|Analog| D[Determine Full Scale Value] D --> E[Count Number of Divisions N] E --> F[Apply R = FSV / N] C -->|Digital| G[Identify Bit Depth n] G --> H[Identify Reference Voltage Vref] H --> I[Apply R = Vref / 2n] I --> J[Compute Percentage Resolution] F --> K[Final Resolution Value] J --> K K --> L[End] ``` --- ## 4.3 Comparison Diagram: Resolution Hierarchy in Sensors ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Measurement System Performance] --> B[Static Performance] A --> C[Dynamic Performance] B --> D[Resolution] B --> E[Accuracy] B --> F[Precision] B --> G[Sensitivity] B --> H[Linearity] B --> I[Drift] B --> J[Dead Zone] C --> K[Dynamic Resolution] C --> L[Bandwidth] C --> M[Response Time] C --> N[Settling Time] D --> D1[Smallest Detectable Change] E --> E1[Closeness to True Value] F --> F1[Repeatability of Readings] G --> G1[Output per Unit Input] H --> H1[Linearity of Transfer Function] ``` --- ## 4.4 Bit-Depth vs. Resolution Visualization (Block Architecture) The following flow illustrates how a **12-bit ADC** partitions a 5 V reference range into 4096 discrete levels: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["Analog Input Range 0V to 5V"] I0[0.000 V] --> I1[0.00122 V] I1 --> I2[0.00244 V] I2 --> I3[0.00366 V] I3 --> I4[0.00488 V] I4 --> I5[0.00610 V] I5 --> I6[... continues to ...] I6 --> I7[4.99878 V] I7 --> I8[5.00000 V] end subgraph Resolution["Resolution Step"] R1[1 LSB = Vref / 2n] R2[1 LSB = 5 / 4096] R3[1 LSB = 1.22 mV] end Input --> Resolution Resolution --> Output["Digital Output Code
000000000000 to 111111111111"] ``` --- ## 4.5 Encoder Resolution — Quadrature Decoding Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Encoder Disk
N = 2500 lines] --> B[Channel A Pulses] A --> C[Channel B Pulses
90 deg Phase Shift] B --> D[Rising Edge Counter] B --> E[Falling Edge Counter] C --> F[Direction Decoder] C --> G[Rising Edge Counter] C --> H[Falling Edge Counter] D --> I[Summing Unit] E --> I G --> I H --> I F --> I I --> J[Total Counts per Revolution
N effective = 4N] J --> K[Final Resolution
R theta = 360 deg / 4N] ``` --- ## 4.6 Resolution Degradation Chain (Why Resolution Degrades in Practice) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ideal Resolution] --> B[Add Sensor Nonlinearity] B --> C[Add Quantization Noise] C --> D[Add Thermal Drift] D --> E[Add Electromagnetic Interference] E --> F[Add Mechanical Hysteresis] F --> G[Effective Achievable Resolution] G --> H[Result: Resolution is 2 to 4 bits worse than nominal] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Resolution in the context of measurement systems. How does it differ from Sensitivity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Resolution** is defined as the **smallest change in the input (measurand) that can be detected and displayed by an instrument**. It represents the *finest granularity* of measurement. **Difference from Sensitivity:** | Aspect | Resolution | Sensitivity | |:---|:---|:---| | Definition | Smallest detectable input change | Ratio of output change to input change | | Units | Same as measurand | Output units per input unit | | Meaning | Fineness of measurement | Amplification factor | | Example | 0.1 °C | 10 mV/°C | > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Marks are awarded for (i) correct definition, (ii) clear contrast table, and (iii) one illustrative example. Skipping the example loses ½ to 1 mark. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **A pressure gauge has a range of 0–100 kPa with 200 divisions on its scale. Calculate its resolution. What will be the percentage resolution?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Given:** Full Scale Value (FSV) = 100 kPa, Number of divisions = 200 **Step 1 — Compute Resolution:** $$R = \frac{\text{FSV}}{N_{\text{div}}} = \frac{100\;\text{kPa}}{200} = 0.5\;\text{kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Percentage Resolution:** $$R_{\%} = \frac{R}{\text{FSV}} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{100} \times 100 = 0.5\,\%$$ **Final Answer:** Resolution = **0.5 kPa**; Percentage Resolution = **0.5%**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks** with **internal choice**. You must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**. Each option has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each. --- ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — ADC and Sensor Resolution **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Define Resolution of a digital measurement system. An 8-bit ADC is used in a data acquisition system with a reference voltage of 5 V. **Find:** (i) The resolution in volts (per LSB) (ii) The percentage resolution (iii) The Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various sources that limit the resolution of a measurement system in practice. Compare the static and dynamic resolution of a measurement system. **(7 marks)** --- ### 📋 Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (1 mark):** The resolution of a digital measurement system is the **smallest change in the input analog signal that produces a change of 1 LSB (Least Significant Bit)** in the digital output. For an $n$-bit ADC, the resolution is: $$R = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n}$$ **Given:** $n = 8$ bits, $V_{\text{REF}} = 5$ V **(i) Resolution in volts (2 marks):** $$R = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953\;\text{V} \approx 19.53\;\text{mV}$$ **(ii) Percentage Resolution (2 marks):** $$R_{\%} = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{100}{256} = 0.3906\,\%$$ **(iii) SQNR in dB (2 marks):** $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92\;\text{dB}$$ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Block diagram of resolution-limiting factors (4 marks):** A mechatronic measurement system has the following stages, each contributing to resolution loss: ``` [Physical Measurand] → [Sensor] → [Signal Conditioner] → [ADC] → [Display] ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ Friction Pitch Limit Amplifier Quantization Pixel Hysteresis Drift Noise Floor Noise Resolution ``` **Sources limiting resolution:** 1. **Sensor-level limits:** Mechanical friction, hysteresis, manufacturing pitch (e.g., encoder grating). 2. **Signal conditioner limits:** Amplifier noise floor, thermal (Johnson) noise, EMI pickup. 3. **ADC limits:** Quantization noise (inherent to bit-depth), aperture uncertainty, integral non-linearity. 4. **Display/reading limits:** Pixel size, human reading error, least count of indicator. **Static vs. Dynamic Resolution (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Resolution | Dynamic Resolution | |:---|:---|:---| | **Condition** | Measurand constant (DC) | Measurand varying (AC) | | **Magnitude** | Generally better (finer) | Generally worse (coarser) | | **Limiting factors** | Friction, quantization, hysteresis | Inertia, bandwidth, time constant | | **Typical value** | Full bit-depth achievable | Reduced (effective bits drop) | | **Improvement methods** | Better ADC, finer sensor | Higher bandwidth, faster transducer | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **resolution with accuracy**. In Part (b), if the question asks about *sources limiting resolution*, do not discuss calibration errors or systematic errors — those are *accuracy* issues, not *resolution* issues. **Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.** --- ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Encoder and System-Level Resolution **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** What is Resolution? Derive the expression for the resolution of an $n$-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). A 10-bit ADC is interfaced to a microcontroller with $V_{\text{REF}} = 4.096$ V. Find the resolution in mV. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An incremental optical encoder with 5000 lines per revolution is used in a CNC machine. The encoder operates in quadrature mode. **Find:** (i) The angular resolution in degrees (ii) The angular resolution in arc-seconds (iii) The resolution in µm if the encoder is mounted on a ballscrew with 5 mm pitch **(7 marks)** --- ### 📋 Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (1 mark):** Resolution is the **smallest change in the measurand that can be detected and displayed by the instrument**. For digital systems, it is governed by the bit-depth of the ADC. **Derivation (4 marks):** An $n$-bit ADC can represent $2^n$ distinct output codes spanning the full reference range $V_{\text{REF}}$. The voltage corresponding to a change of one LSB is the smallest change in input that produces a different output code: $$R = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^n}$$ This is the **resolution in volts per LSB**. **Numerical Computation (2 marks):** **Given:** $n = 10$, $V_{\text{REF}} = 4.096$ V $$R = \frac{4.096}{2^{10}} = \frac{4.096}{1024} = 0.004\;\text{V} = 4\;\text{mV}$$ **Final Answer:** Resolution = **4 mV** --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $N = 5000$ lines/rev, quadrature mode (×4), ballscrew pitch = 5 mm/rev **(i) Angular Resolution in degrees (2 marks):** $$R_{\theta} = \frac{360°}{4N} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 5000} = \frac{360°}{20000} = 0.018°$$ **(ii) Angular Resolution in arc-seconds (2 marks):** $$R_{\theta} = 0.018° \times 3600\;\text{arc-sec/deg} = 64.8\;\text{arc-seconds}$$ **(iii) Linear Resolution on Ballscrew (3 marks):** The ballscrew advances 5 mm per revolution. The total number of effective counts per revolution (with quadrature) is $4N = 20000$. $$R_{\text{linear}} = \frac{\text{Ballscrew Pitch}}{\text{Total Counts per Revolution}} = \frac{5\;\text{mm}}{20000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{mm} = 0.25\;\mu\text{m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting the ×4 quadrature factor** — leads to 4× worse reported resolution. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 2. **Forgetting to convert 0.25 µm to mm or maintaining consistent units** in sub-part (iii). **Loss: 1 mark.** > 3. **Confusing resolution with accuracy** of the ballscrew (lead error). **Loss: 1–2 marks.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** ### 🎯 Core Concept Bullets - ☐ **Resolution** = smallest detectable change in the measurand by an instrument. - ☐ Resolution has the **same units** as the measurand. - ☐ Resolution is **NOT** the same as accuracy or sensitivity. - ☐ **Analog resolution** = Full Scale Value / Number of Divisions (Least Count). - ☐ **Digital ADC resolution** = $V_{\text{REF}} / 2^n$ (volts per LSB). - ☐ **Percentage resolution** of $n$-bit ADC ≈ $100/2^n$ %. - ☐ **SQNR** of $n$-bit ADC = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB. - ☐ **Encoder resolution** = $360°/N$ (without quadrature), $360°/(4N)$ (with quadrature). - ☐ **Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)** is the *practical* bit-depth after noise. - ☐ Each additional bit **doubles** the resolution (halves the percentage). - ☐ **Static resolution** ≥ **Dynamic resolution** (dynamic is usually worse). - ☐ Resolution is **bounded below by noise**: $R_{\min} = V_{\text{noise}}/S$. ### 🔑 Common KTU Numerical Problem Patterns - ☐ "Find resolution of $n$-bit ADC with $V_{\text{REF}} = X$ V" → $R = X/2^n$ V - ☐ "Find percentage resolution" → $R_\% = 100/2^n$ % - ☐ "Find SQNR" → $6.02n + 1.76$ dB - ☐ "Encoder with $N$ lines in quadrature mode" → $R_\theta = 360/(4N)$ - ☐ "Linear resolution on ballscrew" → Pitch / (4N) mm ### ⚠️ High-Frequency Valuation Traps - ☐ Mixing $2^n$ vs. $2^n - 1$ in resolution formulas. - ☐ Forgetting quadrature ×4 factor in encoder problems. - ☐ Confusing **resolution** (fineness) with **accuracy** (correctness). - ☐ Reporting resolution in wrong units (e.g., degrees instead of radians). - ☐ Forgetting unit conversions (degrees → arc-minutes → arc-seconds). ### 💎 Single-Line Mantra > **"Resolution is the smallest readable step — accuracy is how close to truth — precision is how repeatable — sensitivity is how amplified."**

topics.title: Repeatability and Accuracy of Manipulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repeatability and Accuracy of Manipulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of robotic manipulators and mechatronic systems, the terms **Repeatability** and **Accuracy** represent the two most critical static performance specifications used to quantify the precision of a robot's end-effector positioning. According to standard KTU 2024 scheme mechatronics terminology and ISO 9283 industrial robot performance standards:

- **Accuracy** is defined as the ability of a manipulator to move its end-effector to a commanded (target) position in the workspace. Mathematically, it is the deviation between the **commanded position** (CP) and the **mean of the attained positions** (ATP) when approaching the target from the same direction.

- **Repeatability** is defined as the ability of a manipulator to return to a previously reached position. It is a measure of the closeness of agreement between the **successively attained positions** (ATP) for the same commanded position when approached from the same direction under identical conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):** Accuracy is a *systematic* error (bias from the true target), whereas Repeatability is a *random* error (statistical spread around a mean). A robot can be highly repeatable but still inaccurate, and vice-versa, depending on calibration quality.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a blindfolded archer shooting arrows at a target bullseye:

- **Accuracy** is how close the **average** of all the arrows lands to the bullseye. If all arrows cluster far to the right of the bullseye, the archer is repeatable (consistent) but **not accurate** (systematic bias).
- **Repeatability** is how tightly **clustered** all the arrows are to *each other*, regardless of where they land relative to the bullseye. If arrows scatter all over the board but randomly, the archer has zero repeatability.

In robotic manipulator terms:
- The **bullseye** = the commanded/programmed target position.
- The **arrow cluster** = the actual physical positions the end-effector reaches.
- **Tight cluster, centered** = High accuracy AND high repeatability (calibrated industrial robot).
- **Tight cluster, off-center** = High repeatability, LOW accuracy (uncalibrated but precise robot).
- **Loose spread, centered** = Low repeatability, decent accuracy (poor mechanical tolerance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** In industrial robotics, repeatability is usually specified as **± X mm** (e.g., ± 0.02 mm for SCARA, ± 0.1 mm for 6-axis articulated). Accuracy is typically 5–10× worse than repeatability for the same manipulator.

### 1.3 Additional Related Performance Metric: **Resolution**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution (R)** is the smallest increment of movement that the controller can command to the manipulator. It is the theoretical lower bound on the end-effector's positional change per pulse, dictated by the encoder, DAC, and joint backlash.

The relationship between the three is:
$$R \leq \text{Repeatability} \leq \text{Accuracy (in terms of deviations)}$$

Resolution limits the best-case repeatability achievable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Operational Concept

Let us break down how each performance parameter is defined and measured step-by-step:

#### **A. Resolution (R)**
- The robot's control system has a finite number of addressable positions per joint.
- For a joint with a range of motion $\theta_{range}$ and $N$ bits of encoder resolution:
$$R_{\text{joint}} = \frac{\theta_{range}}{2^N}$$
- For the Cartesian workspace, the end-effector resolution is derived via the Jacobian $J(\theta)$:
$$R_{cartesian} = \vert J(\theta) \vert \cdot R_{\text{joint}}$$

#### **B. Accuracy**
- For a single commanded position $\vec{P}_c$, the robot makes $N$ attempts and the actual positions recorded are $\vec{P}_i$ where $i = 1, 2, ..., N$.
- The **mean attained position** is:
$$\bar{\vec{P}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{P}_i$$
- **Accuracy error** (or just **Accuracy**) is the Euclidean distance:
$$A = \vert \vec{P}_c - \bar{\vec{P}} \vert = \sqrt{(x_c - \bar{x})^2 + (y_c - \bar{y})^2 + (z_c - \bar{z})^2}$$

#### **C. Repeatability**
- The **standard deviation** of the attained positions about their mean:
$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$
- Repeatability $RP$ is typically quoted as the radius of a circle/sphere (in 2D/3D) that encloses a high percentage (commonly **95%** or **99%** of all attempts):
$$RP = \bar{r} \pm 3\sigma \quad \text{(3-sigma criterion, 99.7% confidence)}$$
where $\bar{r}$ is the mean radial distance from $\bar{\vec{P}}$ to each $\vec{P}_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Convention:** When a manufacturer states "Repeatability = ± 0.05 mm", it almost always means the **3-sigma** (or sometimes 2-sigma) bound, *not* the standard deviation itself. Always check the datasheet.

### 2.2 Sources of Error Affecting Accuracy and Repeatability

| **Error Source** | **Type** | **Affects Accuracy?** | **Affects Repeatability?** |
|------------------|----------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Link length tolerances (manufacturing) | Systematic | **Yes** (heavily) | No |
| Joint offset miscalibration | Systematic | **Yes** | No |
| Backlash in gears/transmission | Random + Systematic | Yes (small) | **Yes** (heavily) |
| Encoder quantization | Random | No (bias is fixed) | **Yes** (limits resolution) |
| Structural compliance (deflection) | Load-dependent | Yes | Yes |
| Thermal expansion | Systematic (drift) | Yes | Partially |
| Friction stiction | Random | No | **Yes** |
| Control loop steady-state error | Systematic | **Yes** | No |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Remarks** |
|---------------|-----------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| Joint Resolution | $R_{\theta}$ | $\theta_{range} / 2^N$ | rad | $N$ = encoder bits |
| Cartesian Resolution | $R_{xy}$ | $\vert J(\theta) \vert \cdot R_{\theta}$ | m | Via Jacobian |
| Mean Position | $\bar{\vec{P}}$ | $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vec{P}_i$ | m | Centroid of cluster |
| Accuracy | $A$ | $\vert \vec{P}_c - \bar{\vec{P}} \vert$ | m | Euclidean distance |
| Standard Deviation (1D) | $\sigma$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | m | Spread measure |
| Repeatability (3$\sigma$) | $RP$ | $\pm 3\sigma$ | m | Industrial standard |
| Position Error Variance | $\sigma^2$ | $\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2$ | m$^2$ | 3D composite |
| 3D Position Error Norm | $e$ | $\sqrt{e_x^2 + e_y^2 + e_z^2}$ | m | Instantaneous error |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Welding robots** in automotive assembly demand high **repeatability** (path consistency) but moderate accuracy.
- **PCB pick-and-place machines** need both because component pads are tiny (≤ 0.5 mm pitch).
- **Surgical robots** (e.g., Da Vinci) require sub-millimeter **accuracy**, which is achieved by **calibrating** away the systematic errors and relying on the inherent **repeatability**.
- **CNC machining centers** and 3D printers use closed-loop feedback to improve both metrics simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Joint Resolution to Cartesian Workspace Resolution

We will rigorously derive how a joint-level encoder quantization translates into an end-effector resolution in Cartesian space.

**Step 1: Define the joint increment.**
For an $n$-joint manipulator, the joint variable vector is:
$$\vec{q} = [q_1, q_2, ..., q_n]^T$$

The smallest controllable change in the $i^{th}$ joint is:
$$\Delta q_i = \frac{q_{i, max} - q_{i, min}}{2^{N_i}}$$

where $N_i$ is the encoder bit resolution of joint $i$.

**Step 2: Apply the forward kinematics differential relation.**
The Cartesian velocity of the end-effector $\vec{x}$ is related to joint velocity $\dot{\vec{q}}$ by the **Jacobian matrix** $J(\vec{q})$:
$$\dot{\vec{x}} = J(\vec{q}) \cdot \dot{\vec{q}}$$

For a small displacement $\Delta \vec{x}$ corresponding to a small joint increment $\Delta \vec{q}$:
$$\Delta \vec{x} = J(\vec{q}) \cdot \Delta \vec{q}$$

**Step 3: Bound the Cartesian displacement.**
Taking the Euclidean norm on both sides:
$$\vert \Delta \vec{x} \vert = \vert J(\vec{q}) \cdot \Delta \vec{q} \vert$$

Using the matrix norm inequality $\vert A \vec{b} \vert \leq \vert A \vert \cdot \vert \vec{b} \vert$:
$$\vert \Delta \vec{x} \vert \leq \vert J(\vec{q}) \vert \cdot \vert \Delta \vec{q} \vert$$

**Step 4: Final resolution bound.**
Therefore, the **worst-case Cartesian resolution** is:
$$R_{cart} = \max_{\vec{q} \in \text{workspace}} \left[ \sigma_{min}(J(\vec{q})) \cdot \Delta q_{min} \right]$$

where $\sigma_{min}(J)$ is the smallest singular value of the Jacobian (related to the manipulability index). Near singularities, $\sigma_{min} \to 0$, meaning **resolution becomes very poor** (large Cartesian error per unit joint step) — this is why robots must avoid singular configurations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Statistical Definition of 3-Sigma Repeatability

**Step 1: Collect N samples of attained position.**
For a fixed commanded position $\vec{P}_c$, the robot makes $N$ independent attempts. The $i^{th}$ attempt yields:
$$\vec{P}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$$

**Step 2: Compute the centroid.**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i$$

**Step 3: Compute variances for each axis.**
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$
$$\sigma_y^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$$
$$\sigma_z^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(z_i - \bar{z})^2$$

**Step 4: Form the 3D position variance.**
Assuming axis independence:
$$\sigma_P^2 = \sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2$$

**Step 5: Define 3-sigma repeatability.**
$$RP_{3\sigma} = 3 \cdot \sigma_P = 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$$

**Step 6: Confidence interpretation.**
By the Chebyshev/Chebyshev-like bound and assuming approximate Gaussianity (justified by the Central Limit Theorem from many small independent error sources), the probability that any single attempt lies within the $3\sigma$ ball is:
$$P(\vert \vec{P}_i - \bar{\vec{P}} \vert \leq RP_{3\sigma}) \approx 99.7\%$$

This is why the 3-sigma criterion is the industry default.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 2-DOF planar manipulator is commanded to reach the point $P_c = (300, 200)$ mm. After 5 attempts, the attained positions (in mm) are recorded as:
$P_1 = (300.10, 199.85)$, $P_2 = (299.95, 200.05)$, $P_3 = (300.05, 199.92)$, $P_4 = (299.98, 199.98)$, $P_5 = (300.02, 200.08)$.
Calculate (a) Accuracy, (b) 3-sigma Repeatability.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute mean position.**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{300.10 + 299.95 + 300.05 + 299.98 + 300.02}{5} = \frac{1500.10}{5} = 300.02 \text{ mm}$$
$$\bar{y} = \frac{199.85 + 200.05 + 199.92 + 199.98 + 200.08}{5} = \frac{999.88}{5} = 199.976 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Accuracy.**
$$A = \sqrt{(300 - 300.02)^2 + (200 - 199.976)^2}$$
$$A = \sqrt{(-0.02)^2 + (0.024)^2}$$
$$A = \sqrt{0.0004 + 0.000576}$$
$$A = \sqrt{0.000976}$$
$$A \approx 0.0312 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Standard deviations.**
$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{(0.08)^2 + (-0.07)^2 + (0.03)^2 + (-0.04)^2 + (0.00)^2}{4}}$$
$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{0.0064 + 0.0049 + 0.0009 + 0.0016 + 0}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0138}{4}} = \sqrt{0.00345} \approx 0.0587 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sigma_y = \sqrt{\frac{(-0.126)^2 + (0.074)^2 + (-0.056)^2 + (0.004)^2 + (0.104)^2}{4}}$$
$$\sigma_y = \sqrt{\frac{0.01588 + 0.00548 + 0.00314 + 0.000016 + 0.01082}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.03533}{4}} \approx 0.0940 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Combined deviation.**
$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{0.0587^2 + 0.0940^2} = \sqrt{0.00345 + 0.00884} = \sqrt{0.01229} \approx 0.1109 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: 3-sigma repeatability.**
$$RP_{3\sigma} = 3 \times 0.1109 \approx 0.333 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $A \approx 0.031$ mm, $RP \approx \pm 0.333$ mm (3$\sigma$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: Hierarchy of Robot Positioning Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[End-Effector Positioning Performance]:::main
    A --> B[Resolution R]
    A --> C[Accuracy A]
    A --> D[Repeatability RP]
    
    B --> B1[Smallest controllable increment]
    B --> B2[Set by encoder bits N]
    B --> B3[Joint: theta_range / 2 to N]
    
    C --> C1[Distance from CP to mean ATP]
    C --> C2[Systematic error]
    C --> C3[Improved by CALIBRATION]
    
    D --> D1[Spread of ATP about mean]
    D --> D2[Random error]
    D --> D3[Improved by MECHANICAL quality]
    
    C1 --> F1[Commanded Position CP]
    C1 --> F2[Mean Attained Position]
    D1 --> F2
    D1 --> F3[Std Deviation sigma]
    F3 --> F4[3-sigma ball 99.7 percent]
    
    classDef main fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
    class B,C,D sub
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Factors Influencing Accuracy vs Repeatability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT COMMAND]
        CP[Commanded Position P_c]
    end
    
    subgraph SYS[ROBOT SYSTEM]
        CAL[Calibration Module]
        MECH[Mechanical Structure]
        CTRL[Controller + DAC]
        FBK[Feedback Encoders]
    end
    
    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT ATTAINED]
        ATP[Attained Position P_i]
    end
    
    CP --> CTRL
    CTRL --> MECH
    MECH --> ATP
    FBK --> CTRL
    
    CAL -. Systematic Bias .-> CTRL
    MECH -. Random Noise .-> ATP
    FBK -. Quantization .-> CTRL
    
    ATP --> ANA[Statistical Analyzer]
    CP --> ANA
    ANA --> ACC[Accuracy A]
    ANA --> REP[Repeatability RP]
    
    classDef inp fill:#f4e4bc,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
    class CP inp
    class CAL,MECH,CTRL,FBK sys
    class ATP,ACC,REP out
```

### 4.3 Comparative Visualization: Accuracy vs Repeatability Scenarios

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Scenario 1: HIGH accuracy, HIGH repeatability]:::good
    S2[Scenario 2: LOW accuracy, HIGH repeatability]:::warn
    S3[Scenario 3: HIGH accuracy, LOW repeatability]:::bad
    S4[Scenario 4: LOW accuracy, LOW repeatability]:::worst
    
    S1 --> D1[Arrows clustered tightly ON bullseye - IDEAL industrial robot]
    S2 --> D2[Arrows clustered tightly OFF bullseye - needs calibration]
    S3 --> D3[Arrows spread widely AROUND bullseye - rare, loose mechanics]
    S4 --> D4[Arrows scattered randomly - broken / prototype]
    
    classDef good fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
```
