<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between accuracy and repeatability of a robotic manipulator. Why is repeatability usually better than accuracy in industrial robots? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Accuracy** is the ability of a manipulator to position its end-effector at the *commanded* (target) location. It is quantified as the deviation between the **commanded position** and the **mean of the actually attained positions** after multiple attempts.
- **Repeatability** is the ability of the manipulator to return to a *previously attained* position consistently. It is measured as the **closeness of agreement** among several successive attempts at the same target, typically quantified by the 3$\sigma$ (standard deviation) bound.
- *Why repeatability is usually better:* Repeatability depends mainly on **random errors** (backlash, friction, encoder noise, structural compliance), which are minimized by good mechanical design. Accuracy, in contrast, depends on **systematic errors** (link length tolerances, joint offsets, kinematic parameter mismatches), which are eliminated only through explicit **calibration**. Industrial robots are manufactured with high mechanical precision (good repeatability) but are usually not individually calibrated (moderate accuracy).

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Define spatial resolution of a robot. How is it related to the encoder resolution of its joints? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Spatial resolution** is defined as the smallest measurable or controllable incremental change in the position/orientation of the robot's end-effector in its Cartesian workspace.
- It is related to the joint encoder resolution through the **Jacobian matrix** $J(\theta)$ of the manipulator. For a joint increment $\Delta q_i$ corresponding to $N_i$ bits, the Cartesian resolution is bounded by:
$$R_{cart} = \vert J(\theta) \vert \cdot \Delta q_{joint}$$
- Thus, increasing the encoder bit count (larger $N_i$) reduces $\Delta q_{joint}$ and improves the spatial resolution. Near kinematic singularities, however, the Jacobian determinant approaches zero, causing the spatial resolution to deteriorate rapidly regardless of encoder quality.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the terms **accuracy**, **repeatability**, and **resolution** in the context of a robotic manipulator. Discuss how calibration affects accuracy but not repeatability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6-joint articulated robot has a positioning accuracy of **± 1.0 mm** and a repeatability of **± 0.1 mm** (3$\sigma$). The robot is to be used in an electronics assembly line to place 0402-sized SMD components (pad pitch = 1.0 mm). Critically evaluate whether this robot is suitable, and recommend any required improvements. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 Marks):**
- **Accuracy:** Defined as the ability of the robot to position its end-effector at the *commanded* target. Quantified as the distance between the commanded position $P_c$ and the mean of attained positions $\bar{P}$. Mathematically, $A = \vert P_c - \bar{P} \vert$. Units: mm.
- **Repeatability:** Ability to return consistently to a previously attained position. Quantified as the 3-sigma spread of the attained positions about their mean, $RP = 3\sigma$. Units: mm.
- **Resolution:** Smallest controllable increment in the end-effector position, limited by encoder bits and the Jacobian: $R \approx \vert J(\theta) \vert \cdot (\theta_{range}/2^N)$.
- **Effect of Calibration:** Calibration identifies and corrects the *systematic* component of the positioning error (link length errors, joint offsets, gear eccentricities). Since accuracy is dominated by systematic errors, calibration substantially improves accuracy. However, calibration does not eliminate the *random* errors (backlash, friction, thermal noise, encoder quantization), which dominate repeatability. Therefore, a well-calibrated robot has good accuracy and inherits the *inherent* repeatability of its mechanical structure.
- (Award full 7 marks for definitions, formulas, and a clear distinction of calibration's role.)

**(b) Suitability Evaluation (7 Marks):**
- **Component constraint:** 0402 SMD components have a pad pitch of 1.0 mm; the placement tolerance is typically ± 0.05 mm to ± 0.1 mm.
- **Robot repeatability:** ± 0.1 mm — this is **just at the limit** of the required placement tolerance (3$\sigma$).
- **Robot accuracy:** ± 1.0 mm — this is **10× worse** than the required tolerance. The robot will, on average, miss the target by up to 1 mm.
- **Verdict:** The robot is **NOT suitable** in its current state for 0402 placement. The accuracy is the binding constraint.
- **Recommended improvements:**
  1. Perform **kinematic calibration** using a laser tracker or coordinate measuring machine (CMM) to reduce the systematic accuracy error to ≤ 0.05 mm.
  2. Mount a **vision-guided correction** loop (machine vision) to correct residual errors in real time.
  3. Consider upgrading to a **SCARA robot** which typically offers better accuracy in the planar pick-and-place task.
  4. Operate in a **temperature-controlled environment** to minimize thermal drift.
  5. Replace with a higher-precision robot with sub-0.05 mm accuracy if available.
- (Award 2 marks for evaluation, 3 marks for verdict, 2 marks for recommendations.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **sources of errors** that affect the static positioning performance of a manipulator. Classify them as systematic or random. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF planar robot, the joint encoders are 12-bit with a range of 0 to 180° for joint 1 and -90° to 90° for joint 2. The Jacobian at a particular configuration is $J = \begin{bmatrix} -200 & 100 \\ 300 & 50 \end{bmatrix}$ mm/rad. Compute the **worst-case spatial resolution** of the end-effector in mm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Error Sources (7 Marks):**

**Systematic Errors (affect accuracy):**
1. Link length manufacturing tolerances
2. Joint offset/zero-position miscalibration
3. Gear reduction ratio errors
4. Structural deflection under load (if uncompensated)
5. Thermal expansion of links
6. Control-loop steady-state error

**Random Errors (affect repeatability):**
1. Backlash in gears and transmissions
2. Encoder quantization noise
3. Static and Coulomb friction (stiction)
4. Random vibration and external disturbances
5. Random thermal fluctuations
6. Sensor measurement noise

(A well-labeled block diagram showing: Kinematic Model → Mechanical Structure → Sensors/Encoders → Control Law → End-Effector Position, with error arrows pointing into each block from systematic and random sources, awards full marks.)

**(b) Worst-Case Spatial Resolution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Joint increments.**
Joint 1 range = 180° = $\pi$ rad ≈ 3.1416 rad. With $N_1 = 12$ bits:
$$\Delta q_1 = \frac{\pi}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.1416}{4096} \approx 7.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

Joint 2 range = 180° = $\pi$ rad. With $N_2 = 12$ bits:
$$\Delta q_2 = \frac{\pi}{2^{12}} \approx 7.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: Apply displacement formula.**
$$\Delta \vec{x} = J \cdot \Delta \vec{q}$$

For a unit input along joint 1 alone: $\Delta \vec{x} = J \cdot [7.67\times 10^{-4}, 0]^T$
$$\Delta \vec{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -200 \\ 300 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 7.67 \times 10^{-4} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1534 \\ 0.2301 \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}$$
Magnitude: $\vert \Delta \vec{x}_1 \vert = \sqrt{0.1534^2 + 0.2301^2} = \sqrt{0.0235 + 0.0529} = \sqrt{0.0765} \approx 0.2766$ mm

For joint 2 alone: $\Delta \vec{x}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 7.67 \times 10^{-4} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0767 \\ 0.0384 \end{bmatrix}$ mm
Magnitude: $\vert \Delta \vec{x}_2 \vert = \sqrt{0.0767^2 + 0.0384^2} = \sqrt{0.00588 + 0.00147} = \sqrt{0.00735} \approx 0.0857$ mm

**Step 3: Worst case (combined).**
The **worst-case** occurs when both joints move unfavorably. Conservative upper bound (sum of magnitudes):
$$R_{max} \approx 0.2766 + 0.0857 \approx 0.362 \text{ mm}$$

Or, using the spectral norm approach: $\vert J \vert_2 = \sqrt{\lambda_{max}(J^T J)}$
$$J^T J = \begin{bmatrix} -200 & 300 \\ 100 & 50 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -200 & 100 \\ 300 & 50 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 130000 & -5000 \\ -5000 & 12500 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues: solve $\lambda^2 - 142500\lambda + (130000 \cdot 12500 - 25000000) = 0$
$\lambda^2 - 142500\lambda + 1.6 \times 10^9 = 0$
$\lambda_{max} \approx 1.42 \times 10^5$ (approximately)
$\vert J \vert_2 \approx \sqrt{1.42 \times 10^5} \approx 377$ mm/rad

**Step 4: Apply to a unit joint step of $7.67 \times 10^{-4}$ rad:**
$$R_{cart} \approx 377 \times 7.67 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.289 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** The worst-case spatial resolution is **≈ 0.29 mm** (or ≈ 0.36 mm by the conservative bound).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Common Mark-Deduction Points):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Failing to convert degrees to radians before applying the Jacobian. The Jacobian is in mm/rad, so the joint increment must also be in **radians**, not degrees. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 2. **Confusing 3-sigma with std deviation:** When a datasheet says "repeatability = ± 0.05 mm", students often write $\sigma = 0.05$ mm in their formulas. The correct interpretation is $3\sigma = 0.05$ mm, hence $\sigma = 0.05/3$ mm. Marks deducted: 1.
> 3. **Forgetting the Jacobian:** Students often compute joint resolution only and forget to map it to Cartesian space. The question asks for *end-effector* resolution, which **requires** the Jacobian. Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 4. **Mixing up accuracy and repeatability formulas:** Using the standard deviation formula to compute "accuracy" instead of the distance to the mean — frequent conceptual error. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 5. **Not stating the confidence level:** Always mention "3-sigma implies 99.7% confidence" when reporting repeatability. Marks deducted: 0.5.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- **Accuracy** = distance from *commanded* position to *mean attained* position. It is a **systematic** error dominated measure.
- **Repeatability** = spread (3$\sigma$) of attained positions about their *mean*. It is a **random** error dominated measure.
- **Resolution** = smallest controllable increment, bounded below by encoder quantization: $R_{joint} = \theta_{range}/2^N$.
- Hierarchy: $R \leq RP$ (typically), and $A$ can be much larger than $RP$.
- Calibration improves **accuracy** but **NOT** repeatability (which is mechanical).
- The **Jacobian** $J(\theta)$ maps joint resolution to Cartesian (workspace) resolution: $\Delta x = J \cdot \Delta q$.
- Near **singularities**, $det(J) \to 0$, causing infinite effective resolution loss.
- Industrial repeatability is conventionally reported at the **3$\sigma$** level (99.7% confidence).
- For a sample of $N$ positions, use the **unbiased** estimator $(N-1)$ in the denominator of the variance formula.
- **Accuracy** is a single number per command; **repeatability** is a statistical number per command.
- High-accuracy + high-repeatability is the goal; achieved by **calibration + precision mechanics**.
- ISO 9283 is the international standard governing robot accuracy/repeatability testing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT601 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotics Classification** is the systematic categorization of robotic systems based on a defined set of parameters including **kinematic structure (coordinate system)**, **degrees of freedom (DOF)**, **control methodology**, **drive technology (power source)**, **intended application domain**, **generation of evolution**, and **locomotion strategy**. In KTU 2024 Scheme Mechatronics (PCBRT601), this topic forms the foundational taxonomy required to select, design, and integrate robotic manipulators and mobile platforms for industrial automation, manufacturing, and emerging service sectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> Classification is a high-yield, frequently-tested topic. The Board Examiner expects students to:
> - Map robot architectures to their **coordinate systems (Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, Delta)**.
> - Differentiate between **servo-controlled** and **non-servo (open-loop)** robots.
> - Justify the selection of **drive (electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic)** based on payload and precision.
> - Identify **generations of robots** and the **degree of freedom** requirements for typical industrial tasks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Robot Zoo" Analogy — Building Intuition**
>
> Imagine a **car manufacturing plant** where different workers specialize in different tasks. One worker uses a gantry crane (moving strictly in straight lines up/down/left/right) to lift car bodies — that is a **Cartesian robot**. Another worker uses a telescoping pole mounted on a rotating turntable to reach a point in 3D space — that is a **Spherical/Polar robot**. A third worker has a human-like arm with shoulder, elbow, and wrist joints — that is an **Articulated robot**. Each worker is "classified" by the **shape of the volume** they can reach (called the **workspace**) and the **way their joints move**.
>
> **Think of classification as answering three simple questions:**
> 1. **How does it move?** → Coordinate system (Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated).
> 2. **How is it controlled?** → Servo (closed-loop, accurate) vs. Non-servo (open-loop, cheap).
> 3. **What powers it?** → Electric, Hydraulic, or Pneumatic drives.

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> The **payload capacity**, **workspace geometry**, **repeatability (typically ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm)**, and **degrees of freedom** of a robot determine its fitness for a task. Misclassification or improper selection leads to **bottlenecks in production, safety hazards, and capital loss**. A SCARA robot excels at vertical assembly, while a Delta robot excels at high-speed pick-and-place — swapping them would be an engineering disaster.

---

## 1.4 Master Classification Matrix (At-a-Glance)

| Classification Basis | Sub-Categories (with examples) |
|---|---|
| **Coordinate System / Kinematic Structure** | Cartesian / Gantry, Cylindrical, Spherical (Polar), Articulated (Jointed-Arm), SCARA, Delta (Parallel) |
| **Control Method** | Servo-Controlled (Closed-loop), Non-Servo (Open-loop / Pick-and-Place) |
| **Power Source / Drive** | Electrically Powered, Hydraulically Powered, Pneumatically Powered |
| **Application Domain** | Industrial, Medical / Surgical, Military / Defense, Space, Underwater, Service / Domestic |
| **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** | 3-DOF, 4-DOF, 5-DOF, 6-DOF, 7-DOF+ |
| **Locomotion** | Stationary / Fixed, Wheeled, Tracked, Legged (1, 2, 4, 6, 8 legs), Aerial (Drone), Aquatic |
| **Generation of Evolution** | 1st Gen (Pick-and-Place), 2nd Gen (Teach/Playback), 3rd Gen (Sensory / Adaptive), 4th Gen (Intelligent), 5th Gen (Cognitive / Collaborative) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Based on Coordinate System (The Most Important — KTU High-Yield)

This is the **principal classification** demanded by KTU Module 4. The classification hinges on the **configuration of the robot's arm**, which dictates the shape of the **reachable workspace** (the volume in 3D space the end-effector can reach).

### 2.1.1 Cartesian / Gantry Robot (3 Linear Prismatic Joints: L-L-L)

- **Structure**: Three mutually perpendicular **prismatic (linear sliding)** joints, typically labeled **X, Y, Z**.
- **Joints**: 3 × **P (Prismatic)** → Often denoted **PPP** or **L-L-L** (Linear-Linear-Linear).
- **Workspace**: A **rectangular cuboid** (box-shaped) volume.
- **Advantages**:
  - Extremely high **rigidity and accuracy** (repeatability up to **±0.02 mm**).
  - Easy **offline programming**; linear kinematics simplifies controller design.
  - Large workspace can be scaled by extending rails.
- **Disadvantages**:
  - Requires **large floor space / ceiling gantry**.
  - Slow speed relative to articulated arms.
  - Cannot reach into confined spaces.
- **Industrial Applications**: CNC pickers, **PCB assembly**, adhesive dispensing, palletizing large flat panels, coordinate measuring machines (CMM), 3D printers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Cartesian:** "**L**asers move in **L**ines" — Cartesian = **L**inear, **L**inear, **L**inear.

### 2.1.2 Cylindrical Robot (R-P-P or P-P-P with base rotation)

- **Structure**: One **revolute (rotational)** joint at the base, followed by two **prismatic** joints.
- **Joints**: 1 × **R** + 2 × **P** → Notation **RPP** or **TRP** (where T is the rotating column).
- **Workspace**: A portion of a **cylinder** (hence the name).
- **Coordinate System Used**: Cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$.
- **Advantages**:
  - Good reach into **cylindrical cavities** (e.g., inside a machine tool).
  - Simple inverse kinematics (only one rotation).
- **Disadvantages**:
  - Cannot reach around obstacles easily (workspace is open in front).
  - Less rigid at full reach.
- **Industrial Applications**: Die-casting, injection moulding machine tending, machine tool loading/unloading.

### 2.1.3 Spherical / Polar Robot (R-R-P or 2R-P)

- **Structure**: Two **revolute** joints (waist and shoulder) + one **prismatic** (telescopic) joint.
- **Joints**: 2 × **R** + 1 × **P** → Notation **RRP**.
- **Workspace**: A portion of a **sphere** (or spherical shell).
- **Coordinate System Used**: Spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$.
- **Advantages**:
  - Larger workspace volume than cylindrical for same reach.
  - Good for tasks with obstacles above or below.
- **Disadvantages**:
  - Limited dexterity near the pole.
  - Telescopic arm reduces stiffness.
- **Industrial Applications**: Early Unimate-style robots, forging, die-handling in foundries, basic welding.

### 2.1.4 Articulated / Jointed-Arm / Anthropomorphic Robot (R-R-R or more)

- **Structure**: All **revolute** joints mimicking a human arm — waist (J1), shoulder (J2), elbow (J3), and optionally wrist pitch/yaw/roll (J4–J6).
- **Joints**: 3 or more × **R** → Notation **RRR** (or **RRRRRR** for 6-DOF).
- **Workspace**: A **complex lobe-shaped** volume (almost spherical but with an internal hollow).
- **Advantages**:
  - **Maximum dexterity** — can reach over and around obstacles.
  - Industry standard for complex assembly, welding, painting.
- **Disadvantages**:
  - Complex **forward and inverse kinematics** (Denavit-Hartenberg parameters required).
  - Lower mechanical rigidity than Cartesian.
- **Industrial Applications**: **Automotive welding and painting (e.g., FANUC, ABB, KUKA)**, complex assembly, palletizing, machine tending.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** *An articulated robot is one whose arm consists of at least three revolute joints connected in series, providing a workspace resembling a portion of a sphere.*

### 2.1.5 SCARA — Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm (R-R-P with vertical compliance)

- **Structure**: Two **revolute** joints in the horizontal plane + one **prismatic** joint for vertical motion.
- **Joints**: 2 × **R** + 1 × **P** (vertical) → Notation **RRP**.
- **Workspace**: A **horizontal disc-shaped** volume (cylindrical in nature, but the compliance is selective).
- **Key Feature**: **Rigid in the vertical Z-axis**, but **compliant (flexible) in X-Y horizontal plane** — perfect for inserting round pegs into holes with slight misalignment.
- **Industrial Applications**: **PCB component insertion, vertical assembly, screw-driving, pick-and-place** of small parts.

### 2.1.6 Delta / Parallel Robot (3 Translational + Optional 1 Rotational Wrist)

- **Structure**: Three parallelogram arms connected to a common base, driving a triangular end-plate.
- **Joints**: 3 × **R + R + R** (in parallelograms) + 1 × **P** (central quill).
- **Workspace**: A **dome-shaped (truncated cone / cylinder)** workspace directly below the base.
- **Advantages**:
  - **Ultra-high speed** (up to **300 picks/minute**).
  - Exceptional **rigidity** (parallel structure shares load).
  - Very low moving mass at the end-effector.
- **Industrial Applications**: **High-speed packaging, food sorting, pharmaceutical pick-and-place, 3D printing** (sometimes).

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison Shortcut for KTU Viva:**
> - **Reach all over the place?** → Articulated
> - **Box-shape workspace, ultra-precise?** → Cartesian
> - **High-speed picking in a small dome?** → Delta
> - **Vertical insertion of components?** → SCARA

---

## 2.2 Classification Based on Control Method

### 2.2.1 Non-Servo (Open-Loop / Pick-and-Place / Bang-Bang) Robots

- No feedback of joint position.
- Driven by **limit switches** and **mechanical stops**.
- **Sequence control** — executes a fixed sequence of ON/OFF actions.
- **Stop points are hard-coded** by physical stops.
- **Pros**: Cheap, simple, highly repeatable at fixed stops, very fast for limited motion.
- **Cons**: No path control, no trajectory planning, cannot deviate from the preset program.
- **Example**: Seiko D-Transervo, early pick-and-place units in the 1970s.

### 2.2.2 Servo-Controlled (Closed-Loop) Robots

- Continuous feedback of **position, velocity, and sometimes torque** via encoders and resolvers.
- **Path control** is possible — can move along straight lines or smooth curves.
- **Pros**: Programmable, accurate, adaptive trajectory, handles complex tasks.
- **Cons**: More expensive, complex controller (PID-based), requires calibration.
- **Sub-classification of Servo Robots**:
  - **Point-to-Point (PTP)**: Moves between taught points without controlling the path between them (e.g., spot welding).
  - **Continuous Path (CP)**: Controls the entire trajectory (e.g., arc welding, spray painting, cutting).

---

## 2.3 Classification Based on Power Source (Drive Technology)

| Drive Type | Power Source | Strengths | Weaknesses | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electric** | AC / DC servo motors, stepper motors | **Precise, clean, quiet, easily controlled** | Limited torque for very heavy loads | Assembly, light welding, clean rooms |
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized oil (typically **70–200 bar**) | **Very high payload (hundreds of kg)**, strong, fast | Leakage, noise, requires pump and reservoir | Heavy payload handling, forging, die-cast |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air (typically **4–8 bar**) | **Simple, cheap, fast, intrinsically safe** | Inaccurate, low payload, compressible medium | Small pick-and-place, packaging, food industry |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Fact:** Hydraulic robots dominate the **heavy-payload (>50 kg)** segment. Pneumatic robots dominate **high-speed, low-cost** applications. Electric robots dominate **precision tasks** in modern Industry 4.0 cells.

---

## 2.4 Classification Based on Application Domain

- **Industrial Robots**: Manufacturing, assembly, welding, painting, palletizing (ISO 8373 defines these).
- **Medical / Surgical Robots**: Da Vinci Surgical System, rehabilitation exoskeletons.
- **Military / Defense**: Bomb disposal, reconnaissance, unmanned combat vehicles.
- **Space Robots**: NASA Canadarm (6-DOF manipulator on the ISS), Mars rovers (Sojourner, Curiosity, Perseverance).
- **Underwater / AUVs**: Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) for pipeline inspection.
- **Service / Domestic**: Vacuum cleaners (Roomba), elderly care assistants.
- **Agricultural Robots**: Autonomous tractors, fruit-picking arms.
- **Educational / Hobbyist**: LEGO Mindstorms, Arduino-based arms.

---

## 2.5 Classification Based on Degree of Freedom (DOF)

**Degree of Freedom (DOF)** = number of independent parameters that define the configuration of the robot in space.

- **3 DOF**: Position only (X, Y, Z) — simple pick-and-place.
- **4 DOF**: 3 translations + 1 rotation — common in SCARA and palletizing.
- **5 DOF**: Adds a second wrist rotation — palletizing, simple welding.
- **6 DOF**: Position (X, Y, Z) + Orientation (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) — **the industrial standard for full dexterity**.
- **7+ DOF**: Redundant manipulators (avoiding singularities, human-arm-like motion) — research platforms like Franka Emika Panda.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exam:** Be ready to compute the DOF using the **Kutzbach-Grübler criterion**:
>
> $$M = d(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{J} (d - f_i)$$
>
> where $M$ is the mobility (DOF), $d$ is the number of independent parameters per joint (typically 3 in 3D), $n$ is the number of links (excluding ground), $J$ is the number of joints, and $f_i$ is the DOF of the $i$-th joint.

---

## 2.6 Classification Based on Locomotion

- **Stationary / Fixed-Base Manipulators**: Industrial arms mounted to the floor or a linear track.
- **Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMRs)**: Differential drive, Ackermann steering, omnidirectional (Mecanum / Swedish wheels).
- **Tracked Robots**: Tank-like treads — used in rough terrain (military, agricultural).
- **Legged Robots**:
  - 1 leg (hopping) — theoretically minimal; 2 legs (bipedal — humanoids like ASIMO); 4 legs (quadruped — Boston Dynamics Spot); 6 legs (hexapod — insect-like); 8 legs (octopod).
- **Aerial Robots (UAVs)**: Quadcopters, fixed-wing drones.
- **Aquatic / Underwater Robots (AUVs/ROVs)**: Fin-based or propeller-based swimmers.

---

## 2.7 Classification Based on Generation of Evolution

| Generation | Era | Defining Feature | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | 1950s–1970s | **Pick-and-place**, non-servo, no feedback | Unimate 2000A (first industrial robot, 1961) |
| **2nd Gen** | 1980s | **Teach-pendant programming**, servo control, simple sensors | PUMA 560, ASEA IRB |
| **3rd Gen** | 1990s | **Sensory feedback** (vision, force, tactile), adaptive control | Fanuc i-series, ABB IRB 1400 |
| **4th Gen** | 2000s | **Intelligent / autonomous**, mobile, networked | KUKA KR AGILUS, collaborative robots |
| **5th Gen** | 2010s–present | **Cognitive / AI-driven, collaborative (cobots), swarm robotics** | Rethink Baxter, Universal Robots UR10e, Boston Dynamics Atlas |

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula & Fact Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this section — it covers 80% of the marks.**

| Concept | Key Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of DOF in 3D space | $M = 3(n-1) - \sum(3 - f_i)$ | dimensionless | Kutzbach-Grübler criterion |
| Maximum reach (Cartesian) | $L_{max} = X_{travel} + Y_{travel} + Z_{travel}$ | m | Sum of linear travels |
| Workspace volume (Cartesian) | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | m³ | Rectangular cuboid |
| Repeatability (industrial) | $R = \pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm | mm | KTU: define "repeatability" vs "accuracy" |
| Resolution (encoder) | $Res = \frac{360°}{N_{counts/rev}}$ | °/count | N = encoder pulses |
| Hydraulic pressure | $P = 70$–$200$ bar | bar (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa) | Standard industrial |
| Pneumatic pressure | $P = 4$–$8$ bar | bar | Standard shop-floor air |
| Payload mass typical | Electric: ≤ 20 kg; Hydraulic: 50–1000 kg; Pneumatic: ≤ 5 kg | kg | KTU-mandated figures |
| Delta robot pick rate | up to **300 picks/min** | picks/min | Industry benchmark |
| 6-DOF requirement | $X, Y, Z, Roll, Pitch, Yaw$ | mixed | Position + full orientation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeatability vs Accuracy — KTU commonly asks the difference:**
> - **Accuracy**: How close the robot can come to a *programmed* point in space (varies with location in workspace).
> - **Repeatability**: How close the robot returns to a *previously taught* point on repeated cycles. Industrial robots have **high repeatability but lower accuracy** — repeatability is the metric the industry uses.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the **Kutzbach-Grübler DOF calculation** applied to a typical 6-DOF industrial robot, which is a high-yield analytical problem for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination).

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU Typical 7-Mark Question)

> *"A 6-DOF industrial articulated robot has 6 revolute joints and 7 links (including the base). Using the Kutzbach-Grübler criterion, verify the mobility (DOF) of the manipulator. Also determine the DOF for a SCARA robot having 2 revolute and 1 prismatic joint with 4 links."*

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — State the Kutzbach-Grübler Formula

The general Kutzbach-Grübler mobility equation in **3D space** is:

$$M = d(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{J} (d - f_i)$$

where:
- $M$ = Mobility / Degree of Freedom of the mechanism
- $d$ = Number of independent parameters per joint (in 3D, $d = 3$ for full spatial, $d = 6$ for full spatial with orientation; for planar mechanisms $d = 3$)
- $n$ = Total number of rigid links (excluding the fixed ground/base)
- $J$ = Total number of joints
- $f_i$ = Degrees of freedom of the $i$-th joint

> For most manipulator robotics problems, we use $d = 3$ (i.e., each link has 3 translational DOF in 3D, and we account for orientation separately in the wrist).

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Apply to the 6-DOF Articulated Robot

**Given:**
- Number of revolute joints, $J = 6$ (J1: waist, J2: shoulder, J3: elbow, J4-J6: wrist)
- Number of links, $n = 7$ (base + 6 arm links) — excluding ground
- Each revolute joint has $f_i = 1$ DOF
- Working in 3D space, $d = 3$

**Substitute into the formula:**

$$M = 3(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{6} (3 - f_i)$$

**Compute the link contribution term:**

$$3(n - 1) = 3(7 - 1) = 3 \times 6 = 18$$

**Compute the joint constraint term:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} (3 - f_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{6} (3 - 1) = 6 \times 2 = 12$$

**Therefore:**

$$M = 18 - 12 = 6$$

> ✅ **Result: 6 DOF — verified.** This corresponds to position $(X, Y, Z)$ and orientation $(\text{Roll, Pitch, Yaw})$ — the full spatial dexterity of a 6-DOF robot.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Apply to the SCARA Robot

**Given:**
- $J = 3$ joints (2 revolute + 1 prismatic)
- $n = 4$ links
- Revolute joint: $f_i = 1$; Prismatic joint: $f_i = 1$

**Substitute:**

$$M = 3(4 - 1) - [(3 - 1) + (3 - 1) + (3 - 1)]$$

**Compute the link term:**

$$3(n - 1) = 3(4 - 1) = 9$$

**Compute the joint term:**

$$(3 - 1) + (3 - 1) + (3 - 1) = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6$$

**Therefore:**

$$M = 9 - 6 = 3$$

> ✅ **Result: 3 DOF** — SCARA has 3 useful DOF (X, Y translation + Z vertical insertion), which is why it is excellent for vertical pick-and-place but cannot orient parts arbitrarily.

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Apply to a Delta Robot (Parallel Manipulator)

**Given (standard Delta with central quill):**
- $J = 12$ joints (3 parallelograms × 4 joints each)
- $n = 8$ links (base + 3 upper arms + 3 lower arms + moving platform + central quill)
- All joints are revolute with $f_i = 1$

**Substitute:**

$$M = 3(8 - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{12} (3 - 1)$$

$$M = 3(7) - 12(2) = 21 - 24 = -3$$

> The negative result indicates **redundancy / over-constraint** in the simple count — Delta robots have **passive DOF** in their parallelogram structures, requiring a **modified Grübler equation** that accounts for parallel structure and passive joints. The **effective DOF of the end-effector is 3 (X, Y, Z translations)**.

### 3.2.5 Summary Table of DOF Computations

| Robot Type | $n$ (links) | $J$ (joints) | Joint DOF Sum | Mobility $M$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6-DOF Articulated | 7 | 6 | 12 | **6** | Position + Orientation |
| SCARA (R-R-P) | 4 | 3 | 6 | **3** | X, Y, Z translations |
| Delta (parallel) | 8 | 12 | 24 | **3 (effective)** | XYZ translations only |
| Cartesian (X-Y-Z) | 4 | 3 (prismatic) | 6 | **3** | XYZ translations |
| Cylindrical (R-P-P) | 4 | 3 | 6 | **3** | r, θ, z coordinates |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Joint Type and Workspace Shape

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the joint configuration dictate the workspace shape?**
>
> The joint arrangement determines the **coordinate system** in which the end-effector position is described. This is not a coincidence — it is a direct consequence of the Jacobian transformation between joint space and Cartesian space.

| Robot Type | Joint Sequence | Coordinate System | Workspace Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (L-L-L) | P-P-P | Cartesian $(x, y, z)$ | Rectangular cuboid |
| Cylindrical (R-P-P) | R-P-P | Cylindrical $(r, \theta, z)$ | Cylindrical shell |
| Spherical (R-R-P) | R-R-P | Spherical $(r, \theta, \phi)$ | Spherical dome |
| Articulated (R-R-R + Wrist) | R-R-R-R-R-R | Generalized coordinates | Spherical lobe with orientation |
| SCARA (R-R-P) | R-R-P | Cylindrical-like (with Z-vertical) | Cylindrical disc |
| Delta (3 Parallelograms) | 3 × 4R | Cartesian via parallel math | Cylindrical dome below base |

---

## 3.4 Selection Justification Matrix (KTU Application Question)

> *"For a 14-mark ESE question, justify the robot selection for three different industrial tasks."*

| Industrial Task | Recommended Robot | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **PCB Pick-and-Place (small components, vertical insertion)** | **SCARA** | Selective vertical compliance handles slight misalignments; high speed; small footprint |
| **Car body welding (large sheet metal, around obstacles)** | **Articulated (6-DOF)** | Full dexterity to reach over and inside the car body; ~6 m reach |
| **High-speed sorting of biscuits in a packaging line** | **Delta (parallel)** | 200+ picks/min; high rigidity; cleanable stainless steel for food |
| **CNC machine loading (cylindrical work envelope)** | **Cylindrical** | Naturally fits cylindrical machine interior; cost-effective |
| **3D printing of a large concrete slab** | **Cartesian (Gantry)** | Maximum rigidity over long travel; simple kinematics; large workspace |
| **Heavy forging die handling (500 kg payload)** | **Hydraulic Articulated** | Hydraulic drive provides high torque; can survive hot environments |
| **Clean room semiconductor wafer handling** | **SCARA (electric)** | No air/oil contamination; high precision; cleanroom-rated |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Cartesian robot and a SCARA robot in terms of joint configuration, workspace shape, and typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cartesian Robot | SCARA Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Joint Configuration** | Three prismatic (linear) joints in **L-L-L** arrangement | Two revolute + one prismatic (**R-R-P**) |
| **Workspace Shape** | Rectangular cuboid | Cylindrical disc (dome) |
| **Typical Application** | CNC pick-and-place, 3D printing, large-area gantry | PCB assembly, vertical insertion, screw-driving |
| **Compliance** | Rigid in all axes | Rigid vertically, compliant horizontally |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | Moderate |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: What is a non-servo (pick-and-place) robot? State its two main limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **non-servo robot** (also called a **pick-and-place** or **bang-bang** robot) is a robotic manipulator that operates in an **open-loop control mode** without any feedback of joint position or velocity. Motion is controlled by **mechanical stops and limit switches**, and the robot executes a **fixed sequence** of actions from start to end.

**Two main limitations:**
1. **No path control** — The robot cannot follow a continuous or curved trajectory between two points; it simply moves from one taught point to the next.
2. **No trajectory feedback** — Open-loop operation means any external disturbance (load variation, mechanical wear) goes uncorrected, reducing reliability and precision.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not confuse "repeatability" with "accuracy"** — KTU examiners routinely award 0 marks if these are interchanged.
> - **Joint notation must be precise** — Use **P for prismatic** and **R for revolute**. Writing "linear" instead of "prismatic" loses 1 mark.
> - **Workspace names must match the joint sequence** — A robot with R-R-P joints is *not* always SCARA; you must also state "selective compliance in the horizontal plane".
> - **DOF calculations must show all intermediate steps** — A single number without the Grübler expansion is penalized.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE – Module 4 Choice A]`

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the classification of robots based on the **coordinate system / kinematic structure**. Draw the kinematic structure of an **articulated robot** and a **Delta robot**, and state two industrial applications of each.

> **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the three types of robot drives — **electric, hydraulic, and pneumatic** — with respect to payload capacity, precision, cleanliness, cost, and typical industrial application. Justify which drive you would select for (i) **PCB pick-and-place**, (ii) **forging die handling in a foundry**.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **Q(a) Model Answer [7 Marks]:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The classification of robots based on the **coordinate system** (also called the **kinematic structure** or **arm configuration**) groups robots by the **type and sequence of their joints**, which in turn determines the **shape of their workspace** in 3D space.

**Joint Notation Convention (1 mark):**
- **P** = Prismatic (linear sliding) joint
- **R** = Revolute (rotational) joint

**The Six Principal Configurations:**

**1. Cartesian / Gantry Robot (L-L-L or P-P-P) [1 mark]:**
- Structure: Three mutually perpendicular linear joints providing motion along X, Y, Z.
- Workspace: Rectangular cuboid.
- Example: Gantry crane used in CNC machines.
- Applications: CNC pick-and-place, 3D printing, PCB assembly, large-panel palletizing.

**2. Cylindrical Robot (R-P-P) [0.5 marks]:**
- Structure: Rotating base + radial linear + vertical linear joints.
- Workspace: Cylindrical shell.
- Applications: Machine tool loading, die-casting.

**3. Spherical / Polar Robot (R-R-P) [0.5 marks]:**
- Structure: Two revolute joints (waist + shoulder) + one telescopic prismatic.
- Workspace: Spherical dome.
- Applications: Forging, basic welding.

**4. Articulated / Jointed-Arm Robot (R-R-R + Wrist) [1 mark]:**
- Structure: All revolute joints mimicking a human arm — waist, shoulder, elbow, and 3-axis wrist (total 6 R-joints for full dexterity).
- Workspace: Complex lobe-shaped volume; nearly full sphere with internal singularities.
- Applications: Automotive body welding (FANUC R-2000iC), spray painting, complex assembly.

**5. SCARA — Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm (R-R-P) [0.5 marks]:**
- Structure: Two horizontal revolute + one vertical prismatic.
- Workspace: Cylindrical disc; **rigid in Z, compliant in X-Y**.
- Applications: PCB component insertion, screw-driving, vertical pick-and-place.

**6. Delta / Parallel Robot (3 × parallelogram arms) [0.5 marks]:**
- Structure: Three parallelogram arms meeting at a moving triangular end-plate.
- Workspace: Truncated dome directly below the base.
- Applications: High-speed packaging (up to 300 picks/min), pharmaceutical sorting, food handling.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The choice of robot configuration must align with the **task geometry, speed, payload, and precision** requirements.

#### **Q(b) Model Answer [7 Marks]:**

**Comparison Table of Robot Drives [4 marks]:**

| Parameter | Electric Drive | Hydraulic Drive | Pneumatic Drive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | AC / DC servo motors, stepper motors | Pressurized oil at **70–200 bar** | Compressed air at **4–8 bar** |
| **Payload Capacity** | Up to ~20 kg (industrial) | 50 kg to 1000 kg | Up to ~5 kg |
| **Precision** | High (sub-mm repeatability) | Moderate (affected by oil compliance) | Low (air is compressible) |
| **Cleanliness** | Excellent — no leakage | Poor — risk of oil leaks | Good — air is clean |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | High (pump, reservoir, valves) | Low (only compressor + valves) |
| **Noise** | Low | Moderate to high | High (exhaust noise) |
| **Speed** | Fast, easily controlled | Fast and powerful | Very fast for small motions |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High (seals, filters) | Low |
| **Typical Use** | Precision assembly, welding, clean rooms | Heavy forging, die handling, large manipulators | Pick-and-place, packaging, food industry |

**Justification of Drive Selection [3 marks]:**

**(i) PCB Pick-and-Place (1.5 marks):**
→ **Electric Drive** is selected.
- **Reason**: PCB assembly requires **high positional accuracy (sub-millimeter), clean environment (no oil contamination), and repeatable motion at high speed.** Electric servo motors deliver excellent precision and are cleanroom-compatible.

**(ii) Forging Die Handling in a Foundry (1.5 marks):**
→ **Hydraulic Drive** is selected.
- **Reason**: Forging dies are extremely **heavy (often several hundred kilograms), hot, and require strong gripping forces.** Hydraulic actuators provide the **highest payload-to-weight ratio and force density**, and can operate reliably in hot, dirty environments where electric motors would overheat.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU ESE – Module 4 Choice B]`

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **five generations of robots** with their defining features, control characteristics, and one representative example each. How does the 5th generation differ fundamentally from the earlier generations?

> **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** A **SCARA robot** has 2 revolute joints and 1 prismatic joint with 4 links. Calculate its **degree of freedom (DOF)** using the **Kutzbach-Grübler criterion**. State the coordinate system it operates in, the shape of its workspace, and explain why it is preferred for **vertical assembly of electronic components** over an articulated robot.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### **Q(a) Model Answer [7 Marks]:**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
The **generation-based classification** of robots traces the historical evolution of robotics from simple mechanical pickers to today's **AI-driven collaborative robots (cobots)**. Each generation marks a leap in **sensing, decision-making, and autonomy**.

**The Five Generations:**

**1st Generation — Pick-and-Place Robots (1950s–1970s) [1 mark]:**
- **Defining feature**: Non-servo, open-loop control using limit switches and mechanical stops.
- **Control**: Sequence control with no feedback.
- **Example**: **Unimate 2000A** (1961) — the first industrial robot used by General Motors for die casting.

**2nd Generation — Teach-Playback / Servo Robots (1980s) [1 mark]:**
- **Defining feature**: Closed-loop servo control using position feedback; robots are taught a path via a teach pendant.
- **Control**: PTP (Point-to-Point) and CP (Continuous Path).
- **Example**: **PUMA 560** (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly) — used widely in research and light assembly.

**3rd Generation — Sensory / Adaptive Robots (1990s) [1 mark]:**
- **Defining feature**: Integration of **sensors — vision (CCD cameras), force/torque, tactile** — for real-time adaptation.
- **Control**: Sensor-based adaptive control; first to use machine vision.
- **Example**: **FANUC i-series, ABB IRB series** with built-in vision systems for arc welding and inspection.

**4th Generation — Intelligent / Networked Robots (2000s) [1 mark]:**
- **Defining feature**: **IoT connectivity, mobile autonomy, machine learning**, and integration with factory networks.
- **Control**: Distributed intelligence, cloud-based analytics.
- **Example**: **KUKA KR AGILUS, mobile logistics robots** in smart warehouses (e.g., Amazon Kiva).

**5th Generation — Cognitive / Collaborative Robots (2010s–present) [1.5 marks]:**
- **Defining feature**: **Cobots (collaborative robots), AI-based perception, swarm robotics**, and direct human-robot interaction without safety cages.
- **Control**: Cognitive AI, deep learning, force-sensing skin, natural-language programming.
- **Example**: **Universal Robots UR10e, Rethink Baxter, Boston Dynamics Atlas** (humanoid).

**Fundamental Difference of 5th Generation (1 mark):**
- Earlier generations operated **in isolation behind safety cages** with pre-programmed repetitive tasks.
- 5th generation robots are **collaborative, adaptive, and cognitive** — they can work **safely alongside humans**, **learn from demonstration**, and **make autonomous decisions** using AI, fundamentally changing human-robot interaction.

#### **Q(b) Model Answer [7 Marks]:**

**DOF Calculation Using Kutzbach-Grübler Criterion [3 marks]:**

**Given:**
- Number of joints, $J = 3$ (2 revolute + 1 prismatic)
- Number of links (excluding base), $n = 4$
- Each revolute joint: $f_i = 1$; prismatic joint: $f_i = 1$
- Working in 3D space: $d = 3$

**Apply the formula:**

$$M = d(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{J}(d - f_i)$$

$$M = 3(4 - 1) - [(3 - 1) + (3 - 1) + (3 - 1)]$$

$$M = 3(3) - [2 + 2 + 2]$$

$$M = 9 - 6 = 3$$

> ✅ **DOF = 3** (Three independent motions: rotation θ₁, rotation θ₂, and vertical translation Z.)

**Coordinate System and Workspace [2 marks]:**
- SCARA operates in a **cylindrical-coordinate-like frame** — two horizontal rotations (analogous to r and θ) and one vertical Z-translation.
- The resulting **workspace** is a **cylindrical disc / dome** with height equal to the vertical stroke of the prismatic joint.
- Typical reach: **0.5 m to 1 m radius**, vertical stroke: 0.2–0.5 m.

**Why SCARA is Preferred for Vertical Assembly [2 marks]:**

1. **Selective Compliance in X-Y** — SCARA is **compliant (slightly flexible) in the horizontal X-Y plane** but **rigid in the vertical Z direction**. This means small misalignments between a peg and a hole can be **absorbed by the arm's flexure**, allowing **easy insertion** of components into PCB holes or connectors without jamming.

2. **High Speed and Repeatability** — SCARA's lightweight arm and the absence of heavy rotational inertia enable **cycle times as low as 0.3 seconds per pick-and-place**, making it ideal for high-volume assembly lines.

3. **Compact Footprint** — It occupies minimal floor space and can be **benchtop-mounted**, ideal for the dense layout of an electronics assembly cell.

4. **Cost-Effectiveness** — A SCARA is significantly **cheaper** than a 6-DOF articulated robot but fully adequate for tasks that **do not require arbitrary part orientation** — which is precisely the case in vertical PCB assembly where components are picked from a feeder and inserted straight down.

> **In contrast**, an articulated robot's 6 DOF (and high cost) is wasted in vertical assembly where orientation around the vertical axis is not needed.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam**

### A. **Coordinate System Classification (KTU's #1 Favorite)**
- **Cartesian (L-L-L)** → Cuboid workspace → CNC, 3D printing
- **Cylindrical (R-P-P)** → Cylindrical workspace → Machine tool loading
- **Spherical (R-R-P)** → Spherical workspace → Forging, die-cast
- **Articulated (R-R-R + Wrist)** → Lobed sphere → Welding, painting, complex assembly
- **SCARA (R-R-P)** → Cylindrical disc → Vertical assembly, PCB pick-and-place
- **Delta (3 × parallelogram)** → Truncated dome → High-speed packaging

### B. **Control Method**
- **Non-Servo** = open-loop, limit-switch, no feedback, **pick-and-place**
- **Servo** = closed-loop, encoder feedback
  - **PTP** (Point-to-Point) — no path control
  - **CP** (Continuous Path) — full path control (welding, painting)

### C. **Drive Technology**
- **Electric** → ≤ 20 kg, high precision, clean rooms, **most common**
- **Hydraulic** → 70–200 bar oil → **heavy forging, die handling**
- **Pneumatic** → 4–8 bar air → **fast, light, food industry**

### D. **DOF Calculation**
- Kutzbach-Grübler: $M = d(n-1) - \sum(d - f_i)$ with $d = 3$ in 3D
- **6-DOF** = position $(X,Y,Z)$ + orientation (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) → **Industrial standard**
- 3-DOF = position only; 4-DOF = position + 1 rotation (e.g., palletizing)

### E. **Generations of Robots**
1. **1st Gen** — Pick-and-Place (Unimate 1961)
2. **2nd Gen** — Teach-Playback (PUMA 560)
3. **3rd Gen** — Sensory Adaptive (FANUC i-series with vision)
4. **4th Gen** — Intelligent / Networked (Mobile warehouse robots)
5. **5th Gen** — Cognitive / Cobots (UR10e, Boston Dynamics Atlas)

### F. **Key Definitions to Memorize Verbatim**
- **Robot** (ISO 8373): *An automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes.*
- **Repeatability**: Closeness of agreement between repeated measurements of the same point under identical conditions (typical industrial: **±0.02 to ±0.1 mm**).
- **Accuracy**: Closeness of the robot's actual position to the commanded/programmed position.
- **Workspace**: The volume of space the end-effector can reach.
- **Degree of Freedom (DOF)**: Number of independent motions a robot can perform.
- **Payload**: Maximum weight the robot can lift without performance degradation.

### G. **Locomotion**
- **Fixed-base** (manipulator), **Wheeled** (WMR), **Tracked**, **Legged** (2/4/6/8), **Aerial (UAV)**, **Aquatic (AUV/ROV)**

### H. **Common Pitfalls**
- ❌ Confusing **repeatability with accuracy**
- ❌ Writing **"linear"** instead of **"prismatic"** in joint notation
- ❌ Forgetting that **SCARA** is R-R-P (not R-R-R) and has **selective compliance**
- ❌ Stating Delta robot DOF using the simple Grübler formula (it requires the **parallel-mechanism modified equation**)
- ❌ Confusing **4-DOF SCARA** with **6-DOF articulated** in PCB assembly context
